213	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
214	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
215	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
216	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
217	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
218	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
219	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
220	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
221	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
222	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
223	% SOURCE QUOTE:
224	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
225	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
226	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
227	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
228	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
229	%     intro Φ
230	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
231	%     infer_instance)"
232	\textit{Source: [Mathlib],
233	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
234	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
235	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
236	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
237	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
238	\(A\).
239	
240	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
241	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
242	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
243	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
244	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
245	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
246	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
247	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
248	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
249	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
250	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
251	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
252	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
253	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
254	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
255	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
256	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
257	critical path.
258	
259	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
260	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
261	is already present in Mathlib: the file
262	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
263	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
264	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
265	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
266	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
267	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
268	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
269	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
270	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
271	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
272	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
273	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
274	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
275	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
276	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
277	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
278	
279	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
280	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
281	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
282	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
283	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
284	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
285	independently of any upstream timing.
286	
287	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
288	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
289	
290	\begin{definition}
291	
292	\leanok
293	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
294	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
296	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
297	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
298	  \[
299	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
300	  \]
301	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
302	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
303	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
304	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
305	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
306	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
307	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
308	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
309	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
310	  canonical compatibility
311	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
312	     \;\cong\;
313	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
314	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
315	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
316	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
317	
318	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
319	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
320	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
321	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
322	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
323	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
324	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
325	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
326	\end{definition}
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	
330	\leanok
331	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
332	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
334	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
335	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
336	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
337	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
338	  \[
339	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
340	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
342	  \]
343	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
344	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
345	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
346	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
347	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
348	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
349	  \[
350	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
351	    \qquad
352	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
353	  \]
354	  the associator
355	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
356	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
357	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
358	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
359	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
360	\end{lemma}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
364	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
365	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
366	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
367	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
368	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
369	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
370	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
371	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
372	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
373	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
374	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
375	\end{proof}
376	
377	\begin{remark}
378	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
379	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
380	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
381	        lem:whisker_of_W}
382	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
383	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
384	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
385	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
386	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
387	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
388	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
389	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
390	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
391	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
392	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
393	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
394	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
395	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
396	
397	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
398	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
399	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
400	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
401	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
402	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
403	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
404	  fixed-base presheaves in
405	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
406	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
407	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
408	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
409	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
410	  it.
411	
412	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
413	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
414	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
415	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
416	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
417	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
418	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
419	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
420	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
421	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
422	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
423	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
424	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
425	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
426	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
427	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
428	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
429	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
430	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
431	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
432	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
433	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
434	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
435	\end{remark}
436	
437	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
438	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
439	
440	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
441	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
442	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
443	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
444	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
445	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
446	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
447	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
448	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
450	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
451	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
452	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
453	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
454	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
455	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
456	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
457	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
458	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
459	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
460	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
461	to the off-path route~(e).
462	
463	\begin{lemma}
464	
465	\leanok
466	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
467	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
468	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
469	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
470	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
471	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
472	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
473	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
474	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
475	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
476	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
477	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
478	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
479	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
480	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
481	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
482	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
483	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
484	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
485	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
486	  \[
487	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
488	      \;\;:=\;\;
489	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
490	  \]
491	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
492	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
493	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
494	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
495	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
496	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
497	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
498	\end{lemma}
499	
500	\begin{proof}
501	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
502	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
503	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
504	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
505	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
506	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
507	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
508	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
509	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
510	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
511	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
512	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
513	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
514	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
515	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
516	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
517	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
518	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
519	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
520	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
521	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
522	
523	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
524	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
525	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
526	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
527	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
528	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
529	\end{proof}
530	
531	\begin{lemma}
532	
533	\leanok
534	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
535	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
536	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
537	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
538	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
539	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
540	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
541	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
542	  morphism
543	  \[
544	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
545	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
546	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
547	  \]
548	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
549	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
550	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
551	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
552	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
553	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
554	\end{lemma}
555	
556	\begin{proof}
557	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
558	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
559	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
560	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
561	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
562	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
563	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
564	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
565	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
566	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
567	  isomorphism}
568	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
569	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
570	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
571	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
572	  therefore extension of scalars along
573	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
574	
575	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
576	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
577	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
578	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
579	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
580	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
581	  the abstract pullback functor.
582	
583	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
584	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
585	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
586	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
587	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
588	   \;\cong\;
589	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
590	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
591	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
592	  comparison
593	  \[
594	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
595	      \;\longrightarrow\;
596	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
597	      \;\otimes\;
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
599	  \]
600	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
601	
602	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
603	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
604	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
605	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
606	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
607	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
608	  % "\begin{lemma}
609	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
610	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
611	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
612	  % \begin{enumerate}
613	  % \item The functor
614	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
615	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
616	  % \item The functor
617	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
618	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
619	  %  [\dots]
620	  % \end{enumerate}
621	  % \end{lemma}
622	  %  [\dots]
623	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
624	  % of functors, see
625	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
626	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
627	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
628	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
629	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
630	
631	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
632	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
633	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
634	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
635	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
636	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
637	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
638	
639	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
640	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
641	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
642	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
643	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
644	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
645	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
646	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
647	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
648	  \otimes_S N)\).
649	
650	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
651	  \[
652	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
653	  \]
654	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
655	  presheaf-level adjunction
656	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
657	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
658	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
659	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
660	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
661	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
662	  composing the resulting comparison with
663	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
664	
665	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
666	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
667	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
668	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
669	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
670	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
671	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
672	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
673	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
674	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
675	
676	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
677	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
678	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
679	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
680	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
681	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
682	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
683	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
684	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
685	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
686	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
687	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
688	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
689	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
690	\end{proof}
691	
692	\begin{lemma}
693	
694	\leanok
695	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
696	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
697	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
698	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
699	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
700	  product: the canonical map
701	  \[
702	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
703	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
704	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
705	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
706	  \]
707	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
708	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
709	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
710	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
711	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
712	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
713	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
714	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
715	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
716	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
717	\end{lemma}
718	
719	\begin{lemma}
720	
721	\leanok
722	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
723	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
724	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
725	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
726	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
727	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
728	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
729	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
730	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
731	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
732	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
733	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
734	  Mathlib). Then:
735	  \begin{enumerate}
736	    \item the lax tensorator
737	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
738	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
739	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
740	    \item the lax unit
741	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
742	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
743	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
744	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
745	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
746	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
747	  \end{enumerate}
748	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
749	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
750	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
751	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
752	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
753	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
754	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
755	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
756	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
757	\end{lemma}
758	
759	\begin{proof}
760	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
761	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
762	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
763	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
764	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
765	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
766	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
767	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
768	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
769	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
770	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
771	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
772	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
773	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
774	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
775	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
776	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
777	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
778	
779	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
780	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
781	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
782	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
783	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
784	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
785	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
786	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
787	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,

794	\begin{lemma}
795	
796	\leanok
797	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
798	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
799	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
800	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
801	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
802	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
803	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
804	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
805	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
806	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
807	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
808	\end{lemma}
809	
810	\begin{proof}
811	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
812	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
813	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
814	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
815	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
816	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
817	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
818	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
819	\end{proof}

832	\begin{lemma}
833	
834	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
835	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
836	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
837	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
838	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
839	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
840	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
841	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
842	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
843	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
844	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
845	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
846	  left-whiskered morphism
847	  \[
848	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
849	  \]
850	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
851	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
852	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
853	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
854	
855	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
856	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
857	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
858	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
859	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
860	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
861	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
862	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
863	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
864	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
865	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
866	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
867	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
868	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
869	\end{lemma}
870	
871	\begin{proof}
872	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
873	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
874	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
875	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
876	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
877	
878	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
879	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
880	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
881	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
882	  \[
883	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
884	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
885	  \]
886	
887	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
888	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
889	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
890	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
891	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
892	
893	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
894	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
895	  linear maps (Mathlib
896	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
897	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
898	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
899	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
900	
901	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
902	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
903	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
904	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
905	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
906	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
907	\end{proof}

987	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API

990	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the

995	\paragraph{Two-tier strategy.} The group law is assembled by a \emph{two-tier}
996	strategy, of which this route~(e) is the second tier. The \textbf{primary} tier
997	builds the commutative group directly on isomorphism classes of \emph{locally
998	trivial} (invertible) sheaves (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), mirroring
999	Mathlib's ring-level Picard group \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) /

1008	(d.2). The \textbf{fallback} tier is the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to
1009	\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category on all of \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)

1126	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1127	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1128	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1129	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1130	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1131	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).

1135	      gives a canonical isomorphism

1156	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1157	
1158	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1159	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1160	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1161	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1162	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1163	
1164	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1165	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1166	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1167	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1168	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1169	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1170	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1171	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1172	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1173	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1174	  \[
1175	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1176	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1177	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1178	  \]
1179	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1180	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1181	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1182	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1183	
1184	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1185	  pinned commit:
1186	  \begin{enumerate}
1187	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1188	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1189	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1190	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1191	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1192	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1193	      above.
1194	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1195	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1196	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1197	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1198	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1199	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1200	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1201	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1202	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1203	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1204	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1205	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1206	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1207	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1208	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1209	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1210	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1211	  \end{enumerate}
1212	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1213	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1214	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1215	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1216	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1217	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1218	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1219	
1220	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1221	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1222	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1223	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1224	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1225	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1226	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1227	\end{proof}

1230	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1231	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1232	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local

1237	\begin{lemma}
1238	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1239	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1240	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_W, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_W}
1241	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1242	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1243	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1244	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1245	  \(J.W\):
1246	  \[
1247	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1248	    \qquad
1249	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1250	  \]
1251	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1252	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1253	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1254	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1255	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1256	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1257	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1258	\end{lemma}
1259	
1260	\begin{proof}
1261	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1262	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1263	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1264	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1265	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1266	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1267	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1268	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1269	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1270	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1271	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1272	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1273	\end{proof}

1275	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1276	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1277	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1278	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1279	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1280	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1281	associator is now built directly by
1282	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1283	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized;
1284	no Lean declaration realizes it and none should.

1286	\begin{lemma}
1287	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1288	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1289	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1290	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1291	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1292	  instance
1293	  \[
1294	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1295	  \]
1296	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1297	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1298	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1299	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1300	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1301	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1302	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1303	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1304	  category
1305	  \[
1306	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1307	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1308	  \]
1309	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1310	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1311	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1312	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1313	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1314	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1315	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1316	  composites.
1317	
1318	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it is currently
1319	  \emph{stated-but-unformalized} (no Lean declaration realizes the
1320	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance yet), pending the sole open obligation
1321	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1322	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1323	\end{lemma}
1324	
1325	\begin{proof}
1326	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1327	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1328	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1329	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1330	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1331	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1332	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1333	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1334	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1335	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1336	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1337	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1338	  at any point.
1339	\end{proof}

1341	\begin{lemma}
1342	
1343	\leanok
1344	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1345	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1347	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1348	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1349	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1350	  \[
1351	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1352	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1353	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1354	  \]
1355	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1356	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1357	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1358	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1359	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1360	  ever required.
1361	
1362	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1363	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1364	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1365	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1366	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1367	\end{lemma}
1368	
1369	\begin{proof}
1370	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1371	        lem:whisker_of_W, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1372	  \emph{Current realization (as currently formalized).} The isomorphism is at
1373	  present obtained by transporting the canonical \emph{presheaf}-level associator
1374	  \(\alpha\) of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
1375	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1376	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) through sheafification. The substrate tensor
1377	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf tensor, so the nested object
1378	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\) carries an \emph{inner} sheafification (inside the
1379	  left factor) that must be pulled out before \(\alpha\) can be read off. This is
1380	  done with the whiskering-stability lemmas \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\) and
1381	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), which show that the
1382	  sheafification unit, whiskered on either side by a fixed tensor factor, again lies
1383	  in the sheafification localizer \(J.W\); together with
1384	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_sheafification\_map\_of\_W}\) (a \(J.W\)-morphism becomes an
1385	  isomorphism after sheafification) this identifies the two nested sheafified tensors
1386	  with the sheafification of the presheaf-level nesting, where \(\alpha\) applies. The
1387	  associator is then \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\alpha)\) conjugated by these
1388	  comparison isomorphisms. This current route is transitively gated on the open,
1389	  vestigial proof placeholder \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)
1390	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}), the load-bearing local-injectivity
1391	  half of the left-whiskering stability \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1392	
1393	  \emph{Planned re-route (target; not yet realized).} Once
1394	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is closed (via the presheaf-level pushforward
1395	  adjunction ``H1''), the associator will be \emph{re-built} by gluing canonical
1396	  local isomorphisms, eliminating all dependence on the whiskering apparatus. Fix a
1397	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1398	  composite
1399	  \[
1400	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1401	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1402	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1403	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1404	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1405	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1406	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1407	  \]
1408	  in which:
1409	  \begin{itemize}
1410	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1411	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1412	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1413	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1414	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1415	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1416	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1417	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1418	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1419	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1420	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1421	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1422	  \end{itemize}
1423	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1424	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1425	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1426	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1427	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1428	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1429	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1430	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1431	  composites agree on the overlap.
1432	
1433	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1434	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1435	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1436	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1437	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1438	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1439	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1440	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1441	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1442	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1443	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1444	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1445	
1446	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1447	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1448	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1449	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1450	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1451	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1452	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1453	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1454	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1455	  specialisation can replace it. After this re-route the vestigial whiskering /
1456	  \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
1457	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal})
1458	  is deleted; route~(e) survives only as the named last-resort fallback should the H1
1459	  adjunction itself bottom out.
1460	\end{proof}

1554	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction

1558	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1559	  bundle. The dual
1560	  \[
1561	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1562	  \]
1563	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1564	  isomorphism
1565	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1566	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1567	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1568	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1569	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).

1572	\begin{proof}
1573	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1574	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
1575	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
1576	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
1577	  The contraction map
1578	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
1579	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
1580	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
1581	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
1582	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
1583	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
1584	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
1585	\end{proof}

1597	  \emph{locally-trivial} subtype
1598	  \[

1600	    \;\;=\;\;
1601	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
1602	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}
1603	  \]
1604	  introduced in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. The bifunctor
1605	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
1606	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
1607	  if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M'\) then
1608	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes\)
1609	  closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\), supplying the binary
1610	  operation the relative group law multiplies with.

1613	\begin{proof}

1615	  Closure is exactly
1616	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}: the tensor product of two
1617	  locally-trivial rank-one modules is again locally trivial of rank one
1618	  (\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_isLocallyTrivial}\)). The Lean construction
1619	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjOnProduct}\) applies this directly to a pair of objects of
1620	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\),
1621	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1622	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1623	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1624	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1625	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an

1627	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)

1630	\begin{lemma}
1631	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1632	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1633	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1634	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1635	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1636	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1637	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1638	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1639	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1640	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1641	  functor
1642	  \[
1643	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1644	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1645	  \]
1646	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1647	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1648	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1649	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1650	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1651	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1652	  canonical isomorphism
1653	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1654	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1655	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1656	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1657	\end{lemma}
1658	
1659	\begin{proof}
1660	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1661	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1662	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1663	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1664	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1665	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1666	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1667	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1668	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1669	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1670	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1671	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1672	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1673	  same cover.
1674	\end{proof}

1676	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1677	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1678	
1679	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1680	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1681	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1682	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1683	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1684	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1685	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1686	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1687	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1688	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1689	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1690	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1691	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1692	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1741	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1742	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism

1815	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1816	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1817	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1818	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1819	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1820	  01CR).
1821	
1822	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1823	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1824	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1825	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1826	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1827	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1828	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1829	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1830	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1831	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1832	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1833	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1834	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1835	
1836	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1837	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1838	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1839	  \[
1840	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1841	    \quad
1842	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1843	    \quad
1844	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1845	    \quad
1846	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1847	  \]
1848	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1849	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1850	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1851	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1852	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1853	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1854	
1855	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1856	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1857	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1858	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1859	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1860	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1861	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1862	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1863	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1864	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1865	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1866	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1867	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1868	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1869	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1870	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1871	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1872	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1873	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1874	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1875	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1876	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1877	  pinned commit.
1878	
1879	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1880	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1881	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1882	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1883	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1884	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1885	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1886	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1887	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1888	
1889	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1890	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1891	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1892	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
1893	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
1894	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
1895	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
1896	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
1897	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
1898	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
1899	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
1900	  already supplies.
1901	\end{lemma}

1903	\begin{proof}

1905	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1906	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1907	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1908	  Work directly on isomorphism classes of locally-trivial sheaves, as in Mathlib's
1909	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1910	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
1911	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
1912	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1913	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
1914	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
1915	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
1916	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
1917	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
1918	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
1919	  \[
1920	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
1921	    \quad
1922	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
1923	    \quad
1924	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
1925	    \quad
1926	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
1927	  \]
1928	  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
1929	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1930	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
1931	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
1932	  This is the same one-isomorphism-per-law shape that Mathlib's
1933	  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
1934	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}) uses on a skeletal
1935	  monoidal category via its skeletality map \(\mathtt{hC}\), but we do \emph{not}
1936	  instantiate that construction: it requires a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\)
1937	  (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), which is
1938	  exactly the structure for the varying structure sheaf that is unavailable.
1939	  Finally every element is invertible: the dual \(L^{-1}\) of
1940	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
1941	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
1942	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
1943	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
1944	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
1945	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
1946	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
1947	  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) on
1948	  iso-classes of invertible modules (there off a fixed-ring \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
1949	  here by hand).
1950	
1951	  Alternatively (fallback), once \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} provides the full
1952	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
1953	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
1954	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
1955	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
1956	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
1957	\end{proof}

2006	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2007	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2008	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2009	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2010	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2011	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2012	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2013	  \[
2014	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2015	  \]
2016	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2017	  abelian-group structure with addition

2019	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),

2022	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),

2025	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2026	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2027	  map
2028	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2029	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2030	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.

2043	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2044	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2045	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2046	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2047	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2048	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2049	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}

2051	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2052	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
