735	\section{Out of scope}
736	\label{sec:albaneseup_out_of_scope}
737	
738	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Albanese universal property
739	(\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} = Milne Proposition~III~6.1) and the
740	four internal sub-lemmas of the symmetric-power proof
741	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism},
742	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
743	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map},
744	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
745	\cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}), plus the wiring of these into
746	a positive-genus Albanese witness. The following adjacent content is
747	explicitly out of scope:
748	\begin{itemize}
749	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (A.2.c).} Proved in
750	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} as
751	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}; this chapter only consumes it.
752	  \item \textbf{The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and the degree map
753	    (A.3).} The identity component refinement of the Picard scheme together
754	    with its degree map is the subject of a forthcoming chapter; this chapter
755	    consumes \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
756	    \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\) but does not construct it.
757	  \item \textbf{Milne's Theorem~3.2 (A.4.c).} Proved in
758	    \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
759	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only consumes it
760	    inside \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} to promote the
761	    rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism.
762	  \item \textbf{The scheme-theoretic construction of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\).}
763	    Mathlib has no \(g\)-th symmetric power of a scheme; the
764	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} target of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve} is a
765	    project-side sub-build (affine \(S_g\)-invariants
766	    \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\) on each open affine \(\Spec A \subseteq C\),
767	    glued by the standard open-cover patching of Milne~III.3
768	    Proposition~3.1 / Mumford~1970 II.7 \& III.11). The construction itself
769	    is a non-trivial dependency of A.4.d; this chapter \emph{specifies} the
770	    target but does not develop its construction.
771	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\) (Milne~III \S 6.6) and the
772	    moduli / Poincar\'e-bundle Albanese route.} The iter-174 draft of this
773	    chapter proposed a route via the Poincar\'e bundle of \(A\) and the
774	    autoduality of \(J\), which gates on the theorem of the cube. The cube
775	    was excised from the project in iter-163; the autoduality / moduli
776	    route is therefore demoted and replaced by the symmetric-power route
777	    of this chapter. A reference recording of the moduli wording is given
778	    in the ``Alternative route history'' \texttt{\%~NOTE} block at the end
779	    of this file.
780	  \item \textbf{Milne Proposition~III~6.4 / Remark~6.5 / Theorem~6.6} (the
781	    divisorial-correspondence universal property for \(C \times C \to A\),
782	    the identification of \((J, F)\) with the Albanese variety in the sense
783	    of Lang, and the autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\)). Not needed by the
784	    symmetric-power route.
785	  \item \textbf{Weil's reconstruction of \(J\) from the rational law of
786	    composition on \(C^{(g)}\) (Milne~III \S 7).} The project does not need
787	    this alternative construction.
788	  \item \textbf{The genus-\(0\) case.} The standing hypothesis of this chapter
789	    is \(g(C) > 0\) (Milne's standing hypothesis of \S III.6). The genus-\(0\)
790	    case of the Albanese witness is supplied by
791	    \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}), routed through

793	    shortcut; that construction is independent of and disjoint from the

795	  \item \textbf{Generalisations to a non-algebraically-closed base \(k\).}
796	    The chapter works over \(\bar k\) throughout. The descent of the witness
797	    back to \(k\) via Galois descent of morphism equality is the subject of

799	\end{itemize}

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The
126	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} dependency is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
176

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  
150	\leanok
151	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
152	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
153	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
154	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
155	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
156	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
157	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
158	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
159	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
160	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
161	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
162	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
163	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
164	\end{proof}

166	\section{Projective dimension}
167	\label{sec:ab_pd}
168	
169	\begin{definition}
170	
171	\leanok
172	  [Projective dimension]
173	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
174	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
175	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
176	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
177	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
178	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
179	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
180	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
181	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
182	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
183	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
184	  \[
185	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
186	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
187	  \]
188	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
189	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
190	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
191	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
192	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
193	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
194	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
195	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
196	\end{definition}
197	
198	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
199	\begin{itemize}
200	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
201	    iff \(M\) is free);
202	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
203	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
204	\end{itemize}
205	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
206	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
207	
208	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
209	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
210	
211	\begin{lemma}
212	
213	\leanok
214	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
215	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
216	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
217	  
218	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
219	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
220	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
221	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
222	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
223	  %  \begin{enumerate}
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
228	  %  \item
229	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
230	  %  \end{enumerate}"
231	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
232	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
233	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
234	  \(R\)-modules. Then
235	  \begin{enumerate}
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
237	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
238	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
239	  \end{enumerate}
240	\end{lemma}
241	
242	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
243	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
244	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
245	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
246	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
247	%  from Lemma REF."
248	\begin{proof}
249	  
250	  
251	  
252	  
253	  
254	\leanok
255	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
256	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
257	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
258	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
259	  \[
260	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
261	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
262	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
263	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
264	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
265	  \]
266	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
267	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
268	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
269	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
273	\label{sec:ab_main}
274	
275	\begin{theorem}
276	
277	\leanok
278	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
279	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
280	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
281	  
282	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
283	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
284	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
285	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
286	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
287	  %  \(\)
288	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
289	  %  \(\)"
290	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
291	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
292	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
293	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
294	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
295	  \[
296	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
297	  \]
298	\end{theorem}
299	
300	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
301	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
302	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
303	%  \(\)
304	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
305	%  \(\)
306	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
307	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
308	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
309	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
310	%  Proposition REF we see that
311	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
312	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
313	%  \begin{align*}
314	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
315	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
316	%  \ldots,\\
317	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
318	%  \end{align*}
319	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
320	%  \begin{align*}
321	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
322	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
323	%  \ldots,\\
324	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
325	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
326	%  \end{align*}
327	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
328	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
329	%
330	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
331	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
332	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
333	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
334	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
335	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
336	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
337	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
338	%  \(\)
339	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
340	%  \(\)
341	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
342	%  shows that
343	%  \(\)
344	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
345	%  M/xM \to 0
346	%  \(\)
347	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
348	%  By Lemma REF we have
349	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
350	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
351	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
352	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
353	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
354	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
355	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
356	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
357	\begin{proof}
358	  
359	  
360	  
361	\leanok
362	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
363	
364	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
365	  finite free resolution
366	  \[
367	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
368	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
369	  \]
370	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
371	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
372	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
373	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
374	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
375	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
376	  sequences
377	  \[
378	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
379	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
380	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
381	  \]
382	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
383	  yields the chain of inequalities
384	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
385	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
386	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
387	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
388	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
389	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
390	
391	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
392	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
393	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
394	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
395	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
396	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
397	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
398	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
399	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
400	  \[
401	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
402	  \]
403	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
404	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
405	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
406	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
407	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
408	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
409	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
410	  \[
411	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
412	  \]
413	  Substituting yields
414	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
415	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
416	\end{proof}
417	
418	\begin{lemma}
419	
420	\leanok
421	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum: inductive step \(\text{pd} = k+1\)]
422	  \label{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
423	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
424	  % NOTE iter-202 review: RESOLVED. The `private` keyword was dropped and the
425	  % body of `auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd` was closed axiom-clean
426	  % (iter-202 Lane AB Path B). The declaration is now public and sync_leanok
427	  % resolves it; the prior iter-199/200/201 NOTE chain is removed.
428	  \uses{thm:auslander_buchsbaum, lem:depth_drops_by_one}
429	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum),
430	  induction-on-\(\text{pd}\) re-organisation; cf.\ Matsumura, Theorem 19.1.}
431	
432	  Inductive-step variant pinning the project-internal helper used by
433	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}: for a Noetherian local ring
434	  \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite \(R\)-module \(M\) of projective
435	  dimension \(k+1\), and the AB formula known for projective dimension
436	  \(\leq k\), one has
437	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\).
438	\end{lemma}
439	\begin{proof}
440	  \uses{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
441	  \leanok
442	  Replay of the induction step in the main proof's
443	  \emph{inductive step} paragraph (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum},
444	  proof block), using \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one} for the
445	  depth-drop on both \(R\) and \(M\) after quotienting by an
446	  \(M\)-regular \(x \in \mathfrak m\), plus a snake-lemma carving of
447	  the minimal resolution to extract the \(R/(x)\)-minimal resolution
448	  of \(M/xM\) of the same length. The four ingredients are:
449	  \begin{enumerate}
450	    \item Minimal-resolution carving (Stacks
451	      \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}): every finite free
452	      resolution of a finite module over a Noetherian local ring can
453	      be trimmed to a minimal one of the same projective dimension.
454	    \item ``What is exact'' criterion (Stacks 00MF /
455	      \texttt{proposition-what-exact}): characterises exactness in
456	      terms of depths of \(r\)-minors. Used to extract a common
457	      non-zero-divisor \(x \in \mathfrak m\) for both \(R\) and \(M\).
458	    \item Snake lemma applied to multiplication-by-\(x\) on the
459	      minimal resolution: produces a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\)
460	      over \(R/(x)\) of the same length, so
461	      \(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
462	    \item Depth drops by one
463	      (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}): once an \(M\)-regular element
464	      \(x \in \mathfrak m\) is fixed,
465	      \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\) and likewise on \(R\).
466	  \end{enumerate}
467	  Combining (1)-(4) with the inductive hypothesis on \(M/xM\) over
468	  \(R/(x)\) closes the inductive step. Pieces (1)-(3) remain Mathlib
469	  gaps as of \texttt{b80f227}; piece (4) is now formalised
470	  axiom-clean (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}).
471	\end{proof}
472	
473	\begin{lemma}
474	
475	\leanok
476	  [Depth drops by one under a regular element]
477	  \label{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
478	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_depth_of_isSMulRegular}
479	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
480	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LL (\texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}).}
481	
482	  For a Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite
483	  \(R\)-module \(M\), and an \(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
484	  \[
485	    \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M/xM) + 1 \;=\; \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M).
486	  \]
487	\end{lemma}
488	\begin{proof}
489	
490	\leanok
491	  Reduces to the Ext characterisation
492	  (\cref{lem:depth_via_ext}) on both sides; the short exact sequence
493	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{\,\cdot x\,} M \to M/xM \to 0\) gives a
494	  long exact sequence in \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) on which
495	  multiplication by \(x\) is zero (since \(x \in \text{Ann}_R \kappa\)),
496	  breaking the LES into short exact pieces
497	  \(0 \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M) \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M/xM) \to
498	  \text{Ext}^{i+1}(\kappa, M) \to 0\). The translation to the
499	  \(\mathbb N_\infty\) depth equality goes through
500	  \texttt{ENat.forall\_natCast\_le\_iff\_le}.
501	\end{proof}

507	\paragraph{Status: RESOLVED iter-202.}

509	\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} are both closed axiom-clean as of

517	together with the long exact sequence. \textbf{Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF,
518	the ``what is exact'' criterion) was OBVIATED} by this Path~B route ---
519	no Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion was needed. \textbf{All

524	\medskip
525	The inductive step
526	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
527	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula was originally gated, against an
528	earlier pinned Mathlib snapshot, by three absent substrate ingredients
529	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth in hand
530	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
531	decomposition that the proof block of

533	its purpose was to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort

537	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are

544	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)

547	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
548	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
549	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
550	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
551

605	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the

609	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
610	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
611	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
612	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
613	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
614	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream

617	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
618	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
619	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
620	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
621	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
622	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
623	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
624	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
625	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
626	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
627	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
628	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
629	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
630	
631	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
632	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
633	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
634	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
635	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
636	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
637	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
638	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
639	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
640	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
641	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
642	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
643	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
644	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
645	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
646	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
647	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
648	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
649	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
650	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
651	candidate.
652	
653	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
654	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
655	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
656	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
657	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
658	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
659	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES
660	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) plus the iter-200 helpers
661	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker\_of\_surjection}\) +
662	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
663	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
664	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
665	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
666	helper
667	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
668	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
669	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
670	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
671	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine
672	to get \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\), then apply
673	the IH (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\)) for
674	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges
675	to \((k+1) + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) as required.
676	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
677	inductive code.
678	
679	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
680	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
681	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly

698	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
699	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
700	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
701	
702	\begin{lemma}
703	
704	\leanok
705	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
706	    over a local ring]
707	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
708	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
709	  \uses{def:depth}
710	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
711	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
712	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
713	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
714	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
715	  %     \ldots
716	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
717	  %     R^{n_i}
718	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
719	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
720	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
721	  %     \ldots
722	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
723	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
724	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
725	  %  complex
726	  %     \ldots \to
727	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
728	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
729	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
730	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
731	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
732	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
733	  %     \ldots
734	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
735	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
736	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
737	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
738	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
739	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
740	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
741	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
742	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
743	  \begin{enumerate}
744	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
745	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
746	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
747	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
748	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
749	      summands).
750	  \end{enumerate}
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	
754	\leanok
755	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
756	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
757	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
758	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
759	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
760	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
761	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
762	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
763	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
764	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
765	
766	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
767	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
768	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
769	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
770	
771	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
772	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
773	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
774	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
775	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
776	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
777	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
778	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
779	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
780	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
781	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
782	\end{proof}
783	
784	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
785	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
786	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
787	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
788	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
789	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target
790	with abstract \(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES descent: only
791	the per-syzygy SES is consumed; the iterated chain-complex carrier
792	is not needed.

878	  \(R\)-module, \(n \geq 1\), and \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) a
879	  surjection with \(\ker f\) nontrivial. Then

881	   \texttt{depth}\,(\ker f)\).

907	matrix-collapse).} The iter-201 \texttt{ab-stacks00mf} mathlib-
908	analogist verdict identifies an alternative closure that sidesteps
909	gap (2): instead of building Stacks 00MF as a standalone substrate,
910	split \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} into an
911	\emph{inductive step} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)) and a
912	\emph{base case} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\)). Each piece uses only
913	already-existing LES infrastructure plus one new
914	\(\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC matrix-collapse helper.
915	
916	\textit{Inductive step \(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)
917	\((\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC).} From
918	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection} +
919	the contrapositive of
920	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker},
921	\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\) exactly. Strong induction gives
922	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), hence
923	\(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f < \texttt{depth}\,R\) since \(k \geq 1\).
924	Lower bound \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\):
925	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) part (2). Upper bound
926	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\): by
927	contradiction --- assume \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\),
928	look at the LES of \(\texttt{Ext}^*(\kappa, -)\) at
929	\(i = \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\), infer
930	\(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,\ker f}(\kappa, R^n) \neq 0\)
931	(forcing \(\texttt{depth}\,R \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\)),
932	contradicting the IH.
933	
934	\textit{Base case \(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\) \((\sim 80\)--\(120\) LOC).}
935	\(\ker f\) is projective (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = 0\)), hence free
936	over the local Noetherian \(R\) by Mathlib's
937	\(\texttt{Module.free\_of\_flat\_of\_isLocalRing}\) +
938	\(\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_projective}\). So \(\ker f \cong R^m\) with
939	\(m \geq 1\). The inclusion \(\ker f \hookrightarrow R^n\) is an
940	\((n \times m)\)-matrix \(A\) with entries in
941	\(\texttt{Ann}(\kappa) = \mathfrak m\) (from
942	\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}'s minimality
943	clause \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\)).
944	
945	The \textbf{matrix-collapse helper}
946	(CLOSED iter-201, 4 axiom-clean private decls at
947	\texttt{AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} L1418--L1517):
948	\[
949	  \forall p \geq 0,\quad \forall e \in \texttt{Ext}^p(\kappa, R^n),\quad
950	  e \circ \texttt{Ext.mk}_0\,(\texttt{ofHom}\,A) = 0
951	  \quad \text{in } \texttt{Ext}^{p+0}(\kappa, R^n)
952	  \text{ when } A_{ij} \in \texttt{Ann}_R\,\kappa.
953	\]
954	The 4 helpers are \(\texttt{Module.elemMap}\) (the elementary map
955	sending \(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1 \mapsto \texttt{Pi.single}\,i\,1\)
956	and other basis vectors to zero), \(\texttt{Module.elemMap\_apply}\),
957	\(\texttt{Module.linearMap\_finFunR\_matrix\_decomp}\)
958	(\(A = \sum_{i,j} A(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1)\,i \cdot
959	   \texttt{elemMap}_{ij}\)), and the closure helper
960	\(\texttt{Module.ext\_comp\_mk}_0\texttt{\_ofHom\_eq\_zero\_of\_entries\_mem\_annihilator}\)
961	itself. The realised API path uses Mathlib's
962	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_sum}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_sum}\)
963	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_sum}\) and the scalar siblings
964	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_smul}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_smul}\)
965	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_smul}\) (\emph{not}
966	\(\texttt{Ext.bilinearCompOfLinear}\) as originally projected; the
967	mathematical content is identical). Each summand reduces to a scalar-
968	bilinear form killed by the existing
969	\(\texttt{ext\_smul\_eq\_zero\_of\_mem\_annihilator}\) (L229).
970	
971	With matrix-collapse, the LES at \(i = \texttt{depth}\,R - 1\)
972	gives
973	\[
974	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R - 1}(\kappa, M)
975	  \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;}
976	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f)
977	  \xrightarrow{\;(- \circ A)\;}
978	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^n)
979	\]
980	with the second arrow zero. If
981	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,R\), the source is zero,
982	hence \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) = 0\) ---
983	but \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) \simeq
984	\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^m) \neq 0\)
985	(\(m \geq 1\), \(\texttt{depth}\,R^m = \texttt{depth}\,R\)).
986	Contradiction. So \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\), and the
987	base case
988	\(\texttt{pd}\,M + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) follows
989	from \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\) (since \(\ker f\)
990	free).

1032	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
1033	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
1034	
1035	\begin{definition}
1036	
1037	\leanok
1038	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
1039	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
1040	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
1041	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
1042	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
1043	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1044	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
1045	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
1046	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
1047	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
1048	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
1049	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
1050	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
1051	\end{definition}
1052	
1053	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
1054	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
1055	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
1056	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
1057	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
1058	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
1059	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
1060	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
1061	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
1062	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
1063	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
1064	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
1065	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
1066	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
1067	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
1068	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
1069	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
1070	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
1071	\begin{corollary}
1072	
1073	\leanok
1074	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1075	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1076	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1077	  
1078	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1079	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1080	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1081	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1082	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1083	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1084	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1085	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1086	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1087	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1088	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1089	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1090	\end{corollary}
1091	
1092	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1093	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1094	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1095	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1096	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1097	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1098	\begin{proof}
1099	  
1100	  
1101	  
1102	  
1103	  
1104	\leanok
1105	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1106	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1107	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1108	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1109	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1110	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1111	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1112	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1113	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1114	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1115	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1116	
1117	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1118	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1119	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1120	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1121	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1122	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1123	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1124	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1125	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1126	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1127	\end{proof}
1128	
1129	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1130	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1131	
1132	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1133	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1134	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1135	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1136	
1137	\begin{enumerate}
1138	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1139	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1140	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1141	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1142	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1143	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1144	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1145	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1146	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1147	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1148	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1149	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1150	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1151	\end{enumerate}
1152	
1153	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1154	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1155	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1156	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1157	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1158	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
1159

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
207	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
208	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
209	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
210	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
216	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
218	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
219	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
220	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
221	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
222	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
223	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
224	  \(S_M\)-module.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  The canonical comparison map
229	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
230	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
231	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
232	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
233	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
234	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
235	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
236	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
237	  the conclusion.
238	\end{proof}
239	
240	\begin{lemma}
241	
242	\leanok
243	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
244	  the relative dimension]
245	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
247	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
248	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
249	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
250	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
251	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
252	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
253	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
254	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  Mathlib's
259	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
260	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
261	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
262	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
263	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
264	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
265	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
266	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
267	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
268	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
269	  ranks equal \(n\), so
270	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
271	\end{proof}
