273	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
274	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
275	
276	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
277	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
278	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
279	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
280	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
281	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
282	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
283	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
284	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
285	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
286	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
287	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
288	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
289	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
290	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
291	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
292	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
293	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
294	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
295	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
296	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
297	named iter-199+ prover targets.
298	
299	\begin{lemma}
300	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
301	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
302	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
303	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
304	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
305	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
306	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
307	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
308	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
310	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
311	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
312	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
313	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
314	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
315	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
316	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
317	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
318	  \[
319	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
320	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
321	  \]
322	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
323	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
324	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
325	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
326	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
327	
328	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
329	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
330	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
331	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
332	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
333	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
334	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
335	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}
339	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
340	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue}
342	  % \lean{...} updated iter-199 review: iter-199 prover landed the
343	  % closed-point iso as a project-local axiom-clean helper (private
344	  % theorem in AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme namespace). Companion siblings:
345	  %   cotangent_iso_maximalIdeal_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue
346	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_formallySmooth_residue
347	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_algebraMap_residue (closed-point bundled)
348	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
349	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
350	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
351	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
352	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
353	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
354	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
355	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
356	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
357	  % $S'$-modules
358	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
359	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
360	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
361	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
362	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
363	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
364	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
365	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
366	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
367	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
369	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
370	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
371	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
372	  \[
373	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
374	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
375	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
376	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
377	  \]
378	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
379	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
380	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
381	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
382	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
383	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
384	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
385	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
386	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
387	  \[
388	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
389	  \]
390	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
391	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
392	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
393	\end{lemma}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
397	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
398	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
399	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
400	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
401	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
402	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
403	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
404	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
405	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
406	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
407	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
408	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
409	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
410	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
411	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
412	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
413	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
414	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
415	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
416	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
417	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
418	  dimension equals \(1\).
419	\end{lemma}
420	
421	\begin{proof}
422	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
423	  \begin{enumerate}
424	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
425	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
426	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
427	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
428	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
429	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
430	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
431	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
432	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
433	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
434	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
435	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
436	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
437	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
438	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
439	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
440	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
441	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
442	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
443	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
444	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
445	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
446	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
447	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
448	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
449	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
450	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
451	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
452	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
453	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
454	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
455	      the conclusion.
456	  \end{enumerate}
457	\end{proof}

637	\paragraph{Iter-201+ Jacobian-regular-sequence witness recipe.} The
638	residual substrate of Stage 6.A is the Jacobian-criterion

1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean
2	
3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)
82	via the project's \(\Module\bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
182	
183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}

333	  \(\to\) \texttt{constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}

339	\begin{lemma}
340	
341	\leanok
342	[H\(^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes]
343	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}

362	
363	\begin{lemma}\leanok
364	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
365	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
367	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
368	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
369	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
370	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
371	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
372	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
373	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
374	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
375	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
376	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
377	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
378	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
379	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
380	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
381	  \[
382	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
383	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
384	  \]
385	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
386	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
387	  \[
388	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
389	  \]
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve,
394	        lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one,
395	        lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
396	  \leanok
397	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
398	  (REF),
399	  \[
400	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
401	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
402	  \]
403	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements
404	  (\cref{lem:H0_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_one} and
405	  \cref{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}), attacked separately
406	  by the prover.
407	
408	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
409	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
410	  \[
411	    H^0(C, k(P))
412	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
413	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
414	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
415	      k(P)
416	    \bigr)
417	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
418	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
419	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
420	    (k(P))(C),
421	  \]
422	  realised by the project lemmas
423	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
424	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
425	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
426	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
427	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
428	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
429	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
430	  goal to
431	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
432	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
433	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
434	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
435	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
436	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
437	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
438	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
439	
440	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
441	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
442	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
443	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
444	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
445	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
446	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
447	
448	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
449	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
450	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
451	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
452	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
453	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
454	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
455	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
456	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
457	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
458	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
459	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
460	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
461	  % gap to clear.
462	
463	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
464	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
465	\end{proof}

467	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
468	
469	\begin{theorem}
470	
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
474	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
476	  
477	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
478	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
479	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic
488	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
489	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[
493	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
494	  \]
495	\end{theorem}
496	
497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
556	
557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
615	
616	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
617	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
619	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
620	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
621	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
622	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
623	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
624	  REF (\(\chi\) on
625	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
626	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
627	  identity REF
628	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
630	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
631	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
632	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
633	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
634	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
635	  specific additivity statement
636	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
637	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
638	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
639	\end{proof}
640	
641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
702	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish

707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)

710	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
711	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
712	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
723	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
724	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
725	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
726	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
727	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
728	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
729	\end{proof}
730

745	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)

3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +

10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.

89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction

99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173 introduced

105	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
106	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
107	    \begin{quote}
108	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\

110	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\

114	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
115	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
116	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
117	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
118	    properness; integrality threaded).
119	\end{itemize}
120	\noindent
121	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
122	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
123	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
124	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
125	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads

127	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
128	\begin{definition}
129	\leanok
130	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
131	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
133	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
134	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
135	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
136	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
137	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
138	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
139	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
140	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
141	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
142	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
143	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
144	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
146	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
147	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
148	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
149	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
150	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
151	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
152	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
153	  the Mathlib predicate
154	  \[
155	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
156	  \]
157	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
158	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
159	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
160	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
161	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
162	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
163	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
164	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
165	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
166	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
167	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
168	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
169	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
170	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
171	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
172	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
173	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
174	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
175	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
176	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
177	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
178	  \begin{itemize}
179	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
180	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
181	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
182	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
183	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
184	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
185	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
186	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
187	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
188	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
189	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
190	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
191	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
192	      uses.
193	  \end{itemize}
194	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
195	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
196	  needs.
197	\end{definition}
198	\begin{definition}
199	\leanok
200	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
201	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
202	  
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
204	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
205	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
206	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
207	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
209	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
210	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
211	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
212	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
214	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
215	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
216	  \[
217	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
218	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
219	  \]
220	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
221	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
222	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
223	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
224	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
225	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
226	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
227	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
228	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
229	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
230	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
231	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
232	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
233	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
234	\end{definition}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Regular in codimension one]
238	  \label{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne}
240	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II \S6, p.~130, the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)
241	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 147).
242	  % Stacks pointers: tag 0BX5 (regular schemes), tag 02ME
243	  % (DVR-of-stalk characterisation at codim-1 points).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme
245	  % which is regular in codimension one."
246	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130 (the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)).}
247	  A scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in codimension one} when for every point
248	  \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) (equivalently,
249	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1\)) the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is
250	  a discrete valuation ring. The Lean encoding bundles this conjunction as
251	  a one-field \texttt{Prop} class so that downstream order / principal /
252	  degree lemmas may consume it as a typeclass hypothesis.
253	\end{definition}
254	\section{Open-immersion descent for prime divisors}
255	\label{sec:open_immersion_descent}
256	\textit{Source: Stacks Project tags 02IZ (stalk under open immersion) +
257	01J6 (open immersion preserves regularity); cross-referenced with the
258	project-local iter-183 substrate
259	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}.}
260	
261	The chapter's order / principal-divisor API is naturally stated on the
262	ambient scheme \(X\), but several downstream proofs (the
263	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) non-zero branch in
264	particular) require localising to an affine open \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
265	identifying prime divisors of \(X\) lying in \(U\) with prime divisors of
266	\(U\), and transporting stalk-level data along the open-immersion stalk
267	isomorphism. This section records the substrate bijection
268	\(\{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
269	 \;\simeq\; U.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\)
270	and the open-immersion descent of the \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
271	class.
272	
273	\begin{definition}
274	
275	\leanok
276	[Restriction of a prime divisor to an open subscheme]
277	  \label{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}
278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen}
279	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
280	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), a prime
281	  divisor \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), and a witness \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\),
282	  the restriction \(Y |_U : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages
283	  the point \(Y.\texttt{point}\) (viewed as a point of \(U\) via the open
284	  inclusion) with the coheight witness from
285	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) (project-local
286	  substrate, iter-183 \texttt{CoheightBridge.lean} L175--L185).
287	\end{definition}
288	\begin{definition}
289	
290	\leanok
291	[Push of a prime divisor along an open immersion]
292	  \label{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen}
294	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
295	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), and a
296	  prime divisor \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), the push
297	  \(\texttt{ofOpen}\,U\,Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages the point
298	  \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U \hookrightarrow X\) (viewed as a point of \(X\))
299	  with the coheight witness from
300	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) applied in the
301	  opposite direction.
302	\end{definition}
303	\begin{lemma}
304	
305	\leanok
306	[Bijection of prime divisors under open immersion]
307	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_equivOpen}
308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.equivOpen}
309	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen, def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
310	  For an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\) of a scheme \(X\), there is a
311	  bijection
312	  \[
313	    \{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
314	    \;\simeq\;
315	    U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}
316	  \]
317	  packaging the restriction (\ref{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}) and the
318	  push (\ref{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}) as mutual inverses. Both
319	  \texttt{left\_inv} and \texttt{right\_inv} hold definitionally
320	  (\texttt{rfl}) because both directions preserve the underlying point
321	  field via subtype eta + structure eta.
322	\end{lemma}
323	\begin{lemma}
324	
325	\leanok
326	[Stalk isomorphism across an open immersion]
327	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso}
329	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}

333	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}.}
334	  For \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\), the
335	  stalk presheaf of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) at the restricted point coincides,
336	  up to canonical isomorphism, with the stalk presheaf of \(X\) at
337	  \(Y.\texttt{point}\). The Lean wrapper packages Mathlib's
338	  \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\) for the project's
339	  \texttt{PrimeDivisor} bundle.

347	  \uses{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne, lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso,

350	  and \(U \hookrightarrow X\) an open subscheme with
351	  \(\texttt{IsIntegral}\,U.\texttt{toScheme}\) (the latter is \emph{not}
352	  automatic from integrality of \(X\); it is threaded explicitly).
353	  Then \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) also satisfies

355	  codim-1 point \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) is

400	\paragraph{End-to-end map: Sub-builds 1--3 \(\Rightarrow\) closure of
401	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) (\ref{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}, non-zero branch L535).}
402	The L535 closure target is: for an integral, locally-Noetherian scheme
403	\(X\) satisfying \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) and a non-zero
404	\(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\),
405	\((\texttt{Function.support}\,(Y \mapsto \texttt{order}\,Y\,f)).\texttt{Finite}\).
406	Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV proves this by reducing to an
407	affine open neighbourhood and citing height-1 prime decomposition
408	on a Noetherian ring. The project decomposes the proof into three
409	project-local Sub-builds plus a terminal closure step:
410	
411	\begin{itemize}
412	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 1 (CLOSED iter-200, 8 axiom-clean decls
413	    L153--L305).} Open-immersion descent of the
414	    \(\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) bundle: bijection between
415	    prime divisors of \(X\) lying in an open \(U\) and prime divisors
416	    of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\); stalk-isomorphism wrapper around
417	    Mathlib's \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\); descent of the
418	    \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) class.
419	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 2 (CLOSED iter-201, 6 axiom-clean decls
420	    L247--L519).} Naturality of \(\texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\) across a
421	    ring isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) of Noetherian-domain stalks of
422	    Krull dim \(\leq 1\), with compatible fraction-field iso
423	    \(K_R \simeq K_S\). The realised modular decomposition is
424	    4 private \(\texttt{Ring.*}\) helpers
425	    (\(\texttt{ord\_ringEquiv}\),
426	     \(\texttt{nonZeroDivisors\_ringEquiv}\),
427	     \(\texttt{ordMonoidWithZeroHom\_ringEquiv}\),
428	     \(\texttt{ordFrac\_ringEquiv}\) anchor), plus the public
429	    function-field iso \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso}\)
430	    (\ref{def:functionFieldIso}) and the parameterised packaging
431	    \(\texttt{Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ordFrac\_stalkIso\_naturality}\)
432	    (private). The \(\texttt{ordFrac\_ringEquiv}\) anchor proof
433	    case-splits on \(x = 0\); in the non-zero branch it obtains
434	    \((a, b \in \mathrm{nzd}\,R)\) from
435	    \(\texttt{IsLocalization.surj}\), lifts the numerator via
436	    \(\texttt{mem\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_ne\_zero}\), expresses both
437	    sides as \(\texttt{mk'}\,K_R\,a\,\langle b, \cdot\rangle\) /
438	    \(\texttt{mk'}\,K_S\,(e\,a)\,\langle e\,b, \cdot\rangle\), applies
439	    \(\texttt{Ring.ordFrac\_eq\_div}\), and closes via
440	    \(\texttt{ordMonoidWithZeroHom\_ringEquiv}\) on numerator and
441	    denominator.
442	  \item \textbf{Sub-build 3 (iter-202 target, \(\sim 30\)--\(60\) LOC).}
443	    Discharge the \(\texttt{h\_compat}\) hypothesis of
444	    \(\texttt{Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ordFrac\_stalkIso\_naturality}\)
445	    by specialising \(e_K\) to
446	    \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso}\,U\) (\ref{def:functionFieldIso}).
447	    The compatibility reduces to a naturality of
448	    \(\texttt{stalkSpecializes}\) (to the generic point) along the
449	    open-immersion stalk iso \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\); the
450	    morphism-level form is the equation
451	    \(\texttt{stalkIso} \,\ggg\, \texttt{stalkSpecializes}\,(\text{gp}_X \rightsquigarrow Y)
452	     = \texttt{stalkSpecializes}\,(\text{gp}_U \rightsquigarrow Y')
453	       \,\ggg\, \texttt{functionFieldIso}\)
454	    in \(\texttt{CommRingCat}\). Reference: Mathlib's
455	    \(\texttt{PresheafedSpace.stalkMap.stalkSpecializes\_stalkMap\_assoc}\)
456	    (\texttt{Geometry.RingedSpace.Stalks}). Naturality of
457	    \(\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}\) across the stalk iso of
458	    Sub-build 1 follows by the resulting equation: for
459	    \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\)
460	    and \(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\),
461	    \(\texttt{order}\,Y\,f
462	     = \texttt{order}\,(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\,U\,Y\,h)\,f'\)
463	    with \(f'\) the image of \(f\) under
464	    \(X.\texttt{functionField} \simeq U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{functionField}\).
465	    Direct consumer of Sub-build 2.
466	  \item \textbf{Terminal closure (iter-203 target, \(\sim 40\)--\(80\) LOC).}
467	    Given a non-zero \(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\):
468	    (a) reduce to a finite affine cover \(X = \bigcup_{i = 1}^N U_i\)
469	    (Hartshorne II.6 hypothesis; project-side
470	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]} or just
471	    \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]}); (b) by Sub-build 3, every
472	    \(Y \in \texttt{support}\) lies in some \(U_i\) and has
473	    \(\texttt{order}\,Y\,f = \texttt{order}\,(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\,
474	     U_i\,Y\,h)\,f_i\) for \(f_i\) the corresponding fraction-field
475	    element on \(U_i\); (c) each \(U_i\) is affine Noetherian, so the
476	    height-1 prime decomposition of \(\mathrm{div}(f_i)\) in
477	    \(\Gamma(U_i, \mathcal O)\) is finite by Mathlib's
478	    \(\texttt{Ideal.finite\_minimalPrimes\_of\_isNoetherianRing}\)
479	    applied to the principal ideal \((f_i)\); (d) bound
480	    \(|\texttt{support}|\) by the sum of the finitely many
481	    \(|\texttt{support of div}(f_i)|\) terms; (e) conclude finiteness.
482	\end{itemize}

485	Archon-original; landed axiom-clean iter-202) are pinned below.

487	\begin{lemma}\leanok
488	[Function-field-iso compatibility, morphism level]
489	  \label{lem:functionFieldIso_compat}

491	  \uses{def:functionFieldIso, lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso}

493	  prime divisor \(Y\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\), the square

503	  \uses{def:functionFieldIso}

505	  an open \(V\) containing the point, both composites reduce to
506	  \(\texttt{germ} \,\ggg\, \texttt{stalkSpecializes}\) and agree by

508	  \(\texttt{stalkCongr}\) with \(\texttt{stalkSpecializes}\) along

511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Order is invariant under restriction to an open chart]
514	  \label{lem:order_eq_order_restrict}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.order_eq_order_restrict}
516	  \uses{lem:functionFieldIso_compat}
