546	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
547	\begin{definition}
548	\leanok
549	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
550	  \label{def:order_at_point}
551	  
552	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
554	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
555	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
556	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
557	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
558	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
559	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
560	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
561	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
562	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
563	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
564	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
565	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
566	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
567	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
568	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
569	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
570	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
571	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
572	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
573	  \[
574	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
575	  \]
576	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
577	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
578	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
579	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
580	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
581	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
582	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
583	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
584	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
585	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
586	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
587	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
588	  \begin{quote}
589	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
590	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
591	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
592	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
593	  \end{quote}
594	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
595	  see below). The body is
596	  \[
597	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
598	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
599	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
600	      \bigr).
601	  \]
602	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
603	  \begin{itemize}
604	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
605	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
606	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
607	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
608	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
609	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
610	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
611	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
612	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
613	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
614	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
615	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
616	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
617	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
618	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
619	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
620	      X.functionField} via
621	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
622	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
623	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
624	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
625	      and from the explicitly threaded
626	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
627	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
628	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
629	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
630	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
631	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
632	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
633	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
634	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
635	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
636	  \end{itemize}
637	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
638	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
639	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
640	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
641	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
642	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
643	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
644	  signature.
645	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
646	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
647	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
648	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
649	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
650	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
651	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
652	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
653	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
654	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
655	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
656	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
657	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
658	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
659	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
660	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
661	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
662	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
663	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
664	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
665	\end{definition}
666	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
667	\begin{definition}
668	\leanok
669	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
670	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
671	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
672	  
673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
674	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
675	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
676	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
677	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
678	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
679	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
680	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
681	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
682	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
683	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
684	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
685	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
686	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
687	  \[
688	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
689	  \]
690	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
691	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
692	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
693	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
694	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
695	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
696	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
697	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
698	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
699	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
700	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
701	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
702	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
703	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
704	  codim-\(1\) regime:
705	  \[
706	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
707	      \begin{cases}
708	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
709	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
710	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
711	      \end{cases}
712	  \]
713	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
714	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
715	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
716	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
717	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
718	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
719	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
720	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
721	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
722	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
723	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
724	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
725	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
726	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
727	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
728	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
729	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
730	\end{definition}
731	\begin{lemma}
732	\leanok
733	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
734	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
735	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
736	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
737	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
738	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
739	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
740	  then
741	  \[
742	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
743	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
744	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
745	  \]
746	\end{lemma}
747	\begin{proof}
748	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
749	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
750	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
751	\end{proof}
752	\begin{lemma}
753	\leanok
754	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
755	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
756	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
757	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
758	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
759	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
760	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
761	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
762	  scheme), then
763	  \[
764	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
765	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
766	  \]
767	\end{lemma}
768	\begin{proof}
769	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
770	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
771	  negative branch using the hypothesis
772	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
773	\end{proof}
774	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
775	\begin{definition}
776	\leanok
777	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
778	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
779	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
780	  
781	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
782	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
783	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
784	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
785	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
786	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
787	  nonsingular curve).}
788	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
789	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
790	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
791	  \[
792	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
793	  \]
794	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
795	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
796	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
797	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
798	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
799	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
800	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
801	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
802	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
803	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
804	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
805	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
806	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
807	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
808	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
809	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
810	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
811	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
812	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
813	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
814	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
815	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
816	\end{definition}
817	\begin{theorem}
818	\leanok
819	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
820	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
821	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
822	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
823	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
824	  homomorphism
825	  \[
826	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
827	    \qquad
828	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
829	    \qquad
830	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
831	  \]
832	\end{theorem}
833	\begin{proof}
834	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
835	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
836	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
837	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
838	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
839	\end{proof}
840	\section{Principal divisors}
841	\begin{lemma}
842	
843	\leanok
844	[Finite support of the order function — Hartshorne 6.1 / Stacks 02RV]
845	  \label{lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
846	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_order_finite_support}
847	  % \lean{...} corrected iter-199 review: the iter-199 plan-phase
848	  % pin used the wrong namespace (".Scheme.") and the Lean theorem at
849	  % WeilDivisor.lean:357 sits in `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` only
850	  % (Scheme.RationalMap closed at L159). The theorem is also declared
851	  % `private` — sync_leanok may not resolve private decls cleanly. The
852	  % chapter pin is retained as documentation for the future-closed
853	  % version; the iter-199 plan agent's directive to consume this via
854	  % the `[IsNoetherian X]` strengthening remains the closure path.
855	  \uses{def:order_at_point, def:prime_divisor}
856	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (read from
857	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
858	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
859	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
860	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
861	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1, p.~131 (a.k.a.\ Stacks 02RV).}
862	
863	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral scheme that is regular in
864	  codimension one, and let \(f \in K(X)\) be a nonzero rational function.
865	  Then \(\{Y \in X^{(1)} \mid \ord_Y(f) \ne 0\}\) is finite.
866	\end{lemma}
867	
868	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an open
869	% affine subset of \(X\) on which \(f\) is regular, \(f \in A\). Then for any
870	% prime divisor \(Y\) which meets \(U\), \(Y \cap U\) corresponds to a minimal
871	% prime ideal \(\mathfrak p\) of \(A\) containing \(f\). But these are finite
872	% in number (II, 3.10). On the other hand, \(X - U\) is a proper closed
873	% subset of \(X\), so it can contain only finitely many prime divisors of
874	% \(X\) [\dots]"
875	\begin{proof}
876	  \uses{def:order_at_point}
877	  Following Hartshorne II.6.1 / Stacks 02RV:
878	  \begin{enumerate}
879	  \item Pick a nonempty affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec} R \subset X\) on
880	    which \(f\) is regular. Then \(R\) is a Noetherian integral domain.
881	  \item For each prime divisor \(Y\) meeting \(U\), the corresponding
882	    prime \(\mathfrak p_Y\) is a height-1 prime of \(R\); the height-1 primes
883	    containing \(f \cdot 1\) are precisely the minimal primes of
884	    \((f)\), which by Krull's Hauptidealsatz form a finite set (Stacks tag
885	    00KZ / Atiyah--Macdonald 11.16). Of those, \(\ord_Y(f) \ne 0\) holds
886	    only at the finitely many minimal primes of \((f)\) over \(R\).
887	  \item The remaining prime divisors \(Y\) lie in the closed complement
888	    \(X \setminus U\), which is a proper closed subset of \(X\); the
889	    Noetherian topological structure of \(X\) (Stacks 04ZE) bounds the
890	    irreducible components of \(X \setminus U\) of codimension one in
891	    \(X\) by a finite number. For each such component, the stalk
892	    \(\mathcal O_{X, Y}\) is a DVR (regularity in codim 1) and \(f\) has
893	    finite valuation; together these contribute only finitely many
894	    nonzero values to the order function.
895	  \item Combining (2) and (3) gives the finiteness of the support.
896	  \end{enumerate}
897	  \emph{Hypothesis health.} The argument in (3) uses that \(X\) is
898	  Noetherian as a topological space (so \(X \setminus U\) has finitely
899	  many irreducible components). For \([X]\) only locally Noetherian +
900	  integral (without quasi-compactness), one can produce
901	  counter-examples with infinitely many disjoint codim-1 prime
902	  divisors outside \(U\). The Lean signature thus takes
903	  \texttt{[IsNoetherian X]} (or equivalently
904	  \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X] + [CompactSpace X]}). The hybrid
905	  WeilDivisor file pins the public-facing declaration under the
906	  weaker \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]} hypothesis and exposes the
907	  \texttt{[CompactSpace X]} (or full Noetherian) strengthening
908	  as the substantive sub-claim that this lemma resolves.
909	\end{proof}
910	
911	\begin{definition}
912	\leanok
913	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point, lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support}
914	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
915	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
916	  
917	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
918	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
919	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
920	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
921	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
922	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
923	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
924	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
925	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
926	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
927	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
928	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
929	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
930	  p.~131.}
931	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
932	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
933	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
934	  \[
935	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
936	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
937	  \]
938	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
939	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
940	  the same definition specialises to
941	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
942	  sum over closed points.
943	\end{definition}
944	\begin{theorem}
945	\leanok
946	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
947	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
948	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
949	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
950	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
951	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
952	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
953	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
954	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
955	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
956	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
957	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
958	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
959	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
960	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
961	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
962	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
963	  \[
964	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
965	    \qquad
966	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
967	    \qquad
968	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
969	  \]
970	\end{theorem}
971	\begin{proof}
972	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
973	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
974	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
975	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
976	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
977	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
978	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
979	  identities.
980	\end{proof}
981	\begin{theorem}
982	\leanok
983	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
984	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
985	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
986	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
987	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
988	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
989	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
990	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
991	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
992	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
993	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
994	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
995	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
996	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
997	  \[
998	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
999	  \]
1000	\end{theorem}

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,

14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

157	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
168	  The refinement
169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;
173	    \{\, f \;\mid\; U \ne \bot \,\lor\, f = 0 \,\}.
174	  \]

176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).

196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and

205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible

209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}

323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	  
393	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
394	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
395	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
396	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
397	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
398	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
399	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
400	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
401	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
402	  and only if:
403	
404	  \begin{itemize}
405	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
406	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
407	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
408	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
409	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
410	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
411	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
412	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
413	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
414	  \end{itemize}
415	  In divisorial language (REF,
416	  REF), the two conditions combine to
417	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
418	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
419	  zero global section.
420	
421	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
422	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
423	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
424	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
425	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
426	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
427	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
428	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
429	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
430	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
431	\end{proof}

433	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
434	
435	\begin{lemma}
436	
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
440	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
442	  
443	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
444	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
445	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
446	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
447	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
448	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
450	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
451	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
452	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
453	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
454	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
455	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
456	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
457	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
458	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
459	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
460	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
461	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
462	  \[
463	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
464	  \]
465	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
466	\end{lemma}
467	
468	\begin{proof}
469	  
470	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
471	  exact sequence
472	  \[
473	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
474	  \]
475	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
476	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
477	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
478	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
479	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
480	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
481	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
482	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
483	  \[
484	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
485	  \]
486	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
487	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
488	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
489	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
490	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
491	  \begin{multline*}
492	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
493	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
494	  \end{multline*}
495	  Two ingredients close the proof:
496	
497	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
498	  hypothesis: by REF,
499	  \[
500	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
501	  \]
502	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
503	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
504	
505	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
506	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
507	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
508	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
509	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
510	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
511	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
512	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
513	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
514	
515	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
516	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
517	  \[
518	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
519	  \]
520	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
521	
522	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
523	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
524	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
525	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
526	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
527	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
528	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
529	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
530	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
531	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
532	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
533	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
534	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
535	  complex degenerates).
536	\end{proof}

538	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
539	
540	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The Lean body is now structurally
541	% sorry-free (closed transitively through two named substrate
542	% helpers). The substrate helpers `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two`
543	% (the Int-valued $H^0 - H^1 = 2$ \(\chi\)-bridge; gated on
544	% `OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + `RRFormula.lean` $\chi$-additivity)
545	% and `h1_vanishing_genusZero` (gated on Lane H \texttt{H1Vanishing.lean}
546	% substrate) carry the remaining typed sorries.  Future consumers
547	% reading the body should consult those.
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	
551	\leanok
552	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
553	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
554	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
555	  
556	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
557	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
558	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
559	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
560	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
561	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
562	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
563	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
564	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
565	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
566	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
567	  % is the Euler characteristic
568	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
569	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
570	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
571	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
572	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
573	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
574	  \[
575	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
576	  \]
577	\end{theorem}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  
581	  
582	  
583	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
584	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
585	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
586	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
587	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
588	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
589	  \[
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
591	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
592	  \]
593	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
594	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
595	  \[
596	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
597	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
598	    \;=\; 2.
599	  \]
600	  By
601	  REF, the second
602	  term vanishes:
603	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
604	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
605	
606	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
607	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
608	  REF computes
609	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
610	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
611	  \[
612	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
613	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
614	  \]
615	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
616	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
617	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
618	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
619	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
620	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
621	  segment yields the short exact sequence
622	  \[
623	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
624	  \]
625	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
626	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
627	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
628	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
629	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
630	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
631	\end{proof}

801	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
802	  Unfold the witness section \(s\) along the linear-equivalence chain
803	  defining \(\iota = \texttt{toFunctionField}\) (the five-layer
804	  composition recorded in
805	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}). The
806	  underlying carrier element at the top open is a pair
807	  \((\widetilde f,\,p) \in \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(\op\top)\)
808	  with \(\widetilde f = f\) (from the hypothesis \(\iota(s) = f\)) and
809	  \(p\) a membership witness. By
810	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}, membership
811	  in \(\mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(\op\top)\) unfolds as the
812	  conjunction of (i) order \(\ge 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\) of
813	  \(\top\) other than \(P\), and (ii) order \(\ge -1\) at \(P\)
814	  (intersected with the trivial \texttt{trivAtBot} factor, which is
815	  \(\top\) on the top open). Projecting \(p\) onto its first and
816	  second components returns exactly the pair of order bounds claimed
817	  (the auxiliary \(Q.\mathrm{point}\in \mathrm{Set.univ}\) premises
818	  are discharged by \(\mathrm{Set.mem\_univ}\)). The nonzero hypothesis
819	  on \(f\) is carried for downstream typing but is not consumed in the
820	  membership-projection step.
821	\end{proof}
822	
823	\begin{lemma}
824	
825	\leanok
826	[Sub-claim (c) substrate: a rational function with zero order at every
827	prime divisor on a complete curve is constant
828	(Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~\texttt{02P0})]
829	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero}
830	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero}
831	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
832	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
833	  over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Let \(f \in K(C)\) be
834	  a nonzero rational function such that
835	  \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in C\). Then \(f\)
836	  lies in the image of the constant inclusion
837	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\): there exists \(c \in \bar k\) with
838	  \[
839	    f \;=\; \texttt{algebraMap}\,\bar k\,K(C)\,c.
840	  \]
841	\end{lemma}
842	
843	% NOTE (iter-197 rename — substrate vs.\ contrapositive). The original
844	% iter-196 phrasing of sub-claim~(c) was the contrapositive form
845	% ``principal divisor of a non-constant rational function on a complete
846	% curve is non-zero'':
847	%   \mathtt{Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}\,f\,h_f \;\ne\; 0
848	% whenever \(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) and \(f = \iota(s)\). The two
849	% forms are mathematically equivalent: \(\mathrm{principal}\,f =
850	% \sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot[Q]\), so \(\mathrm{principal}\,f = 0\) iff
851	% \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q\), and the substrate
852	% above then forces \(f \in \bar k\). The iter-196 prover chose to
853	% extract the Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~\texttt{02P0} form as the
854	% standalone named substrate — it cleanly isolates the substantive
855	% Mathlib gap (``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete
856	% geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)'') — and inlined the
857	% contrapositive ``\(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1 \Rightarrow
858	% \mathrm{principal}\,f \ne 0\)'' wrapper inside
859	% \texttt{exists\_nonconstant\_rational\_from\_dim\_eq\_two}
860	% (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean} lines~1596--1635).
861	% The contrapositive runs as follows: assume \(\mathrm{principal}\,f
862	% = 0\); apply the substrate to obtain \(f = c \in \bar k\); use
863	% \(\bar k\)-linearity of \(\iota\) plus \(\iota(s_1) = 1\) and
864	% injectivity of \(\iota\)
865	% (\cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}) to
866	% conclude \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting
867	% \(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1\).
868	
869	\begin{proof}
870	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
871	  This is the classical Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement
872	  ``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete geometrically
873	  irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically closed field
874	  \(\bar k\)'', combined with the
875	  ``no-zeros-no-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' content of
876	  Stacks~\texttt{02P0}, in two ingredients:
877	
878	  \emph{Step (i) — algebraic Hartogs.} On the normal Noetherian scheme
879	  \(C\), a rational function regular at every codimension-\(1\) point
880	  extends uniquely to a global section of the structure sheaf
881	  (Stacks tag~\texttt{0BCK}: $\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) =
882	  \bigcap_{Q\in C^{(1)}} \mathcal O_{C, Q}$). On the smooth curve
883	  \(C\) every prime divisor \(Q\) is a codimension-\(1\) point and
884	  \(\ord_Q\) is the standard discrete valuation on
885	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\); the hypothesis \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every
886	  \(Q\) places \(f\) in the intersection
887	  \(\bigcap_Q \mathcal O_{C, Q} = \Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)\).
888	
889	  \emph{Step (ii) — global sections are constants.} On a proper
890	  geometrically irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically
891	  closed field \(\bar k\), the global-sections ring of the structure
892	  sheaf coincides with the base field,
893	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4; or
894	  cohomologically: \(\Gamma\) is finite-dimensional over \(\bar k\)
895	  by Hartshorne~III.5.2, hence integral over \(\bar k\), hence
896	  algebraic, hence \(\bar k\) by algebraic closure). Combining
897	  step~(i) with step~(ii) places \(f\) in the image of the constant
898	  inclusion \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\), as required.
899	
900	  \emph{Mathlib status.} At snapshot \texttt{b80f227} both ingredients
901	  are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps: the Lean substrate helper carries
902	  a single typed sorry pending upstream development; the named-helper
903	  extraction makes the upstream dependency explicit for future
904	  iterations.
905	\end{proof}

907	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
908	
909	\begin{corollary}
910	
911	
912	\leanok
913	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
914	single simple pole]
915	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
916	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
917	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
918	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
919	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
920	  
921	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
922	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
923	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
924	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
925	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
926	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
927	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
928	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
929	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
930	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
931	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
932	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
933	  \begin{itemize}
934	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
935	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
936	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
937	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
938	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
939	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
940	  \end{itemize}
941	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
942	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
943	\end{corollary}
944	
945	\begin{proof}
946	  
947	  
948	  
949	  By
950	  REF,
951	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
952	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
953	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
954	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
955	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
956	  identification of
957	  REF), is a one-dimensional
958	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
959	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
960	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
961	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
962	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
963	
964	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
965	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
966	  REF the section
967	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
968	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
969	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
970	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
971	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
972	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
973	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
974	  of the statement are now immediate.
975	
976	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
977	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
978	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
979	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
980	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
981	
982	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
983	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
984	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
985	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
986	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
987	  REF's output. The
988	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
989	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
990	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
991	\end{proof}

3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
