136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  
143	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
144	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
145	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
146	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
147	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
148	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
149	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
150	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
151	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
153	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
154	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
155	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
156	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
157	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
158	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
159	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
161	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
162	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
163	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
164	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
165	  % field of \(X\)."
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
167	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
168	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
169	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
170	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
171	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
172	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
173	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
176	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
177	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
178	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
179	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
180	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
181	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
182	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
183	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
184	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
185	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
186	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
187	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
188	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
189	  field) defined section-wise by
190	  \[
191	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
192	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
193	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
194	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
195	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
196	  \]
197	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
198	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
199	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
200	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
201	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
202	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
204	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
205	
206	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
207	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
208	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
209	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
210	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
211	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
212	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
213	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
214	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
215	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
216	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
217	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
218	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
219	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
220	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
221	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
222	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
223	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
224	  \(U_i\).
225	
226	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
227	  REF of REF)
228	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
229	  \mathcal K_C\( is an invertible sheaf on \)C$, independently of the
230	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
231	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
232	
233	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
235	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
236	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
237	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
238	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
239	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
240	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
241	  REF of
242	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
243	  REF below. The construction follows
244	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
245	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
246	  \(\bar k\)-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
247	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
248	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
249	\end{definition}
250	
251	\section{Immediate corollaries}
252	
253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
254	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  
258	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
265	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  
270	  At \(D = 0\) the coefficient \(n_Q = 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\), so the
271	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
272	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
290	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
291	REF]
292	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
294	  
295	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  \([P] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) via REF. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) of REF agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
306	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  
311	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
312	  \([P]\) has coefficients \(n_Q = 1\) if \(Q = P\) and \(n_Q = 0\) otherwise
313	  (where \(P\) is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
314	  REF). Thus
315	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) contains \(0\) and those
316	  \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) satisfying \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime
317	  divisor \(Q \in U\) with \(Q \neq P\), plus \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\) if
318	  \(P \in U\).
319	
320	  On the REF side, the same conditions
321	  are exactly the ones extracted by
322	  REF of
323	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open \(U\) (not only at the global section
324	  \(U = C\)): a rational function \(f \in K(C)\) is a section of
325	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) on \(U\) iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
326	  \(Q \in U \setminus \{P\}\) and has at most a simple pole at \(P\) when
327	  \(P \in U\).
328	
329	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on \(K(C)\). The two
330	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) are therefore equal
331	  section-by-section as subsets of \(K(C)\), and the equality propagates
332	  to an equality of sheaves of \(\mathcal O_C\)-modules. The
333	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
334	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\), so the identification is
335	  \(\bar k\)-linear on each section.
336	\end{proof}
337	
338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
339	[Short exact sequence for \(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
344	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any
345	  % point...''). Read from
346	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
347	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
348	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
349	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
350	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
351	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
352	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
353	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
354	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
355	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\).
356	  % (Since \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
357	  % it does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\).)"
358	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
359	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
360	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
361	  \(\bar k\), let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor, and let \(P \in C\) be
362	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
363	  REF on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
364	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) of
365	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) fits into a short exact
366	  sequence
367	  \[
368	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
372	  \]
373	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (equivalently, in
374	  \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)), where \(k(P)\) denotes the
375	  skyscraper sheaf at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\) (the residue field of \(P\)
376	  is \(\bar k\) since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(P\) is a
377	  \(\bar k\)-rational closed point).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
383	  proceeds in two beats:
384	
385	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at \(P\)).} The closed-subscheme
386	  inclusion \(P \hookrightarrow C\) has structure sheaf
387	  \(\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)\) (a skyscraper at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\)),
388	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
389	  \(\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])\) (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
390	  \(\mathcal K_C\) generated by a local uniformiser \(\pi_P\) at \(P\) and by
391	  \(1\) away from \(P\), i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
392	  principal closed subscheme \(P\)). This produces the structural short
393	  exact sequence
394	  \[
395	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
396	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
397	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
399	  \]
400	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\).
401	
402	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\)).} The line bundle
403	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank~\(1\)
404	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), REF), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
405	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\( is exact on \)\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
406	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
407	  \[
408	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
409	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
410	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
411	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
412	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
413	  \]
414	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
415	  \begin{itemize}
416	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
417	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
418	      \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\)-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
419	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
420	      ring-theoretic identity
421	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
422	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
423	      rational functions, where \(n_Q^{D}\) is the coefficient of \(Q\)
424	      in \(D\));
425	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
426	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
427	    \item \(k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)\) (tensoring a
428	      skyscraper at \(P\) by a locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf preserves the
429	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over \(P\) the line bundle
430	      \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P\) is a free \(\mathcal O_{C,P}\)-module of
431	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
432	      \cong k(P)\( is just \)k(P)\(; away from \)P$ the skyscraper is the
433	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
434	  \end{itemize}
435	  Substituting yields
436	  \[
437	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
438	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
439	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
440	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
441	  \]
442	  exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), as required.
443	
444	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
445	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is the
446	  inclusion of subsheaves of \(\mathcal K_C\): at the level of sections,
447	  the constraint \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) of
448	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) implies the weaker constraint
449	  \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1\) of \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
450	  when \(P \in U\) (constraints on \(Q \neq P\) are unchanged), and the map
451	  is identity on \(K(C)\) at the carrier level. The second map
452	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)\) is the evaluation
453	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at \(P\)'' (i.e.\ the
454	  \(\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}\)-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of \(f\)
455	  at \(P\)), which is well-defined on \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
456	  modulo the subgroup of sections with \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) ---
457	  i.e.\ modulo \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
458	\end{proof}
459	
460	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
461	
462	The construction of REF is presheaf-by-hand: a
463	per-open \(\bar k\)-submodule of \(K(C)\), with restriction along
464	\(V \hookrightarrow U\) given by the identity on \(K(C)\). This section
465	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
466	target category \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
467	
468	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For \(V \subseteq U\), the
469	constraint set defining \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is a \emph{subset}
470	of the constraint set defining \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\) (fewer
471	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on \(K(C)\) restricts
472	to a well-defined inclusion
473	\(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
474	Composition \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) is again identity-on-\(K(C)\), so
475	strict functoriality holds.
476	
477	\paragraph{\(\bar k\)-linearity.} A \(\bar k\)-scalar \(\lambda \in \bar k\)
478	acts on \(K(C)\) by multiplication, and
479	\(\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)\) for \(\lambda \neq 0\)
480	(units have order zero); for \(\lambda = 0\), \(\lambda \cdot f = 0\) is
481	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
482	\(\bar k\)-submodule and the restriction maps are \(\bar k\)-linear.
483	
484	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
485	``\(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\)'' is
486	\emph{stalk-local} at each \(Q\): it depends only on the germ of \(f\) at
487	the point of \(C\) underlying \(Q\), equivalently on the image of \(f\) in
488	the DVR \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\). Consequently the sheaf property reduces
489	to two ingredients:
490	\begin{enumerate}
491	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is a
492	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at \(K(C)\).} The constant
493	    presheaf at \(K(C)\) is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
494	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
495	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
496	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
497	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
498	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
499	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
500	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
501	    prime divisor.
502	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
503	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) to
504	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})\).} The categorical forget
505	    \(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}\) creates limits, hence
506	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules is a
507	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
508	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
509	    \(\bar k\)-module-valued \(\mathcal O_C(D)\).
510	\end{enumerate}
511	
512	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open \(U \subseteq C\) disjoint from
513	the support of \(D\), the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
514	constraint \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\); hence
515	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U\), and the trivialisation
516	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U\) is the identity. On a small
517	open \(U \subseteq C\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) from the
518	support of \(D\), the local generator \(\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)\) trivialises
519	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U\) as a free rank-\(1\) \(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-module: a
520	section \(f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
521	-n_Q\( iff \)\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
522	``multiplication by \(\pi_Q^{n_Q}\)'' map is an
523	\(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-linear isomorphism
524	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U\). The two types
525	of trivialising opens cover \(C\) (any prime divisor lies in at most
526	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of \(D\) is
527	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
528	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
529	the cocycle is in \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}\) and the sheaf is invertible.
530	
531	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
532	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
533	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
534	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
535	landed the analogous bottom-up
536	\(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)-valued construction for the
537	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
538	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
539	\texttt{RR.1} (REF and surrounding lemmas).

580	\section{Out of scope}
581	
582	\begin{itemize}
583	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
584	    isomorphism \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
585	    \(D \sim D'\) (i.e.\ \(D - D' = \div(f)\) for some
586	    \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
587	    It is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
588	    consumes only \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))\) and the SES additivity at
589	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
590	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
591	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) rather than
592	    in \(\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \mathrm{Div}(C) / \sim\). The
593	    Cartier-divisor \(\leftrightarrow\) \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
594	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
595	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
596	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
597	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
598	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
599	    REF at the single instance
600	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
601	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
602	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
603	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
604	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
605	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
606	  \item \textbf{Pullback of \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) along a morphism.} The
607	    pullback \(f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)\) for
608	    a morphism of curves \(f : C' \to C\), used in degree-computation
609	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
610	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path.
611	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
612	    space.} The identification
613	    \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)\) for a general Weil divisor
614	    \(D\) (i.e.\ the analogue of
615	    REF for general
616	    \(D\)) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
617	    case \(D = [P]\) is the only specialisation consumed by the
618	    genus-\(0\) critical path, and it is handled in
619	    \texttt{RR.3} via REF.
620	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
621	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
622	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme
623	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) critical path
624	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
625	    does not engage them.
626	\end{itemize}

16	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}

24	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map

49	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
50	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
51	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
53	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,

80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	\leanok
84	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
85	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
86	  
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
89	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
90	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
91	  \[
92	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
93	  \]
94	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
95	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
96	  \[
97	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
98	  \]
99	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
100	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
101	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
102	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
103	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
104	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
106	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
107	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
108	\end{lemma}
109	\begin{proof}
110	
111	
112	\leanok
113	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
114	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
115	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
116	  under the name
117	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
118	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
119	  \begin{quote}
120	  \small
121	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
122	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
123	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
124	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
125	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
127	  \end{quote}
128	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
129	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
130	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
131	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
132	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
133	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
134	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
135	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
136	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
137	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
138	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
139	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
140	  project-bespoke wrapper
141	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
142	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
143	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
144	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
145	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
146	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
147	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
148	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
149	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
150	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
151	\end{proof}
152	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
153	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
154	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
155	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
156	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
157	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
158	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
159	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
160	\begin{lemma}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
164	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
165	  
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
167	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
168	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
169	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
170	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
171	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
172	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
173	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
174	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
175	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
176	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
177	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
178	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
179	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
180	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
181	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
182	  \[
183	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
184	    \quad\text{and}\quad
185	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
186	  \]
187	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
188	  \[
189	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
190	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
191	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
192	  \]
193	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
194	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
195	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
196	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
197	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
198	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
199	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
200	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
201	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
202	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
203	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
204	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
205	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
206	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
207	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
208	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
209	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
210	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
211	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
212	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
213	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
214	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
215	\end{lemma}
216	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
217	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
218	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
219	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
220	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
221	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as
222	% `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart`; the substantive Hartshorne~II.6.9
223	% identity that powers `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is now pinned as
224	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` in `WeilDivisor.tex`.
225	% With these substrates in place, `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is
226	% mathematically correct as stated: the body's existential witness `D`
227	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
228	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
229	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
230	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
231	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
232	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
233	%   2. New lemma
234	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a
235	%      named typed-sorry pin in `WeilDivisor.lean` (~5 LOC signature; body
236	%      ~50-80 LOC via affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route
237	%      owed iter-191+).
238	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
239	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
240	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
241	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
242	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
243	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
244	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
245	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
246	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
247	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
248	\begin{proof}
249	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
250	  \leanok
251	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
252	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
253	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
254	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
255	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
256	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
257	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
258	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
259	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
260	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
261	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
262	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
263	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
264	  is finite.
265	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
266	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
267	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
268	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
269	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
270	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
271	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
272	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
273	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
274	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
275	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
276	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
277	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
278	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
279	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
280	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
281	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
282	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
283	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
284	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
285	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
286	\end{proof}
287	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
288	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
289	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
290	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
291	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly
292	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
293	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
294	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
295	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
296	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
297	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
298	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
299	% only the input shape changes.
300	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer; refreshed iter-196): The Lean
301	% `iso_of_degree_one` body inline Step 1 was refactored iter-194 from
302	% 16 LOC of `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one +
303	% Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff + RingHom.injective +
304	% RingEquiv.ofBijective` to 4 LOC consuming two new axiom-clean substrate
305	% helpers (`algebraMap_bijective_of_finrank_one` +

307	% `phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one` was reformulated iter-196
308	% via Mathlib's `LocallyQuasiFinite.of_finite_preimage_singleton`
309	% (`QuasiFinite.lean:295`) with in-scope derivation of `IsProper φ.left`
310	% + `LocallyOfFiniteType φ.left`; the residual gap is now the concrete
311	% topological claim `(φ.left ⁻¹' {x}).Finite` for arbitrary

318	\begin{lemma}
319	
320	\leanok
321	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
322	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
323	  
324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
325	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
326	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
327	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
328	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
329	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
330	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
331	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
332	  % equivalent:}
333	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
334	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
335	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
336	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
337	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
338	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
339	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
340	  (p.~299).}
341	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
342	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
343	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
344	  degree one,
345	  \[
346	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
347	  \]
348	  formally
349	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
350	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
351	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
352	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
353	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
354	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
355	  \[
356	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
357	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
358	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
359	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
360	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
361	  \]
362	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
363	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
364	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
365	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
366	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
367	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
368	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
369	\end{lemma}
370	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
371	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
372	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
373	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
374	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
375	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
376	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
377	\begin{proof}
378	
379	
380	\leanok
381	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
382	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
383	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
384	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
385	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
386	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
387	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
388	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
389	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
390	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
391	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
392	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
393	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
394	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
395	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
396	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
397	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
398	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
399	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
400	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
401	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
402	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
403	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
404	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
405	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
406	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
407	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
408	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
409	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
410	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
411	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
412	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
413	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
414	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
415	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
416	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
417	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
418	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
419	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
420	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
421	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
422	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
423	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
424	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
425	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
426	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
427	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
428	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
429	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
430	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
431	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
432	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
433	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
434	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
435	\end{proof}
436	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
437	\begin{theorem}
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
441	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
442	  
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
444	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
445	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
446	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
447	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
448	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
449	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
450	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
451	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
452	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
453	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
454	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
455	  \[
456	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
457	  \]
458	  Concretely, the Lean signature
459	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
460	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
461	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
462	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
463	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
464	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
465	\end{theorem}
466	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
467	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
468	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
469	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
470	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
471	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
472	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
473	\begin{proof}
474	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
475	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
476	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
477	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
478	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
479	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
480	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
481	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
482	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
483	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
484	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
485	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
486	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
487	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
488	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
489	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
490	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
491	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
492	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
493	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
494	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
495	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
496	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
497	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
498	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
499	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
500	  exists a unique morphism
501	  \[
502	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
503	  \]
504	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
505	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
506	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
507	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
508	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
509	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
510	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
511	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
512	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
513	  \[
514	    \deg(\varphi)
515	    \;=\;
516	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
517	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
518	  \]
519	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
520	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
521	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
522	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
523	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
524	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
525	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
526	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
527	  and
528	  \[
529	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
530	  \]
531	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
532	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
533	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
534	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
535	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
536	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
537	   C.functionField]} instance). By
538	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
539	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
540	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
541	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
542	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
543	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
544	  closing the headline target
545	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
546	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
547	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
548	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
549	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
550	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
551	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
552	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
553	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
554	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
555	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
556	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
557	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
558	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
559	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
560	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
561	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
562	\end{proof}
563	\begin{remark}
564	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
565	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}

576	\end{remark}
577	\section{Out of scope}
578	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
579	are sequenced elsewhere:
580	\begin{itemize}
581	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
582	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
583	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
584	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
585	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
586	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
587	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
588	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
589	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
590	    consumed as a black box by
591	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
592	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
593	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
594	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
595	    is consumed as a black box by \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
596	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
597	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
598	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
599	    the same logical level as the closely related
600	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
601	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
602	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
603	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
604	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
605	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
606	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
607	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
608	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
609	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
610	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
611	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
612	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
613	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
614	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
615	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
616	    does not restate it.
617	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a curve
618	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
619	    particular the \(H^1\)-vanishing of \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a genus-\(0\)
620	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
621	    \texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
622	    re-derived here.
623	\end{itemize}

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
