59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
362	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
363	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
364	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
365	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
366	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
367	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
368	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
369	  closed embedding.
370	
371	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
372	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
373	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
374	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
375	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
376	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
377	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
378	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
379	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
380	
381	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
382	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
383	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
384	
385	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
386	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
387	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
388	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
389	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
390	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
391	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
392	\end{proof}
393	
394	\section{Group-scheme structure}
395	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
396	
397	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
398	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
399	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
400	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
401	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
402	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
403	
404	\begin{theorem}\leanok
405	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
406	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
408	  
409	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
410	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
411	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
412	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
413	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
416	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
417	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
418	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
419	  %  topologies  by
420	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
421	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
422	  %   \end{dfn}"
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
424	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
425	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
426	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
427	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
428	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
429	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
430	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
431	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
432	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
433	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
434	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
435	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
436	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
437	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
438	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
439	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
440	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
441	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
442	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
443	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
444	  scheme locally of finite type.
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
448	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
449	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
450	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
451	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
452	% records the standard transfer.
453	\begin{proof}
454	  
455	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
468	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
473	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}\leanok
488	[The Picard scheme]
489	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
491	  
492	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
493	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
494	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
497	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
498	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
499	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
500	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
501	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
502	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
503	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
504	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
505	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
506	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
507	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
508	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
509	\end{definition}
510	
511	\section{Lean encoding}
512	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
513	
514	The Lean target file is the new
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
516	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
517	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
518	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
519	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
520	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
521	
522	\begin{itemize}
523	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
524	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
525	    representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); it is the witness extracted
526	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
527	    \texttt{representable} below.
528	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
529	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
530	    \(k\)-schemes \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) sending a
531	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
532	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
533	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
534	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
535	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
536	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
537	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
538	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
539	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
540	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
541	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
542	    \times_k Z\( on a quasi-projective \)k\(-scheme \)Z$ to a
543	    quasi-projective quotient \(Q\), packaged in the form that consumes
544	    a \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for \(Z\) and produces
545	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
546	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
547	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
548	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
549	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
550	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
551	    \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for
552	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) via the four steps of the proof above
553	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, \(m\)-regularity bound, Abel-map
554	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
555	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
556	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
557	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
558	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
559	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
560	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
561	\end{itemize}
562	
563	No new core axiom is required: this chapter is an assembly chapter on
564	top of the QuotScheme engine, the relative-Picard \'etale sheafification,
565	and the line-bundle pullback machinery already in place. The single
566	large external input (the Altman--Kleiman theorem of effective
567	flat-and-proper equivalence relations on quasi-projective schemes) is
568	recorded as \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} and is itself a standard
569	result; if its Lean formalisation is not yet in Mathlib, the
570	\texttt{smoothProperQuotient} Lean declaration may be sorried and the
571	gap reported to the plan agent, as it is logically downstream of the

784	\subsection{Sorry 4 --- \texttt{instHasSmoothProperQuotient} (L349)}
785	\label{subsec:sorry_smooth_proper_quotient}
786	
787	\paragraph{Location.} Lean file
788	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean},
789	line~349; instance constructor
790	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.instHasSmoothProperQuotient}
791	of the iter-199 \texttt{Prop}-valued typeclass
792	\texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient} (L338-341). The associated theorem
793	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
794	(L377) extracts \texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient.is\_representable}
795	and is itself axiom-clean.
796	
797	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The smooth-proper quotient lemma
798	of \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}: given a surjection
799	$\alpha : Z \to P$ of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ with $Z$
800	quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$ representable, and the first
801	projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper, the quotient $P$ is
802	representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme. The proof packages
803	the Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem for
804	flat-and-proper relations on quasi-projective schemes.
805	
806	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
807	\begin{itemize}
808	  \item \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable},
809	    \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Limits.pullback}:
810	    \emph{exists}.
811	  \item \texttt{Smooth}, \texttt{IsProper} typeclasses on scheme
812	    morphisms: \emph{exists}.
813	  \item EGA IV~8.11.5 "proper monomorphism is a closed immersion":
814	    \emph{missing}. Mathlib has closed immersions, monomorphisms of
815	    schemes, and properness, but the EGA combination lemma is not yet
816	    isolated as a named result.
817	  \item Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem
818	    (flat-and-proper equivalence relation on a quasi-projective
819	    scheme is effective, with quasi-projective quotient):
820	    \emph{missing}. This is the structural heart of the lemma and is
821	    not in Mathlib in any form.
822	  \item Coequaliser uniqueness in the category of \'etale sheaves:
823	    \emph{exists} (general categorical fact, uniqueness up to unique
824	    isomorphism of coequalisers in any category).
825	\end{itemize}
826	
827	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} None: the lemma is a
828	structural fact about quasi-projective schemes and \'etale sheaves,
829	independent of curves and Riemann--Roch. It does, however, sit on the
830	Mathlib gap "effective equivalence relations of schemes", which is a
831	substantial development in its own right (corresponds to roughly
832	Altman--Kleiman, "Compactifying the Picard scheme", and the
833	flat-and-proper descent of equivalence relations from EGA IV~17).
834	
835	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Two routes are available:
836	\begin{enumerate}
837	  \item[(a)] Wait for the Mathlib gap "effective equivalence
838	    relations of quasi-projective schemes" to be filled and import
839	    the result. This is the cleanest long-term path but is outside
840	    the project's control timeline.
841	  \item[(b)] Formalise the Altman--Kleiman construction directly in
842	    the project as a sibling Lean file, gated on the EGA IV~8.11.5
843	    lemma (proper monomorphism is a closed immersion) which can be
844	    extracted from existing Mathlib infrastructure in a focused
845	    sub-task.
846	\end{enumerate}
847	Route~(b) is the recommended iter-199+ attempt: the EGA IV~8.11.5
848	extraction is bounded and the remaining structural construction
849	(graph of the equivalence relation inside the Hilbert scheme of $Z$,
850	descent to a closed subscheme of $\Hilb_{Z/k}$) is intrinsic.
851	
852	\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 2} (substantial Mathlib
853	gap; no Riemann--Roch substrate; recommended first attempt under the

864	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The carrier asserts that there
865	exists an object $X \in \mathrm{Over}(\Spec k)$ together with a
866	\texttt{RepresentableBy} witness for $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$. In
867	other words: the Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$ exists. The semantic
868	content is the conclusion of Kleiman \S4 Thm.~\texttt{th:main}
869	specialised by Cor.~\texttt{cor:algsch} to $S = \Spec k$ and $X = C$
870	a smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
871	
872	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
873	\begin{itemize}
874	  \item \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Nonempty},
875	    \texttt{Over}: \emph{exists}.
876	  \item \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1},
877	    \texttt{IsProper}, \texttt{GeometricallyIntegral}: \emph{exists}
878	    as typeclasses, but the rich theory needed to actually produce a
879	    representing scheme is largely missing.
880	  \item The FGA representability machinery itself (the four-step
881	    construction of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, namely
882	    Hilbert polynomial stratification, $m$-regularity bound, Abel
883	    map factorisation, smooth-proper quotient): \emph{missing}.
884	  \item Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity / $m$-regularity for
885	    cohomologically twist-vanishing sheaves: \emph{missing}.
886	  \item Riemann--Roch for line bundles on a smooth proper curve over
887	    a field (used in the $m$-regularity step to bound twists by the
888	    degree): \emph{missing} (Route~C).

891	\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} \textbf{Required}. The
892	$m$-regularity bound (Kleiman \S4 Step~2 of the proof of
893	Thm.~\texttt{th:main}) is the point at which the structural argument
894	meets Riemann--Roch: for a curve $C/k$ of genus $g$ and a line
895	bundle $\mathcal{L}$ of degree $d$, $H^1(C, \mathcal{L}(n)) = 0$ for
896	$n \geq m$ depends on $m \geq 2g - 1 - d + \deg \mathcal{O}_C(1)$, which
897	is the Serre vanishing consequence of Riemann--Roch. Specifically the
898	required result is the curve specialisation of
899	Hartshorne~III.5.2 (Serre vanishing) combined with IV.1.3
900	(Riemann--Roch for curves). Without Riemann--Roch, the $m$-regularity
901	step cannot be discharged and the FGA construction does not
902	terminate.

905	re-engagement that the project STRATEGY.md flags as

909	smooth-proper quotient via Sorry~4) assemble into the existence
910	witness. Until then this sorry is the load-bearing blocker on the

914	substrate; load-bearing on the entire A.2.c representability claim).

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.
78	  %
79	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
80	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
81	  % extending \(k\).
82	  %  \end{lem}"
83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}

116	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
118	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)

138	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite

150	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
152

159	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
160	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
161	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
162	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}

208	\begin{lemma}
209	[Stacks 037Q iff-direction: connected + section ⟹ geometrically connected]
210	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
212	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], tag 037Q (criteria for geometric connectedness via
213	  % algebraic closure of the base field in the global sections) +
214	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE: Stacks 037Q (Lemma): "Let \(X\) be a scheme over the
216	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
217	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)
218	  % \(X\) is connected and \(k\) is algebraically closed in \(K\), (3)
219	  % \(K\) is a field and the inclusion \(k \subset K\) is purely
220	  % inseparable."  (read from references/stacks-varieties.tex section
221	  % \S geom-connected, tag 037Q).
222	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)).}
223	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
224	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
225	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
226	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
227	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
228	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
229	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
230	  connected.
231	\end{lemma}
232	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
233	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
234	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
235	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
236	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
237	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
238	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
239	% ring is a field is connected.''
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
242	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
243	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
244	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
245	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
246	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
247	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
248	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
249	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
250	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
251	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
252	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
253	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
254	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
255	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
256	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
257	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
258	  is therefore connected.
259	
260	  Spelled out: this lemma is the iter-194 first-class formalisation of
261	  the Stacks 037Q iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1) specialised to the
262	  presence of a \(k\)-rational section (which automatically supplies
263	  the \enquote{algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(K\) equals \(k\)}
264	  condition via the section's pullback on global sections — see
265	  Stacks 037R for that step).
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	% Note: the lemma above is the substrate needed for
269	% thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes via the route described
270	% in the proof block below. iter-194+ prover work closes its body.
271	
272	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
273	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
274	% (3).)
275	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
276	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
277	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
278	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
279	% \(G^0\) is open too.
280	%
281	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
282	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
283	% extending \(k\).
284	%
285	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
286	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
287	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
288	%
289	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
290	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
291	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
292	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
293	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
294	% [\dots]
295	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
296	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
297	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
298	% [\dots]
299	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
300	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
301	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
302	\begin{proof}
303	  We establish
304	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
305	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
306	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
307	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
308	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
309	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
310	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
311	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
312	
313	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
314	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
315	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
316	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
317	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
318	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
319	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
320	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
321	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
322	
323	  \emph{Base-change commutation
324	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
325	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
326	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
327	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
328	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
329	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
330	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
331	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
332	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
333	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
334	
335	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
336	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
337	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
338	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
339	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
340	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
341	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
342	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
343	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
344	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
345	
346	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
347	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
348	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
349	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
350	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
351	  \(\overline k\), the product
352	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
353	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
354	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
355	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
356	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
357	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
358	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
359	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
360	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
361	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
362	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
363	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
364	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
365	\end{proof}

367	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
368	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
369	
370	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
371	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
372	
373	\begin{definition}\leanok
374	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
375	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
377	  
378	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
379	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
380	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
381	  % L2921-L2929).
382	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
383	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
384	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
385	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
386	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
387	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
388	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
389	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
390	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
391	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
392	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
393	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
394	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
395	  % \(k\).
396	  %  \end{prp}"
397	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
398	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
399	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
400	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
401	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
402	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
403	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
404	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
405	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
406	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
407	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
408	  extension of the base field.
409	\end{definition}
410	
411	\section{The degree map}
412	\label{sec:pic_degree}
413	
414	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
415	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
416	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
417	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
418	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
419	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
420	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
421	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
422	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
423	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
424	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
425	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
426	degree.
427	
428	\begin{definition}\leanok
429	[Degree of a Picard class]
430	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
432	  
433	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
434	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
435	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
436	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
437	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
438	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
439	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
440	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
441	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
442	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
443	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
444	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
445	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
446	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
447	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
448	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
449	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
450	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
451	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
452	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
453	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
454	  \[
455	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
456	  \]
457	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
458	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
459	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
460	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
461	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
462	  \[
463	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
464	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
465	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
466	  \]
467	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
468	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
469	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
470	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
471	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
472	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
473	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
474	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
475	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
476	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
477	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
478	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
479	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
480	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
481	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
482	  of degree \(d\).
483	\end{definition}
484	
485	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
486	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
487	
488	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
489	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
490	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
491	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
492	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
493	automatically.
494	
495	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
496	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
497	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
498	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
499	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
500	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
501	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
502	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
503	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
504	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
505	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
506	% block.
507	
508	\begin{theorem}\leanok
509	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
510	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
512	
513	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
514	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
515	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
516	  % L3990-L3996).
517	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
518	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
519	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
520	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
521	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
522	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
523	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
524	  % \end{ex}"
525	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
526	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
527	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
528	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
529	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
530	  % Exercise~REF."
531	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
532	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
533	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
535	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of
536	  \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an \emph{abelian variety over
537	  \(k\)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a
538	  \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the
539	  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of
540	  \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]}, and
541	  \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
542	  \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a
543	  \texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity
544	  follows automatically from Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4 and is not
545	  separately stated.)
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	\leanok
551	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
552	  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
554	
555	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
556	  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
557	  % PDF p.~92).
558	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
559	  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
560	  % then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
561	  % when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
562	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
563	  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
564	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
565	  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',
566	  \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac.}
567	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
568	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
569	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
570	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
571	  \[
572	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
573	  \]
574	\end{theorem}
575	
576	\begin{theorem}
577	
578	\leanok
579	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
580	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
582	
583	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
584	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
585	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
586	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
587	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
588	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
589	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
590	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
591	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
592	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
593	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
594	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
595	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
596	  if and only if the degree
597	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
598	  vanishes. Equivalently,
599	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
600	\end{theorem}
601	
602	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
603	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
604	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
605	% identity-component substrate.)
606	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
607	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
608	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
609	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
610	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
611	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
612	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
613	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
614	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
615	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
616	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
617	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
618	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
619	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
620	% everywhere."
621	\begin{proof}
622	  We establish
623	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
624	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
625	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
626	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
627	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
628	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
629	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
630	
631	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
632	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
633	  conjuncts from four inputs.
634	
635	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
636	  By
637	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
638	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
639	  type by
640	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
641	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
642	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
643	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
644	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
645	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
646	
647	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
648	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
649	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
650	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
651	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
652	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
653	
654	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
655	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
656	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
657	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
658	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
659	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
660	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
661	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
662	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
663	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
664	
665	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
666	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
667	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
668	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
669	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
670	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
671	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
672	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
673	
674	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
675	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
676	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
677	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
678	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
679	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
680	
681	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
682	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
683	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
684	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
685	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
686	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
687	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
688	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
689	  the open identity component, and
690	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
691	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
692	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
693	
694	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
695	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
696	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
697	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
698	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
699	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
700	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
701	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
702	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
703	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
704	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
705	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
706	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
707	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
708	  zero on \(C\)'').
709	\end{proof}
710	
711	\section{Lean encoding}
712	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
713	
714	The Lean target file is
715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
716	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
717	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
718	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
719	
720	\begin{itemize}
721	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
722	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
723	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
724	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
725	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
726	    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
727	    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
728	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
729	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
730	    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
731	    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
732	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
733	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
734	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
735	    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
736	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
737	    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
738	    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
739	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
740	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
741	    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
742	    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
743	    In Lean these are the instances
744	    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
745	    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
746	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
747	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
748	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
749	    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
750	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
751	    map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) (in Lean,
752	    \texttt{(IdentityComponent G)\_K \(\to\) IdentityComponent (G\_K)})
753	    is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
754	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
755	    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
756	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
757	    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
758	    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
759	    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
760	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
761	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
762	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
763	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
764	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
765	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
766	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
767	    component.
768	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
769	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
770	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
771	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
772	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
773	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
774	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
775	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
776	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
777	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
778	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
779	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
780	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
781	    underlying scheme).
782	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
783	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
784	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
785	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
786	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
787	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
788	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
789	\end{itemize}

807	\section{Out of scope}
808	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
809	
810	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
811	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
812	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
813	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
814	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
815	
816	\begin{itemize}
817	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
818	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
819	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
820	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
821	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
822	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
823	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
824	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
825	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
826	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
827	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
828	    Route~A.
829	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
830	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
831	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
832	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
833	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
834	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
835	\end{itemize}

849	    \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism},
