3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}
117	
118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}

164	  % topological spaces."

167	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
168	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.

174	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
175	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
176	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
177	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the

179	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
180	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
181	  flasque.

278	\leanok
279	[$\Ext^{n+1}$ vanishes from $\Ext^n$ vanishing on the rightmost term, with injective middle term]
280	  \label{lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ext_succ_eq_zero_of_injective_of_lower_zero}
282	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule}
283	  % SOURCE: Project-bespoke substrate helper for the
284	  % Hartshorne~III.2.5 induction (iter-193 Lane H prover-side
285	  % construction). The underlying mathematical statement is the
286	  % classical degree-shifting argument that, for an injective middle
287	  % term in a short exact sequence, the connecting morphism in the
288	  % long exact $\Ext$ sequence is surjective above the injective
289	  % dimension. No external source is cited verbatim.
290	  \textit{Source: project-bespoke iter-193 substrate.}
291	  Let $\mathcal C$ be an abelian category equipped with a
292	  \texttt{HasExt} instance, let $X$ be an object of $\mathcal C$, and
293	  let
294	  \[
295	    0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0
296	  \]
297	  be a short exact sequence in $\mathcal C$ whose middle term $S_2$ is
298	  injective. Let $n_0 \ge 1$ be a natural number and assume that
299	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ (i.e.\ every element of $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3)$
300	  vanishes). Then $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1) = 0$.
301	
302	  The proof is the classical degree-shift. Since $S_2$ is injective and
303	  $n_0 + 1 \ge 2$, the injective-dimension subsingleton instance forces
304	  $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_2)$ to be subsingleton; combined with exactness
305	  of the covariant $\Ext$ long exact sequence at $\Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X,
306	  S_1)$, every $x_1 \in \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$ is in the image of the
307	  connecting morphism $\delta \colon \Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) \to
308	  \Ext^{n_0 + 1}(X, S_1)$. The hypothesis
309	  $\Ext^{n_0}(X, S_3) = 0$ then gives $x_1 = 0$. The lemma is consumed
310	  in the $i \ge 2$ inductive case of \cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}.
311	\end{lemma}

368	\begin{theorem}
369	
370	\leanok
371	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
372	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
374	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_injective,
375	        lem:flasque_cokernel_short_exact,
376	        lem:ext_succ_zero_of_injective_lower_zero}
377	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
378	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
379	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
380	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
381	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
382	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
383	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
384	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
385	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
386	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
387	  \[
388	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
389	  \]
390	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
391	\end{theorem}
392	
393	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
394	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
395	% be the quotient:
396	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
397	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
398	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
399	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
400	% Ex. 1.16b)
401	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
402	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
403	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
404	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
405	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
406	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
407	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
408	% \(i\) we get the result."
409	\begin{proof}
410	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
411	  \leanok
412	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
413	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
414	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
415	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
416	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
417	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
418	  short exact sequence
419	  \[
420	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
421	  \]
422	  in the sheaf category.
423	
424	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
425	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
426	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
427	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
428	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
429	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
430	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
431	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
432	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
433	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
434	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
435	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
436	
437	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
438	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
439	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
440	  exact sequence on global sections
441	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
442	  \[
443	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
444	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
445	  \]
446	
447	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
448	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
449	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
450	  positive degree:
451	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
452	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
453	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
454	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
455	  \[
456	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
457	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
458	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
459	  \]
460	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
461	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
462	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
463	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
464	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
465	
466	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
467	  exact sequence yields
468	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
469	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
470	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
471	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
472	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
473	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
474	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
475	
476	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
477	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
478	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
479	  % already provided by chapter
480	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
481	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
482	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
483	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
484	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
485	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
486	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
487	  % LOC apiece.
488	\end{proof}

491	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}

493	\begin{lemma}
494	
495	\leanok
496	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
497	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}

536	\begin{proof}
537	  \uses{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
538	  \leanok
539	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
540	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
541	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
542	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
543	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
544	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
545	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
546	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
547	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
548	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
549	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
550	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
551	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
552	
553	  In the Lean formalisation this argument is split into two pieces.
554	  The \emph{outer} step is the presheaf-level equality
555	  \[
556	    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A \;=\;
557	      \bigl(i_*\;\bigr)
558	      \bigl(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A\bigr)
559	  \]
560	  obtained from Mathlib's \texttt{skyscraperPresheaf\_eq\_pushforward}
561	  via \texttt{ObjectProperty.FullSubcategory.ext} (axiom-clean, no
562	  Mathlib gap). The \emph{inner} step is the iso
563	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\;A \cong
564	    (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit})\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A\)
565	  on the one-point space \(\PUnit\); this is the actual blocking
566	  technical step and is isolated as
567	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit} below. Composing
568	  the two pieces (with the inner iso unpacked via
569	  \texttt{Classical.choice} from its current \(\mathtt{Nonempty}\)
570	  wrapper) gives the lemma.
571	\end{proof}
572	
573	\begin{lemma}\leanok
574	[Inner iso: skyscraperSheaf vs.\ constantSheaf on $\PUnit$]
575	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit}
577	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
578	  \textit{Project-bespoke sub-lemma} (Lean-substrate technical step
579	  for \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}; no separate external
580	  source — the underlying mathematical content is the one-point case
581	  of Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.17 quoted in
582	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}).
583	
584	  Let \(\PUnit\) denote the one-point topological space, let
585	  \(J_{\PUnit}\) denote the Grothendieck topology of opens on
586	  \(\PUnit\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module. Then there is an
587	  isomorphism of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(\PUnit\)
588	  \[
589	    \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A \;\cong\;
590	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k)\,.\,\mathrm{obj}\;A.
591	  \]
592	\end{lemma}
593	
594	\begin{proof}
595	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
596	  \leanok
597	  The space \(\PUnit\) has exactly two opens, \(\bot\) (empty) and
598	  \(\top\) (the whole space, which contains \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit}\)).
599	  We build the iso pointwise on these two opens and check naturality
600	  on the unique non-identity inclusion \(\bot \le \top\).
601	
602	  \emph{Forward map.} Apply Mathlib's
603	  \(\mathtt{constantSheafAdj}\;J_{\PUnit}\;\Module \bar k\;
604	   h_{T\top}\,.\,\mathtt{homEquiv}\,.\,\mathtt{symm}\) to the natural
605	  identification
606	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit})\;A\,.\,\mathrm{val}\,.
607	    \,\mathrm{obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;\top) = A\),
608	  which holds by \(\mathtt{simp}\;[\mathtt{skyscraperPresheaf}]\) since
609	  \(\PUnit.\mathrm{unit} \in \top\).
610	
611	  \emph{Inverse map.} Build pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\):
612	  \begin{itemize}
613	    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\bot\): both sheaves give the terminal
614	      object in \(\Module \bar k\) (the constant sheaf is the
615	      sheafification of the constant presheaf, whose value at the
616	      empty open is the zero module after sheafification; the
617	      skyscraper sheaf at the empty open is by construction
618	      \(0\)). Use \(\mathtt{IsTerminal.uniqueUpToIso}\) to identify
619	      them;
620	    \item at \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\): use the constantSheaf-unit-iso on
621	      \(\PUnit\), which is true since the \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\)
622	      functor is fully faithful on \(\PUnit\) (this fully-faithful
623	      instance is the Mathlib gap described below in the route
624	      choice).
625	  \end{itemize}
626	
627	  \emph{Naturality} on the unique non-identity inclusion
628	  \(\bot \le \top\): both sides land in the terminal object at
629	  \(\bot\), so commutativity is automatic by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton}\)
630	  of the terminal object.
631	
632	  \paragraph{Mathlib gap (route choice).} The inverse map's
633	  \(\mathrm{op}\;\top\) component needs that the
634	  \(\mathtt{constantSheaf}\) functor on \(\PUnit\) is fully faithful.
635	  Two routes close the gap; both are tracked for the iter-197+
636	  prover:
637	  \begin{itemize}
638	    \item \emph{Route A} (preferred — Mathlib upstreaming
639	      candidate): provide instances
640	      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
641	      \(.\) \texttt{Full} and
642	      \texttt{(constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology PUnit) D)}
643	      \(.\) \texttt{Faithful}. Likely the smallest project-side
644	      change once a Mathlib PR for the analogous fact for the
645	      one-point space is accepted; the inverse map then follows by
646	      \texttt{Functor.preimage} of the forward map.
647	    \item \emph{Route B} (fallback — no Mathlib upstream): directly
648	      construct the iso pointwise on the two opens of \(\PUnit\) as
649	      above, using \(\mathtt{Sheaf.IsTerminal}\) at \(\bot\) and an
650	      explicit value extraction at \(\top\) (the constant sheaf at
651	      the unique non-empty open of an irreducible space is the
652	      identity on \(A\) after sheafification — same fact as
653	      \cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible} for the non-empty
654	      branch, used here only at the one-point space). Estimated
655	      LOC: ~50-80 standalone.
656	  \end{itemize}
657	\end{proof}

659	\begin{lemma}
660	
661	\leanok
662	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
663	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
664	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
665	
666	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
667	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
668	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
669	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
670	
671	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
672	  no external source pinned).
673	\end{lemma}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	
677	
678	\leanok
679	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
680	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
681	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
682	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
683	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
684	\end{proof}
685	
686	\begin{lemma}
687	
688	\leanok
689	[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
690	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
691	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
692	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
693	
694	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
695	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
696	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
697	  \[
698	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
699	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
700	  \]
701	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
702	
703	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
704	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
705	  above).
706	\end{lemma}
707	
708	\begin{proof}
709	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
710	  \leanok
711	  % NOTE (iter-197 lean-vs-blueprint-checker J-1): The blueprint
712	  % proof block originally listed a four-lemma chain
713	  % (`skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward`, `closedPoint_closure_irreducible`,
714	  % `isFlasque_pushforward`, `isFlasque_constant_irreducible`) in
715	  % `\uses{...}`. The Lean closure at
716	  % `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean:903-939` takes a
717	  % direct route via `skyscraperPresheaf_map` on a `P.point ∈ V` case
718	  % split (the `P.point ∈ V` branch closes via `eqToHom` being an iso;
719	  % the `P.point ∉ V` branch closes via the codomain being terminal /
720	  % `Subsingleton`). None of the four originally-listed lemmas is used
721	  % in the Lean proof, so the `\uses{...}` set has been pruned to
722	  % `def:isFlasque_sheaf` only. The mathematical informal sketch below
723	  % still presents the conceptually clean four-lemma argument because
724	  % it is shorter and more illuminating; readers wishing to track the
725	  % actual Lean proof should consult the file directly.
726	  By
727	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
728	  isomorphic to the pushforward
729	  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
730	  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion
731	  \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow C\). The base
732	  \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
733	  (\cref{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}), so the constant sheaf on
734	  it is flasque (\cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}). Pushforward
735	  of a flasque sheaf is flasque
736	  (\cref{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}). Flasqueness transports along the

741	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
742	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
743	
744	\begin{theorem}
745	
746	\leanok
747	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
748	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
750	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
751	
752	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
753	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
754	  \[
755	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
756	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
757	  \]
758	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
759	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
760	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
761	
762	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
763	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
764	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
765	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
766	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
767	  above.
768	\end{theorem}
769	
770	\begin{proof}
771	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
772	  \leanok
773	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
774	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
775	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
776	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
777	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
778	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
779	  \[
780	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
781	  \]
782	  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
783	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
784	\end{proof}

869	\section{Out of scope}
870	
871	The following items lie outside this chapter and are scheduled into
872	sibling sub-builds:
873	\begin{itemize}
874	  \item \emph{General coherent-sheaf cohomology vanishing.} Higher
875	    cohomology vanishing for general coherent sheaves on a curve
876	    requires Grothendieck vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme
877	    (Hartshorne~III.2.7), which the chapter does \emph{not} use; the
878	    chapter is narrowly tailored to the flasque/skyscraper case
879	    \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) that the genus-\(0\) critical path needs.
880	  \item \emph{Serre duality for curves.} The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch
881	    statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) routes through
882	    Serre duality \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
883	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*\); Mathlib has neither the dualizing sheaf
884	    \(\omega_C\) on a scheme nor the duality theorem. The chapter
885	    sidesteps duality entirely by using flasqueness directly (the
886	    Hartshorne~III~\S2 order of presentation, not the
887	    Hartshorne~III~\S7 one).
888	  \item \emph{The \(H^0\) half of \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).} The
889	    one-dimensionality identification \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) is the
890	    sibling substrate lemma
891	    REF of
892	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, closed axiom-clean iter-188
893	    against the project's four-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain.
894	    The present chapter is the complementary H\(^1\) half.
895	\end{itemize}

300	  %   theorem cotangentSpaceAtIdentity_finrank_eq (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
301	  %       [CategoryTheory.GrpObj G] {n : ℕ} [SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]
302	  %       [IsProper G.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible G.hom] :
303	  %       Module.finrank k (cotangentSpaceAtIdentity G) = n
304	  
305	  In the setting of \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}, with \([\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,n\,G.\mathtt{hom}]\) supplying the relative dimension parameter,
306	  \[
307	    \finrank_{k}\,\mathfrak g^{\vee} \;=\; n,
308	  \]
309	  and consequently (since rank is preserved under \(k\)-linear duality on finitely-generated free modules) \(\finrank_{k}\,\mathfrak g = n\) as well.
310	
311	  \emph{Lean encoding note (iter-128 per \texttt{strategy-critic-iter128}; iter-129 rename).} The right-hand side is the relative-dimension parameter \(n\) from the \texttt{[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n G.hom]} instance, NOT a bare \(\dim G\) (which has no canonical Lean function on \texttt{Over (Spec k)}). The mathematical content is unchanged because for a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, the relative dimension equals the dimension of the cotangent space at the identity equals what informal prose calls \(\dim G\). The Lean target name is \texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_finrank\_eq} (iter-129 rename, replacing the iter-128 placeholder \texttt{lieAlgebra\_finrank\_eq\_dim}, aligned with the renamed (i.a) declaration \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}).
312	\end{lemma}
313	
314	\begin{proof}
315	  
316	  \leanok
317	  We close the rank computation under Replacement (B) (the iter-131 chart-base-change body of \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}) using the existential output of \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega} directly. The proof is in four steps: Steps 1+2 form the live closure path; Step 3 documents an alternative canonical route through \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^{2}\) that is currently deferred under Replacement (B); Step 4 carries the conclusion across \(k\)-linear duality to the tangent side.
318	
319	  \emph{Step 1: chart-side Kähler rank.} Let \(V \subseteq G.\mathrm{left}\) and \(U \subseteq \Spec k\) be the affine charts (around the image \(x_0 = \eta_G(\mathrm{pt}) \in G.\mathrm{left}\)) extracted by \cref{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega} (project lemma \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth\_locally\_free\_omega} in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean}); the same charts are pinned by the body of \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} via the iter-131 \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain and re-extractable through the companion lemma \texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_eq\_extendScalars} (see \S "Iter-131 \texttt{Classical.choose}-chain body shape" below for the recommended downstream rewrite pattern). The existential's payload, on the \(\Gamma(\Spec k, U)\)-algebra \(\Gamma(G, V)\) (with the algebra structure given by the appLE ring map of \(G.\mathrm{hom}\)), supplies in one shot both
320	  \[
321	    \texttt{hfree} \colon \;\texttt{Module.Free}\;\Gamma(G, V)\;\Omega_{\Gamma(G, V)\,/\,\Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)}
322	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
323	    \texttt{hrank} \colon \;\texttt{Module.rank}\;\Gamma(G, V)\;\Omega_{\Gamma(G, V)\,/\,\Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)} \;=\; n,
324	  \]
325	  internally consuming the Mathlib lemmas \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}). Converting the rank-as-cardinal equation \texttt{hrank} to a \(\finrank\) equation via \texttt{Module.finrank\_eq\_of\_rank\_eq} (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.Finrank}; for a commutative semiring \(R\) with \texttt{StrongRankCondition}, \(\texttt{Module.rank}\,R\,M = \uparrow n \Rightarrow \texttt{Module.finrank}\,R\,M = n\)) gives
326	  \[
327	    \finrank_{\Gamma(G, V)}\,\Omega_{\Gamma(G, V)\,/\,\Gamma(\Spec k,\,U)} \;=\; n.
328	  \]
329	
330	  \emph{Step 2: base-change to \(k\) preserves \(\finrank\).} By construction, the body of \cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace} is the base change \(k \otimes_{\Gamma(G, V)} \Omega_{\Gamma(G, V)/\Gamma(\Spec k, U)}\) along the ring map \(\psi_V \colon \Gamma(G, V) \to k\) obtained from the identity section restricted to \(V\) (composed with \(\Gamma(\Spec k, \cdot) \cong k\)). The Mathlib base-change lemma
331	  \[

359	  \label{lem:GrpObj_shearMulRight}

361	  % Lean signature stub (iter-134 substantive closure; the binary-product
362	  % upgrade of `CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mulRight`
363	  % (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Grp_.lean:277--281`), which packages
364	  % only the one-input version `(· · f)` for a fixed global element
365	  % `f : 𝟙_ ⟶ G`. Here both factors of `G ⊗ G` are free, parametrising
366	  % the family of right-translations {mulRight a}_{a ∈ G} globally across G.
367	  % Stated for any `GrpObj G` in any Cartesian monoidal category C, so the
368	  % declaration is general-purpose and instantiates at `Over (Spec (.of k))`
369	  % for the rigidity application):
370	  %   def shearMulRight {C : Type*} [Category C] [CartesianMonoidalCategory C]
371	  %       (G : C) [GrpObj G] : G ⊗ G ≅ G ⊗ G where
372	  %     hom := lift (fst G G) μ
373	  %     inv := lift (fst G G) (lift (fst G G ≫ ι) (snd G G) ≫ μ)
374	  % together with the `@[simps]`-spawned companion lemmas
375	  %   shearMulRight_hom_fst : (shearMulRight G).hom ≫ fst G G = fst G G
376	  %   shearMulRight_hom_snd : (shearMulRight G).hom ≫ snd G G = μ
377	  
378	  Let \(G\) be a group object in a Cartesian monoidal category. The \emph{binary-product shear isomorphism} is the self-iso
379	  \[
380	    \sigma \colon G \otimes G \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; G \otimes G
381	  \]
382	  whose forward and inverse maps are built categorically from the multiplication \(\mu\), the inverse \(\iota\), and the binary-product projections of \(G \otimes G\):
383	  \[
384	    \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \;=\; \texttt{lift\,(fst\,G\,G)\,μ}, \qquad
385	    \sigma_{\mathrm{inv}} \;=\; \texttt{lift\,(fst\,G\,G)\,(lift\,(fst\,G\,G\,≫\,\(\iota\))\,(snd\,G\,G)\,≫\,μ)},
386	  \]
387	  informally \(\sigma\colon (a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)\) with inverse \((a, b) \mapsto (a, a^{-1} \cdot b)\). The forward map is an isomorphism over the first projection and acts as right-translation on the second coordinate; concretely,
388	  \[
389	    \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{1} \;=\; \pr_{1}, \qquad
390	    \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{2} \;=\; \mu.
391	  \]
392	\end{lemma}
393	
394	\begin{proof}
395	  
396	  \leanok
397	  The compositions \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \sigma_{\mathrm{inv}} = \mathrm{id}_{G \otimes G} = \sigma_{\mathrm{inv}} \circ \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}}\) are verified componentwise via the binary-product universal property (\texttt{CartesianMonoidalCategory.hom\_ext}). The first-projection components are trivial (both sides collapse to \(\pr_{1}\) by \texttt{lift\_fst}). The second-projection components reduce to identities of the form
398	  \[
399	    \texttt{lift\,(fst\,G\,G\,≫\,\(\iota\))\,\(\mu\)\,≫\,\(\mu\)} \;=\; \pr_{2}
400	    \quad\text{and}\quad
401	    \texttt{lift\,(fst\,G\,G)\,(lift\,(fst\,G\,G\,≫\,\(\iota\))\,(snd\,G\,G)\,≫\,\(\mu\))\,≫\,\(\mu\)} \;=\; \pr_{2},
402	  \]
403	  closed using the Mathlib calculus \texttt{MonObj.lift\_lift\_assoc} (associativity-via-lift reshuffling), \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.lift\_comp\_inv\_left} / \texttt{lift\_comp\_inv\_right} (left/right-inverse axioms for \(\GrpObj\)), and \texttt{MonObj.lift\_comp\_one\_left} (left-unit axiom). The companion identifications \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{1} = \pr_{1}\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{2} = \mu\) follow directly from \texttt{lift\_fst} and \texttt{lift\_snd} on \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \texttt{lift\,(fst\,G\,G)\,μ}\) and are auto-generated by the \texttt{@[simps]} attribute on \texttt{shearMulRight} into the lemmas \texttt{shearMulRight\_hom\_fst} and \texttt{shearMulRight\_hom\_snd}. The proof shape parallels \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mulRight} (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Grp\_.lean:277--281}) and \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.isPullback} (\texttt{Grp\_.lean:293--323}), but with both factors of \(G \otimes G\) free rather than the one-input version's fixed global element.
404	\end{proof}

493	  \emph{Step 1: Build the shear iso \(\sigma\).} By \cref{lem:GrpObj_shearMulRight}, the binary-product shear \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \rangle \colon G \times_{k} G \to G \times_{k} G\) in \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\) — informally \((a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)\) — is a self-iso of \(G \times_{k} G\) with explicit inverse \(\sigma_{\mathrm{inv}} = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \circ (\iota \circ \pr_{1}, \pr_{2}) \rangle\), and satisfies the companion identifications \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{1} = \pr_{1}\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{2} = \mu\). The Lean target is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.shearMulRight} (closed iter-134, \(\sim 30\) LOC of \texttt{lift\_lift\_assoc} / \texttt{lift\_comp\_inv\_*} / \texttt{lift\_comp\_one\_left} calculus on the \(\GrpObj\) axioms, modelled on \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mulRight} and \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.isPullback} of \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Grp\_.lean:277--281,\,293--323}).
494	
495	  \emph{Step 2: Base-change of differentials.} View \(G \times_{k} G\) as a \(G\)-scheme via the first projection \(\pr_{1}\). The relative cotangent of this \(G\)-scheme, \(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G}\), is canonically isomorphic to the pullback \(\pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\) via \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj} (the load-bearing helper sub-lemma, NEEDS\_MATHLIB\_GAP\_FILL, \(\sim 150\)--\(300\) LOC):
496	  \[
497	    \Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G} \;\cong\; \pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k} \qquad \text{on } G \times_{k} G.
498	  \]
499	  Since \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}}\) is an iso over \(\pr_{1}\) (i.e.\ \(\pr_{1} \circ \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \pr_{1}\), by construction of \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \rangle\) via the binary-product universal property), \(\sigma\) acts as a self-iso of \(G \times_{k} G\) as a \(G\)-scheme via \(\pr_{1}\); functoriality of \(\Omega_{(\cdot)/G}\) under such self-isos then re-routes the Step 2 iso through \(\sigma\) to relate the differentials at the two projections, with \(\pr_{2} \circ \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \mu\).
500	
501	  \emph{Step 3: Restrict along the section \(s := \langle \mathrm{id}_{G},\, \eta_{G} \rangle \colon G \to G \times_{k} G\).} The section \(s\) satisfies \(\pr_{1} \circ s = \mathrm{id}_{G}\) and \(\pr_{2} \circ s = \eta_{G} \circ \pi_{G}\) in \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\), where \(\pi_{G} \colon G \to \Spec k\) is the structure map. Applying \(s^{*}\) to both sides of the Step 2 iso \(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G} \cong \pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\):
502	  \begin{itemize}
503	    \item The LHS \(s^{*}(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G})\) is, by the \(G\)-scheme structure given by \(\pr_{1}\) and the section identity \(\pr_{1} \circ s = \mathrm{id}_{G}\), canonically isomorphic to \(\Omega_{G/k}\) (restricting the relative cotangent of \(G \times_{k} G\) over \(G\) to a section of the \(G\)-projection recovers \(\Omega_{G/k}\) — a standard relative-cotangent identification for a section of a smooth morphism). Mathlib-wise this combines \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp} with \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackId} on the composition \(\pr_{1} \circ s = \mathrm{id}_{G}\).
504	    \item The RHS \(s^{*}(\pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k})\) is, by \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_restrict_to_identity_section} applied to the section identity \(\pr_{2} \circ s = \eta_{G} \circ \pi_{G}\), canonically isomorphic to \(\pi_{G}^{*}(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k})\).
505	  \end{itemize}
506	  Composing the LHS and RHS identifications with the pulled-back Step 2 iso yields the displayed iso \(\Omega_{G/k} \cong \pi_{G}^{*}(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k})\). 3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects/Cross01Substrate.lean

5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-189 plan-phase). This chapter is a project-bespoke
6	% substrate file landing two reusable lemmas needed to close the Lane B
7	% \(\mathbb{G}_m\)-scaling cocycle (path (III.c) of
8	% chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity) together with two off-target sorries
9	% (\texttt{gm\_geomIrred}, \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}) in
10	% \texttt{Genus0BaseObjects/GmScaling.lean}. Both substrates are
11	% generic in the geometric situation (the closed-immersion lift lemma is
12	% scheme-level; the tensor-domain lemma is ring-level keyed on the
13	% \texttt{projectiveLineBar} grading) and live in their own file rather
14	% than inline so they can be re-used by the three GmScaling
15	% applications and, if upstreamed, by Mathlib itself.
16	%
17	% The recipe consolidated below was produced by the
18	% \texttt{mathlib-analogist} subagent in iter-189 and is preserved

20	% ``Concrete project-side recipe''). The chapter itself is the
21	% prover-facing specification.

23	\section{Setup and motivation}
24	\label{sec:cross01_substrate_setup}
25	
26	The Lane B cocycle \texttt{gmScalingP1\_chart\_agreement\_cross01} reduces, after
27	the iter-188 (III.c) separated-locus structural setup, to a single substantive
28	factorisation: a pair morphism
29	\(s_{\text{pair}} : T \to T_0 \times_X T_1\) into a fibre product over the
30	projective line bar \(\mathrm{PLB}_{\overline{k}}\) must factor through the
31	diagonal closed immersion
32	\(\Delta : T_0 \times_{X} T_1 \hookrightarrow T_0 \times_{\overline{k}} T_1\)
33	of the separated-over-\(\overline{k}\) target. Concretely the missing primitive is
34	the equivalence
35	\[
36	  \exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g \iff \mathrm{range}(g) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i)
37	\]
38	for a closed immersion \(i\) with reduced source/target hypothesis, which Mathlib
39	ships only in the kernel-inequality form (\(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift}\),
40	keyed on \(f.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)). Substrate~1 fills this gap.
41	
42	The same Lane B cocycle, together with the two off-target instances
43	\texttt{gm\_geomIrred} and \texttt{projGm\_isReduced}, also requires
44	\[
45	  \mathrm{IsDomain}\bigl(\mathrm{Away}_{\overline{k}}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\,\overline{k}\bigr)
46	\]
47	for a homogeneous generator \(f\) of positive degree in the
48	\(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading. Substrate~2 supplies this via a
49	canonical iso chain
50	\[
51	  \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \overline{k}[t]
52	  \;\cong\; \mathrm{MvPolynomial}_{\mathrm{Unit}}(\mathrm{Away}\,f)
53	\]
54	followed by localisation at \(X()\), reducing the claim to the
55	already-formalised pattern from \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}.
56	
57	\section{Closed-immersion lift by range inclusion}
58	\label{sec:lift_iff_range_subset}
59	
60	\begin{theorem}\leanok
61	[lift through closed immersion via range containment]
62	  \label{thm:IsClosedImmersion_lift_iff_range_subset}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset}
64	  Let \(X,Y,Z\) be schemes (Type \(u\)), let \(i : Z \to X\) be a closed
65	  immersion with \(Z\) reduced, and let \(g : Y \to X\) be a
66	  quasi-compact morphism with \(Y\) reduced. Then there exists a
67	  morphism \(h : Y \to Z\) with \(h \circ i = g\) if and only if the
68	  topological range of \(g\) is contained in the topological range of
69	  \(i\):
70	  \[
71	    (\exists\, h : Y \to Z,\ h \circ i = g)
72	    \;\iff\;
73	    \mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq \mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}).
74	  \]
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{proof}
77	
78	
79	\leanok
80	  \emph{Forward direction} is range pushdown: if \(g = h \circ i\)
81	  then every point in \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}})\) is the image
82	  under \(i_{\text{base}}\) of \(h_{\text{base}}(y)\) for the
83	  corresponding preimage \(y\).
84	
85	  \emph{Backward direction} threads the Galois connection between
86	  vanishing-ideal and support of an ideal sheaf. The proof has six
87	  steps; the Mathlib primitives cited are present at the project's
88	  pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}).
89	
90	  \begin{enumerate}
91	  \item \emph{Range of a closed immersion is its kernel support.}
92	    Because \(i\) is a closed immersion, \(i\) factors (up to
93	    isomorphism) as the canonical subscheme inclusion of its kernel
94	    ideal sheaf, so
95	    \(\mathrm{range}(i_{\text{base}}) = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
96	    (Mathlib:
97	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.range\_subschemeι} +
98	    \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toImage}.)
99	  \item \emph{Range of \(g\) lies in \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).}
100	    The unconditional inclusion
101	    \(\mathrm{range}(g_{\text{base}}) \subseteq g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
102	    is shipped (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.range\_subset\_ker\_support}); under
103	    \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\), \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\)
104	    equals \(\overline{\mathrm{range}\,g_{\text{base}}}\)
105	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.support\_ker}). Since
106	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\) is closed and contains
107	    \(\mathrm{range}(g)\) by hypothesis, the closure also lies in
108	    \(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\):
109	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support} \subseteq i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support}\).
110	    (Mathlib: \texttt{closure\_minimal}.)
111	  \item \emph{\(Y\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(g.\mathrm{ker}\) is radical.}
112	    Under \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\;g]\),
113	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{ideal}\,U = \mathrm{ker}(g.\mathrm{app}\,U)\)
114	    (\texttt{Scheme.Hom.ker\_apply}). Reducedness of \(Y\) makes this
115	    ring kernel a radical ideal pointwise on each affine open, so
116	    \(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical} = g.\mathrm{ker}\).
117	  \item \emph{\(Z\) reduced \(\Rightarrow\) \(i.\mathrm{ker}\) is
118	    radical.} Identical argument with \(i\) in place of \(g\)
119	    (closed immersions are quasi-compact, so the
120	    \texttt{ker\_apply} hypothesis is satisfied automatically).
121	  \item \emph{Galois-connection chain.} Combining Steps~2--4 with
122	    \texttt{IdealSheafData.vanishingIdeal\_support} (which equates
123	    the vanishing ideal of a support with the radical) gives
124	    \[
125	      i.\mathrm{ker}
126	        = i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
127	        = \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(i.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
128	        \le \mathrm{vanishingIdeal}(g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{support})
129	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.\mathrm{radical}
130	        = g.\mathrm{ker}.
131	    \]
132	    The middle inequality uses the antitone half of the Galois
133	    connection (\texttt{IdealSheafData.gc}).
134	  \item \emph{Apply Mathlib's kernel-inequality lift.} The proof
135	    concludes by feeding \(\mathrm{hker} : i.\mathrm{ker} \le g.\mathrm{ker}\)
136	    to \texttt{IsClosedImmersion.lift} and reading off its
137	    factorisation lemma \texttt{lift\_fac}.
138	  \end{enumerate}
139	\end{proof}
140	
141	\section{Tensor reducedness over the algebraic closure}
142	\label{sec:tensor_AwayGm_isDomain}
143	
144	\begin{theorem}\leanok
145	[domain structure on \(\mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\)]
146	  \label{thm:gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmRing_tensor_homogeneousAway_isDomain}
148	  Let \(\overline{k}\) be a field, let \(d \ge 1\), and let \(f\) be a
149	  non-zero homogeneous polynomial of degree \(d\) in the
150	  \(\mathrm{projectiveLineBar}\) grading on
151	  \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\). Then the
152	  tensor product
153	  \[
154	    \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}\bigl(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading}\,\overline{k}\bigr)\,f
155	    \;\otimes_{\overline{k}}\;
156	    \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
157	  \]
158	  is a domain (and hence reduced).
159	\end{theorem}
160	\begin{proof}
161	
162	\leanok
163	  The proof exhibits a canonical isomorphism of \(\overline{k}\)-algebras
164	  identifying the tensor product with the localisation of a
165	  polynomial ring over a domain at a non-zero-divisor; both operations
166	  preserve the domain property.
167	
168	  \begin{enumerate}
169	  \item \emph{Domain instance on the homogeneous localisation.}
170	    \(\mathrm{Away}\,f := \mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away}(\mathrm{projectiveLineBarGrading})\,f\)
171	    embeds via the \texttt{val\_injective} bridge into
172	    \(\mathrm{Localization.Away}\,f\); since the polynomial ring
173	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}(\mathrm{Fin}\,2)\;\overline{k}\) is a domain
174	    and \(f \ne 0\), the localisation is a domain, and the bridge
175	    transports the property to \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\). (Already used in
176	    \texttt{projectiveLineBar\_isReduced}; see
177	    \texttt{GmScaling.lean} L734--738 for the precise instance
178	    threading.)
179	  \item \emph{Collapse the tensor with the polynomial factor.}
180	    Recalling
181	    \(\mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
182	       = \mathrm{Localization.Away}(X()\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k})\),
183	    apply \texttt{MvPolynomial.algebraTensorAlgEquiv} to get
184	    \[
185	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k}
186	      \;\cong\;
187	      \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f),
188	    \]
189	    a \(\overline{k}\)-algebra (in fact \(\mathrm{Away}\,f\)-algebra)
190	    isomorphism.
191	  \item \emph{Push the localisation through the tensor.}
192	    Tensoring with the localisation step
193	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;\overline{k} \to \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}\)
194	    factors through Mathlib's tensor-base-change lemma
195	    \texttt{IsLocalization.Away.tensorRightEquiv}, yielding an
196	    isomorphism
197	    \[
198	      \mathrm{Away}\,f \otimes_{\overline{k}} \mathrm{GmRing}\;\overline{k}
199	      \;\cong\;
200	      \mathrm{Localization.Away}(\iota(X())\,:\,\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f))
201	    \]
202	    where \(\iota\) is the canonical algebra map. The threading uses
203	    \texttt{IsScalarTower} compatibility and the
204	    \texttt{Algebra}/\(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\) instance on the
205	    tensored polynomial ring.
206	  \item \emph{Right-hand side is a domain.}
207	    \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\;\mathrm{Unit}\;(\mathrm{Away}\,f)\) is a
208	    polynomial ring over a domain (Step~1), hence a domain by the
209	    standard instance. The generator \(X()\) is non-zero
210	    (\texttt{MvPolynomial.X\_ne\_zero}), so its powers form a
211	    submonoid of the non-zero-divisors
212	    (\texttt{powers\_le\_nonZeroDivisors\_of\_noZeroDivisors}); the
213	    localisation is then a domain by
214	    \texttt{IsLocalization.isDomain\_localization}.
215	  \end{enumerate}
216	
217	  Transferring the domain property along the iso of Step~3 closes the
218	  goal. Reducedness is a direct corollary of being a domain.
219	\end{proof}
