1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
16	%
17	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}
85	
86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\begin{theorem}
98	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
99	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
100	  
101	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
102	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
105	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
106	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
107	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
109	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
110	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
111	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
112	\end{theorem}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
115	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
116	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
117	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
118	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
119	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
120	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
121	% there exists a finite filtration
122	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
123	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
124	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
125	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
126	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
127	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
128	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
129	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
130	\begin{proof}
131	  
132	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
133	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
134	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
135	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
136	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
137	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
138	  finite \(B\)-module
139	  \[
140	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
141	  \]
142	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
143	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
144	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
145	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
146	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
147	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
148	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
149	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
150	  \[
151	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
152	  \]
153	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
154	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
155	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{theorem}
159	
160	\leanok
161	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
162	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
164	  
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
166	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
169	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
170	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
171	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
172	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
174	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
175	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
176	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
177	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
178	\end{theorem}
179	
180	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
181	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
182	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
183	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
184	% Theorem~REF by restricting to a non-empty
185	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
186	\begin{proof}
187	  
188	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
189	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
190	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
191	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). Theorem~REF
192	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
193	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
194	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
195	\end{proof}
196	
197	\section{Existence of the flattening stratification}
198	\label{sec:flatstrat_exists}
199	
200	\begin{theorem}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Existence of the flattening stratification]
204	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
206	  
207	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification''
208	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1811-L1841).
209	  % Parallel pointer: [Stacks Project], tag 052H (verbatim text not yet
210	  % retrieved into a local references/ file; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'').
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
212	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and let
213	  %  \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on the projective space \(\PP^n_S\)
214	  %  over \(S\). Then the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials of
215	  %  restrictions of \(\F\) to fibers of \(\PP^n_S\to S\)
216	  %  is a finite set. Moreover, for each \(f\in I\) there exist
217	  %  a locally closed subscheme \(S_f\) of \(S\),
218	  %  such that the following conditions are satisfied.
219	  %
220	  %  \textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set \(|S_f|\) of \(S_f\) consists of
221	  %  all points \(s\in S\) where the Hilbert polynomial of the
222	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_s\) is \(f\). In particular,
223	  %  the subsets \(|S_f|\subset |S|\) are disjoint,
224	  %  and their set-theoretic union is \(|S|\).
225	  %
226	  %  \textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
227	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
228	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
229	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
230	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
231	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
232	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
233	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
234	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\).
235	  %
236	  %  \textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: }
237	  %  Let the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials be given a total
238	  %  ordering, defined by putting \(f<g\) whenever \(f(n) < g(n)\) for all
239	  %  \(n \gg  0\). Then the closure in \(S\) of the subset \(|S_f|\)
240	  %  is contained in the union of all \(|S_g|\) where \(f\le g\)."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'';
242	  cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 052H.}
243	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on the relative
244	  projective space \(\PP^n_S\) over \(S\).  Then:
245	  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
246	    \item The set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
247	      \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) that arise as \(s\) ranges over \(S\) is
248	      finite.
249	    \item For each \(f \in I\) there exists a locally-closed subscheme
250	      \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set \(|S_f|\) consists of exactly
251	      those \(s \in S\) with \(P_s = f\).  The collection \(\{|S_f|\}_{f \in I}\) is
252	      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union \(|S|\).  Forming the coproduct
253	      \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled
254	      from the inclusions \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\), the pullback \(i^* \F\) on
255	      \(\PP^n_{S'}\) is flat over \(S'\), and \(i : S' \to S\) is universal with this
256	      property: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) such that \(\varphi^* \F\) on
257	      \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\), \(\varphi\) factors uniquely through \(i\).
258	      Each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
259	    \item Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\). Then the
260	      closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
261	  \end{enumerate}
262	\end{theorem}
263	
264	The proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.  The first sub-lemma is
265	the special case \(n = 0\) (when \(\PP^n_S = S\) and \(\F\) is just a coherent sheaf
266	on \(S\)), which already contains the flat-locus-as-locally-closed-subscheme
267	mechanism. The remaining inputs are (A) the Noetherian-induction reduction to
268	the flat case via generic flatness; (B) uniform vanishing of higher cohomology
269	of \(\F(m)\) for \(m \gg 0\) on \(\F_{V_i}\) for the flat pieces; and (C) base-change
270	isomorphisms for the direct images \(\pi_* \F(m)\), which allow comparing
271	strata for different twists.
272	
273	\begin{lemma}
274	[Flat locus on \(S\) for a coherent sheaf is a locally-closed stratification]
275	  \label{lem:flat_locus_open}
276	  
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Special case
278	  % \(n=0\) (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
279	  % L1849-L1885).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "\textbf{Special case: } Let \(n=0\), so that \(\PP^n_S=S\).
282	  %  For any \(s\in S\), the \textbf{fiber} \(\F|_s\) of \(\F\) over \(s\)
283	  %  will mean the pull-back of \(\F\) to the subscheme
284	  %  \(\spec \kappa(s)\) [...]. The Hilbert polynomial of the
285	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to the fiber over \(s\) is the degree \(0\)
286	  %  polynomial \(e \in \Q[\lambda]\), where \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s\).
287	  %
288	  %  By Nakayama lemma, any basis of \(\F|_s\) prolongs to a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\)
289	  %  to give a set of generators for \(F|_U\). Repeating this argument,
290	  %  we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) in which
291	  %  there is a right-exact sequence
292	  %  ${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}
293	  %  \stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$.
294	  %  Let \(I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V\) be the ideal sheaf
295	  %  formed by the entries of the \(e\times m\) matrix
296	  %  \((\psi_{i,j})\) [...].
297	  %  Let \(V_e\) be the closed subscheme of \(V\) defined by \(I_e\).
298	  %  For any morphism of schemes
299	  %  \(f: T\to V\), the pull-back sequence [...]
300	  %  is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products.
301	  %  Hence the pull-back \(f^*\F\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module of
302	  %  rank \(e\) if and only if \(f^*\psi =0\), that is,
303	  %  \(f\) factors via the subscheme \(V_e\hra V\) defined by the vanishing of all
304	  %  entries \(\psi_{i,j}\).
305	  %  Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.
306	  %
307	  %  As the rank of the matrix \((\psi_{i,j})\) is lower semi-continuous,
308	  %  it follows that the function \(e\) is upper semi-continuous, which
309	  %  proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
310	  %  when \(n=0\)."
311	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (special case \(n=0\) of the main theorem).}
312	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(\F\) be a coherent \(\OO_S\)-module.
313	  For each integer \(e \ge 0\), there exists a locally-closed subscheme
314	  \(S_e \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set is
315	  \(\{s \in S : \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s = e\}\). The family \(\{S_e\}_e\) is a
316	  finite, mutually disjoint, set-theoretically covering stratification of \(S\),
317	  and a morphism \(f : T \to S\) pulls back \(\F\) to a locally-free
318	  \(\OO_T\)-module of rank \(e\) if and only if \(f\) factors through \(S_e\).  The
319	  function \(s \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\) is upper-semicontinuous on \(S\).
320	\end{lemma}
321	
322	\begin{proof}
323	  
324	  By [Nitsure], \S4 (verbatim above): for \(s \in S\), Nakayama produces a
325	  neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) and a right-exact local presentation
326	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\psi} \OO_V^{\oplus e} \to \F|_V \to 0\) where
327	  \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\).  The closed subscheme \(V_e \hookrightarrow V\)
328	  defined by the ideal sheaf generated by the matrix entries of \(\psi\)
329	  represents the locus where \(\F\) becomes locally free of rank \(e\): by
330	  right-exactness of tensor product, a base change \(f : T \to V\) has
331	  \(f^* \F\) locally free of rank \(e\) iff \(f^* \psi = 0\), iff \(f\) factors
332	  through \(V_e\).  Glueing the \(V_e\) over the cover of \(S\) by such
333	  neighbourhoods (and intersecting with \(V \setminus \bigcup_{e' > e} V_{e'}\)
334	  to get the strictly-equal-to-\(e\) stratum) produces the locally-closed
335	  \(S_e\). Upper-semicontinuity of \(e\) follows because the rank of \(\psi\) is
336	  lower-semicontinuous.
337	\end{proof}
338	
339	\begin{lemma}
340	[Noetherian induction reduction to the flat case]
341	  \label{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', General case
344	  % opening paragraphs (read from
345	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1888-L1929).
346	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
347	  % "\textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer \(n\) to be arbitrary.
348	  %  The idea of the proof is as follows:
349	  %  We show the existence of a stratification
350	  %  of \(S\) which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
351	  %  stratifications for direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N\) for some
352	  %  integer \(N\) [...].
353	  %
354	  %  As \(S\) is noetherian, it is a finite union of irreducible components,
355	  %  and these are closed in \(S\).
356	  %  Let \(Y\) be an irreducible component of \(S\), and let \(U\) be the
357	  %  non-empty open subset of \(Y\) which consists of all points which do not
358	  %  lie on any other irreducible component of \(S\).
359	  %  Let \(U\) be given the reduced subscheme
360	  %  structure. Note that this makes \(U\) an integral scheme, which is a
361	  %  locally closed subscheme of \(S\).
362	  %  By Theorem REF on generic flatness,
363	  %  \(U\) has a non-empty open subscheme \(V\) such that
364	  %  the restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_V\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_V\).
365	  %  Now repeating the argument with \(S\) replaced by its reduced
366	  %  closed subscheme \(S-V\),
367	  %  it follows by noetherian induction on \(S\) that there exist
368	  %  finitely many reduced, locally closed, mutually disjoint subschemes
369	  %  \(V_i\) of \(S\) such that set-theoretically
370	  %  \(|S|\) is the union of the \(|V_i|\) and
371	  %  the restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_{V_i}\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_{V_i}\).
372	  %  As each \(V_i\) is a noetherian scheme, and as the Hilbert polynomials
373	  %  are locally constant for a flat family of sheaves, it follows
374	  %  that only finitely many polynomials occur in \(V_i\)
375	  %  in the family of Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\)
376	  %  as \(s\) varies over points of \(V_i\). This allows us to conclude
377	  %  the following:
378	  %
379	  %  \textbf{(A) } Only finitely many distinct Hilbert polynomials
380	  %  \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\) occur, as \(s\)
381	  %  varies over all of \(S\)."
382	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (general case, reduction by Noetherian induction).}
383	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) the relative projective
384	  space, and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on \(\PP^n_S\).  Then there exist finitely many
385	  reduced, locally-closed, pairwise disjoint subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\)
386	  whose set-theoretic union is \(|S|\) and such that \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is
387	  flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) for each \(i\). Consequently, only finitely many
388	  Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) occur as \(s\) varies
389	  over \(S\).
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  
394	  By [Nitsure], \S4: \(S\) being noetherian, decompose \(|S| = \bigcup Y_j\) into
395	  finitely many irreducible components, each closed.  For an irreducible
396	  component \(Y\), let \(U \subseteq Y\) be the open subset not lying on any other
397	  component, given the reduced subscheme structure (an integral, locally-closed
398	  subscheme of \(S\)).  By Theorem~REF applied to
399	  \(\F|_{\PP^n_U}\), there is a non-empty open \(V \subseteq U\) over which \(\F\) is
400	  flat.  Replace \(S\) by the reduced closed complement \(S \setminus V\) and
401	  repeat. Noetherianity of \(S\) terminates the induction in finitely many
402	  steps, producing the \(V_i\).  On each \(V_i\) the Hilbert polynomial is locally
403	  constant (flat families have locally constant Hilbert polynomial), hence
404	  takes finitely many values on the noetherian (hence quasi-compact) scheme
405	  \(V_i\); the finite union of finite sets is finite.
406	\end{proof}
407	
408	\begin{lemma}
409	[Assembly of the strata by Noetherian induction]
410	  \label{lem:noetherian_induction_strata}
411	  
412	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Assembly via
413	  % direct images \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\) (read from
414	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1992-L2090).
415	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
416	  % "Let \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) denote the projection. Consider the
417	  %  coherent sheaves \(E_0, \ldots, E_n\) on \(S\), defined by
418	  %  \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\mbox{ for } i=0,\ldots,n.\)
419	  %  By applying the special case of of the theorem (where the relative dimension
420	  %  \(n\) of \(\PP^n_S\) is \(0\)) to the sheaf \(E_0\) on \(\PP^0_S=S\), we get
421	  %  a stratification \((W_{e_0})\) of \(S\)
422	  %  indexed by integers \(e_0\), such that for any morphism
423	  %  \(f: T\to S\) the pull-back \(f^*E_0\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module
424	  %  of rank \(e_0\) if and only if \(f\) factors via \(W_{e_0} \hra S\).
425	  %  Next, for each stratum \(W_{e_0}\), we take the
426	  %  flattening stratification \((W_{e_0,e_1})\) for \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\), and so on.
427	  %  Thus in \(n+1\) steps, we obtain finitely many
428	  %  locally closed subschemes
429	  %  \(W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \subset S\)
430	  %  such that for any morphism \(f:T\to S\) the pull-back
431	  %  \(f^*E_i\) for \(i=0,\ldots,n\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-modules of
432	  %  of constant rank \(e_i\) if and only if \(f\) factors
433	  %  via \(W_{e_0,\ldots,e_n} \hra S\). [...]
434	  %  This shows that at any point \(s\in W_f\), the Hilbert polynomial
435	  %  \(P_s(m)\) equals \(f\). The desired locally closed subscheme \(S_f \subset S\),
436	  %  whose existence is asserted by the theorem,
437	  %  will turn out to be a certain closed subscheme \(S_f \subset W_f\) whose
438	  %  underlying subset is all of \(|W_f|\). [...]
439	  %  As \(S_f\) is the flattening stratification for \(\pi_*\F(p)\),
440	  %  the property (iii) of the theorem follows from the corresponding property in
441	  %  the case \(n=0\), applied to the sheaf \(\pi_*\F(p)\) on \(S\)."
442	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (assembly step combining the \(n=0\) special case
443	  applied to direct images \(\pi_*\F(N+i)\)).}
444	  Let \(S\), \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\), \(\F\) be as in
445	  Theorem~REF.  There exists an integer
446	  \(N \ge 0\) such that for every \(s \in S\) the higher cohomologies
447	  \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) vanish for all \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N\), and the base-change
448	  homomorphism \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism
449	  for \(m \ge N\), \(s \in S\).  Iterating the \(n=0\) stratification
450	  (Lemma~REF) on the coherent sheaves \(E_i := \pi_* \F(N+i)\)
451	  for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) produces a finite collection of locally-closed
452	  subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\) indexed by tuples
453	  \((e_0, \dots, e_n) \in \Z^{n+1}\). Re-indexing each tuple by the unique
454	  numerical polynomial \(f \in \Q[\lambda]\) of degree \(\le n\) with
455	  \(f(N+i) = e_i\) gives a finite stratification \(\{W_f\}\) of \(S\), and the
456	  locally-closed subschemes \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) of
457	  Theorem~REF are obtained by further
458	  intersecting with the closed-subscheme conditions that
459	  \(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) be locally free of rank \(f(N+i)\) for all \(i \ge 0\)
460	  (terminating by Noetherianity).
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: full proof is the long body of [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem
464	% ``flattening stratification'', spanning L1844-L2090 (~250 lines: special
465	% case, Noetherian induction, statements (A)/(B)/(C), then the assembly via
466	% direct images and the closed-subscheme construction inside each \(W_f\)).
467	% Verbatim retrieved per-sub-lemma above; the structural restatement below in
468	% project notation is the canonical Nitsure-style restated proof.
469	\begin{proof}
470	  
471	  By Lemma~REF, the family of Hilbert polynomials
472	  \(\{P_s\}\) on \(S\) is finite, and there is a finite stratification
473	  \(\{V_i\}\) of \(S\) on each of which \(\F\) is flat with locally-constant
474	  Hilbert polynomial.  Apply semi-continuity (cohomology and base change,
475	  [Hartshorne], III.12 / [Stacks Project], tag 02KH for the \(H^0\)-version) on
476	  the noetherian \(V_i\) to obtain a uniform integer \(N_1\) with
477	  \(R^r \pi_* \F(m) = 0\) for \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\), and \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m)) = 0\)
478	  for all \(s \in S\), \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\) (statement (B) of Nitsure's proof).
479	  Choose \(r_i \ge N_1\) on each \(V_i\) such that the base-change comparison
480	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_{V_i} \to (\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m)\) is an isomorphism for
481	  \(m \ge r_i\); composing with the cohomology-and-base-change isomorphism
482	  \(((\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m))|_s \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) for
483	  the flat family on \(V_i\) yields uniform \(N = \max r_i\) such that
484	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism for \(m \ge N\)
485	  and all \(s \in S\) (statement (C)).
486	
487	  Apply Lemma~REF sequentially to the coherent sheaves
488	  \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\): \(E_0\) produces a
489	  stratification \(\{W_{e_0}\}\) of \(S\); on each \(W_{e_0}\) apply the lemma to
490	  \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\) to refine to \(\{W_{e_0, e_1}\}\), and continue. After \(n+1\)
491	  steps the \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n}\) form a finite locally-closed stratification
492	  of \(S\) universal for the property ``each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free
493	  \(\OO_T\)-module of constant rank \(e_i\)''.  Re-index \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
494	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\) to get
495	  the family \(\{W_f\}\) indexed by polynomials.  At any \(s \in W_f\), statement
496	  (C) and vanishing (B) force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has degree \(\le n\) and is
497	  determined by its values at \(N, N+1, \dots, N+n\)).
498	
499	  The Hilbert-polynomial-coincidence locus \(|W_f|\) may have non-flat scheme
500	  structure; refine to \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) by intersecting with the
501	  closed-subscheme conditions ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally-free of rank
502	  \(f(N+i)\)'' for all \(i \ge 0\).  These conditions are cut out by an increasing
503	  chain of coherent ideal sheaves \(I_0 \subseteq I_0 + I_1 \subseteq \cdots\) on
504	  \(W_f\); Noetherianity terminates the chain in finitely many steps, defining a
505	  coherent ideal sheaf \(I\) and a closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) with
506	  \(|S_f| = |W_f|\) on which \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) is locally-free of rank \(f(N+i)\)
507	  for all \(i \ge 0\). The base-change-without-flatness lemma (cf.\ [Nitsure],
508	  \S3) yields some \(N' \ge N\) such that for \(i \ge N'\),
509	  \((\pi_* \F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism, hence
510	  the graded \(A\)-module \(\bigoplus_{i \ge N'} (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is
511	  locally-free on \(S_f\), hence \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\). The
512	  closure-of-strata property (iii) reduces to the corresponding property in
513	  the \(n=0\) case for \(\pi_* \F(p)\) on \(S\) at any \(p \ge N\) separating all
514	  Hilbert polynomials.
515	\end{proof}
516	
517	\section{Universal property of the strata}
518	\label{sec:flatstrat_universal}
519	
520	\begin{theorem}
521	
522	\leanok
523	[Universal property of the flattening stratification]
524	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_universal}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal}
526	  
527	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', part (ii)
528	  % universal property
529	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1826-L1834).
530	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
531	  % "\textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
532	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
533	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
534	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
535	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
536	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
537	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
538	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
539	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\)."
540	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (part (ii) of the main theorem); cf.\ [Stacks
541	  Project], tag 052H.}
542	  Let \(S\), \(\F\), and \(\{S_f\}_{f \in I}\) be as in
543	  Theorem~REF.  Set
544	  \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) with the coproduct morphism
545	  \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the locally-closed inclusions.  Then \(i^* \F\)
546	  is flat over \(S'\), and the pair \((S', i)\) is universal: for any morphism
547	  \(\varphi : T \to S\) of noetherian schemes (one may take \(T\) arbitrary, the
548	  Noetherian hypothesis on \(S\) being preserved), the pullback
549	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is \(T\)-flat if and only if \(\varphi\) factors
550	  uniquely through \(i\). In particular each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
551	\end{theorem}
552	
553	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that the morphism \(\coprod_f S_f \,\to \,S\)
554	% indeed has the property (ii) of the theorem.
555	% By Lemma REF,
556	% there exists some \(N' \ge N\) such that for all \(i\ge N'\),
557	% the base-change
558	% \((\pi_*\F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_*\F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism for
559	% each \(S_f\). Therefore
560	% \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\) by Lemma REF,
561	% as the direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N'\) are locally
562	% free over \(S_f\). Conversely, if \(\phi : T\to S\) is a morphism such
563	% that \(\F_T\) is flat, then the Hilbert polynomial is locally
564	% constant over \(T\). Let \(T_f\) be the open and closed subscheme of \(T\)
565	% where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\). Clearly, the set
566	% map \(|T_f| \to |S|\) factors via \(|S_f|\). But as the direct images
567	% \({\pi_T}_*\F_T(i)\) are locally free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\), it
568	% follows in fact that the schematic morphism \(T_f \to S\) factors
569	% via \(S_f\), proving the property (ii) of the theorem."
570	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2065-L2082.)
571	\begin{proof}
572	  
573	  Flatness of \(i^* \F\) over \(S'\) was established at the end of the proof of
574	  Theorem~REF (the
575	  base-change-without-flatness lemma plus the graded-module-flatness criterion
576	  applied to the coherent sheaves \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) on each \(S_f\)).
577	  Conversely, if \(\varphi : T \to S\) satisfies \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is
578	  \(T\)-flat, then \(T\)-flatness of the family forces the Hilbert polynomial to be
579	  locally constant on \(T\).  Decompose \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed
580	  subschemes where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\).  The set map \(|T_f| \to |S|\)
581	  factors through \(|S_f|\); further, since the direct images
582	  \((\pi_T)_* \varphi^* \F(i)\) are locally-free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\) for $i
583	  \ge N'\(, the universal property of \)S_f \subseteq W_f$ as the closed
584	  subscheme cut out by these local-freeness conditions
585	  (Lemma~REF) yields a unique scheme
586	  morphism \(T_f \to S_f\) extending the set map. Assembling over \(f\) gives the
587	  unique factorisation \(\varphi = i \circ \tilde \varphi\).
588	\end{proof}
589	
590	\section{Application: relative curves}
591	\label{sec:flatstrat_curve_application}
592	
593	In the Route~A consumer setting, the input to the flattening-stratification
594	machinery is the pullback \(C \times_k T \to T\) where \(C\) is a smooth proper
595	curve over a field \(k\) and \(T\) is any \(k\)-scheme.  The hypotheses of
596	Nitsure's Theorem~REF demand
597	\emph{projective} relative dimension, not merely \emph{proper}; we record that
598	no extra work is required to instantiate the theorem in our setting.
599	
600	\begin{lemma}
601	[Smooth proper curve over \(k\) is projective]
602	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective}
603	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6 (curve \(\Rightarrow\) quasi-projective;
604	  % proper \(\Rightarrow\) projective for curves), and standard consequences in
605	  % chapter III. The precise statement ``every smooth proper curve over a field
606	  % is projective'' is one of the classical equivalences for \(\dim 1\) and is
607	  % textbook material; this block is project-bespoke (no verbatim source
608	  % quote is reproduced here as the result is folklore-level and cited only
609	  % structurally for the Route~A reduction).
610	  \textit{Source: classical; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.6.7 \& II.7.6 (curves).}
611	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\).  Then \(C \to \Spec k\) is
612	  projective.  Consequently, for any noetherian \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the relative
613	  curve \(C \times_k T \to T\) is projective in the sense of Grothendieck (there
614	  exists a relatively very ample line bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\)).
615	\end{lemma}
616	
617	\begin{proof}
618	  Projectivity of a smooth proper curve over a field is classical
619	  ([Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6; alternatively: every divisor of large
620	  positive degree on a smooth proper curve \(C\) is very ample, providing the
621	  embedding \(C \hookrightarrow \PP^N_k\) for some \(N\)). Pulling the ample line
622	  bundle back along \(C \times_k T \to C\) produces a relatively very ample line
623	  bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\).
624	\end{proof}
625	
626	\begin{corollary}
627	[Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative curve]
628	  \label{cor:flattening_stratification_curve}
629	  
630	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a smooth proper curve over \(k\), and \(T\) a noetherian
631	  \(k\)-scheme.  Let \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(C \times_k T\). Then the
632	  flattening-stratification conclusion of
633	  Theorem~REF applies: there is a finite
634	  set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials and locally-closed subschemes
635	  \(T_f \hookrightarrow T\) for \(f \in I\), set-theoretically covering \(T\)
636	  disjointly, such that (a) \(\F|_{C \times_k T_f}\) is flat over \(T_f\) for
637	  each \(f\), and (b) any morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) such that
638	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(C \times_k T'\) is \(T'\)-flat factors uniquely through some
639	  \(T_f\).
640	\end{corollary}
641	
642	\begin{proof}
643	  
644	  By Lemma~REF, \(C \times_k T \to T\) is
645	  projective, hence factors through a closed immersion into \(\PP^n_T\) for some
646	  \(n \ge 0\).  Push \(\F\) forward along the closed immersion (extending by zero
647	  outside the image) to a coherent sheaf \(\tilde \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\); this
648	  operation preserves \(T\)-flatness in both directions because the closed
649	  immersion is a finite morphism.  Apply
650	  Theorem~REF to \(\tilde \F\) on
651	  \(\PP^n_T\) to obtain \(\{T_f\}\) with the stated universal property.
652	\end{proof}
653

27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
41	
42	\begin{theorem}\leanok
43	[Tangent space at the identity: \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)]
44	  \label{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}
45	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.tangentSpaceIso}
46	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
47	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
48	        def:genus}
49	
50	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~thm:tgtsp;
51	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~41 (read from
52	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3265-L3270).
53	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
54	  % "\begin{thm}\label{thm:tgtsp}
55	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
56	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Let \(\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\) denote the
57	  % tangent space at \(0\).  Then
58	  %    \(\)\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}=\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
59	  %  \end{thm}"
60	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
61	  Thm.~thm:tgtsp; cf.\ [Hartshorne], ``Algebraic Geometry'', GTM 52,
62	  IV.1.1 (genus def.\ \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)).}
63	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
64	  \(g = g(C)\) over a field \(k\), and let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
65	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) be the identity component of the
66	  Picard scheme of \(C/k\). There is a canonical isomorphism of
67	  \(k\)-vector spaces
68	  \[
69	    \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \;\cong\; H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C).
70	  \]
71	  In particular, the \(k\)-dimension of \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals
72	  the genus \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)
73	  (\cref{def:genus}).
74	\end{theorem}
75	
76	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
77	% L3358-L3367 + L3404-L3407: the construction of the comparison map and
78	% its identification as an isomorphism. Kleiman's full proof spans
79	% L3271-L3408 and is too long for one verbatim block, so we reproduce
80	% the two paragraphs that contain the load-bearing exact sequence and
81	% the conclusion; see Notes for Plan Agent.)
82	% "To compute this kernel, set \(X_\ve:=X\ox_kk_\ve\), and form the truncated
83	% exponential sequence of sheaves of Abelian groups:
84	%    \(\)0\to \mc O_{\!X} \to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^* \to \mc O_{\!X}^* \to 1,\(\)
85	%  where the first map takes a local section \(b\) to \(1+b\ve\).  This
86	% sequence is split by the map \(\mc O_{\!X}^*\to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*\) defined by
87	% \(a\mapsto a+0\cdot\ve\).  Hence taking cohomology yields this split exact
88	% sequence of Abelian groups:
89	%    \(\)0\to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}) \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*)
90	%     \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}^*) \to 1.\(\)"
91	% "In Square (\ref{CD:tgtsp}), the two vertical maps are isomorphisms by
92	% Exercise~\ref{ex:Alr} since \(\IPic_{X/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\) and
93	% \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, \(v\)
94	% too is an isomorphism, as desired."
95	\begin{proof}
96	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
97	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
98	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~thm:tgtsp. By
99	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
100	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
101	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
102	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
103	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
104	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
105	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
106	
107	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
108	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
109	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
110	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
111	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
112	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
113	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
114	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
115	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
116	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
117	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
118	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
119	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
120	  \[
121	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
122	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
123	  \]
124	
125	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
126	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
127	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
128	  abelian groups
129	  \[
130	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
131	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
132	  \]
133	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
134	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
135	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
136	  \[
137	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
138	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
139	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
140	  \]
141	
142	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
143	  represents the étale-sheafification of
144	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
145	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
146	  \[
147	    \begin{array}{ccc}
148	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
149	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
150	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
151	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
152	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
153	    \end{array}
154	  \]
155	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
156	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 5, Exercise~ex:Alr;
157	  invocation legitimised by
158	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
159	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
160	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
161	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
162	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
163	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
164	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
165	
166	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
167	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
168	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
169	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
170	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
171	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
172	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
173	
174	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
175	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
176	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
177	\end{proof}
178	
179	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
180	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
181	
182	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
183	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
184	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
185	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
186	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
187	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
188	dimension \(g\).
189	
190	\begin{theorem}\leanok
191	[Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
192	  \label{thm:pic0_smooth}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.smooth}
194	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
195	        def:pic_zero_subscheme,
196	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
197	
198	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0;
199	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~44 (read from
200	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3427 +
201	  % L3442-L3446).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
203	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
204	  % \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
205	  %    \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
206	  %  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
207	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
208	  % everywhere.
209	  %  \end{cor}"
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
211	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:ch0}
212	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
213	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\), then
214	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere.
215	  %  \end{cor}"
216	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
217	  Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties''
218	  (course notes, 2008), \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (dimension of \(J\)
219	  equals the genus).}
220	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
221	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
222	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is smooth over \(k\) of dimension
223	  \(g\): the structural morphism
224	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is smooth, and every closed point of
225	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) lies in a smooth open neighbourhood.
226	\end{theorem}
227	
228	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
229	% L3428-L3439: the proof of Cor.~cor:sm. We reproduce the full proof,
230	% which deduces smoothness everywhere from smoothness at \(0\) by
231	% translation.)
232	% "\begin{proof} Plainly we may assume \(k\) is algebraically closed. Then,
233	% given any closed point \(\lambda\in\IPic_{X/k}\), there is an automorphism
234	% of \(\IPic_{X/k}\) that carries 0 to \(\lambda\), namely, ``multiplication''
235	% by \(\lambda\).  So \(\IPic_{X/k}\) has the same dimension at \(\lambda\) as
236	% at 0, and \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is
237	% smooth at 0.
238	%
239	% By general principles, \(\dim_0 \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\),
240	% and equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\).
241	% Moreover, \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\) if and only if it is smooth
242	% at \(0\) since \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, the corollary
243	% results from Theorem~\ref{thm:tgtsp}.
244	%  \end{proof}"
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
247	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
248	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
249	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm, with the curve-specific dimension
250	  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.
251	
252	  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
253	  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
254	  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
255	  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
256	  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
257	  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
258	  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
259	  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
260	  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
261	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
262	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
263	  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
264	  \(0\).
265	
266	  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
267	  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
268	  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
269	  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
270	  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
271	  smoothness. Hence
272	  \(
273	    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
274	  \)
275	  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
276	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).
277	
278	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
279	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
280	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~ex:jac
281	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
282	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
283	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
284	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
285	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:ch0): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
286	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
287	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
288	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
289	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
290	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
291	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
292	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
293	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
294	
295	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
296	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
297	\end{proof}
298	
299	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
300	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
301	
302	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
303	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
304	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
305	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
306	
307	\begin{theorem}\leanok
308	[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
309	  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
311	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
312	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
313	
314	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p;
315	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
316	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2940).
317	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
318	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
319	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
320	  % and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
321	  % quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
322	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective.
323	  %  \end{thm}"
324	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
325	  Thm.~th:qpp\&p; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes,
326	  2008), \S I.6 (abelian varieties are projective, p.~27).}
327	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
328	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
329	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is projective over \(k\), in
330	  particular proper: the structural morphism
331	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is a projective morphism, hence
332	  satisfies the universal closedness and separatedness conditions of
333	  properness.
334	\end{theorem}
335	
336	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
337	% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
338	% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
339	% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on
340	% geometric normality of \(C\).)
341	% "Suppose \(X\) is also geometrically normal.  Since \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is
342	% quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
343	% proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) commutes
344	% with extending \(k\).  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a \(k\)-scheme is
345	% complete if (and only if) it is after extending \(k\).  So we may, and do,
346	% assume \(k\) is algebraically closed.
347	%
348	% Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
349	% groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over \(k\); see
350	% \cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
351	% group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
352	% group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
353	% solvable linear algebraic \(k\)-group is triangularizable (the
354	% Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
355	% of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
356	% and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is commutative, so solvable.
357	% Hence it suffices to show that, if \(T\) denotes the
358	% affine line minus the origin, then every \(k\)-map \(t\:T\to
359	% (\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}\) is constant."
360	\begin{proof}
361	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
362	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
363	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
364	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, specialised to \(X = C\) a
365	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
366	
367	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
368	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
369	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
370	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
371	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
372	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
373	  (\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, first conclusion) then gives that
374	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
375	  is projective and geometrically integral.
376	
377	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
378	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
379	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
380	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
381	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
382	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
383	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
384	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
385	
386	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
387	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
388	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
389	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
390	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
391	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
392	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
393	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
394	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
395	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
396	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
397	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
398	  is constant.
399	
400	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
401	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
402	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
403	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
404	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
405	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:cmp) \(t\) is represented by an
406	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
407	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
408	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
409	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
410	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
411	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
412	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
413	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
414	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
415	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
416	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
417	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
418	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
419	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
420	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
421	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
422	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
423	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
424	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
425	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
426	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
427	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
428	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
429	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
430	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
431	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
432	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
433	  properness.
434	\end{proof}
435	
436	\section{Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
437	\label{sec:pic0_geom_irred}
438	
439	Geometric irreducibility is delivered directly by the abstract
440	identity-component substrate
441	(\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}) applied
442	to the \(k\)-group scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\). We restate the
443	specialisation here for the assembly downstream.
444	
445	\begin{theorem}\leanok
446	[Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
447	  \label{thm:pic0_geom_irred}
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible}
449	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
450	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
451	
452	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0;
453	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
454	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2921-L2929).
455	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
456	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
457	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
458	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
459	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
460	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
461	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
462	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
463	  % \(k\).
464	  %  \end{prp}"
465	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0
466	  (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the
467	  identity).}
468	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
469	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
470	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is geometrically irreducible: after
471	  base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme
472	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) is irreducible.
473	\end{theorem}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  \uses{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
477	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes,
478	        thm:fga_pic_representability}
479	  Apply the abstract identity-component substrate to the \(k\)-group
480	  scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), which is locally of finite type by
481	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. By
482	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}, the
483	  identity component \((\Pic_{C/k})^0 = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite
484	  type and geometrically irreducible over \(k\). The base-change
485	  compatibility of
486	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes} identifies
487	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\),
488	  closing the proof.
489	\end{proof}
490	
491	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
492	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
493	
494	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
495	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
496	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
497	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
498	identity-component group-scheme structure of
499	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
500	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
501	gate for Route~A.
502	
503	\begin{theorem}\leanok
504	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
505	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
506	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
507	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
508	        thm:pic0_proper,
509	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
510	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
511	
512	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
513	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
514	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
515	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
516	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
517	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
518	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
519	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
520	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
521	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
522	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
523	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
524	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
525	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
526	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
527	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
528	  % Exercise~REF."
529	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
530	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
531	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac (the Jacobian
532	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
533	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
535	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
536	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
537	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
538	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
539	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
540	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
541	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
542	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
543	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
544	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
545	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{proof}
549	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
550	        thm:pic0_proper,
551	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
552	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
553	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
554	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
555	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
556	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
557	
558	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
559	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
560	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
561	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
562	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
563	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
564	
565	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
566	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
567	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
568	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
569	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
570	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
571	
572	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
573	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
574	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
575	
576	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
577	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
578	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
579	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
580	
581	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
582	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
583	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
584	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
585	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
586	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
587	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
588	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
589	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
590	
591	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac: for \(X/S\)
592	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
593	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
594	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
595	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
596	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
597	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
598	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
599	\end{proof}
600

657	
658	\section{Out of scope}
659	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety_out_of_scope}
660	
661	This chapter packages \emph{only} the five A.3.iii--vii steps. The
662	following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters
663	and are explicitly out of scope here:
664

666	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}

680	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the trivial group scheme \(\Spec k\)
681	    (Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)); this is consistent with the
682	    abelian-variety assembly (a point is trivially an abelian variety
683	    of dimension \(0\)) and is handled uniformly here, but is not

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined
11	% that Mathlib at the project's pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) ships every
12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a \(\Spec\) with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-\(1\) specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.
88	
89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let \(X\) be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subset. For any \(z \in U\),
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right \(U\) carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle\( denotes \)z\( viewed as a point of \)U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers \(n\), of the
118	  set of \(n\) for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with \(\prec\) denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at \(z\) in \(X\) and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of \(U\).
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in \(U\) embed into \(X\).} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(U\)
127	  then \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(X\), and distinct points of \(U\) remain
128	  distinct in \(X\). Hence every strict-generisation chain in \(U\) starting at
129	  \(z\) lifts to one in \(X\) of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in \(X\) starting at \(z\) all lie in \(U\).} Conversely, suppose
132	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in \(X\) with \(a_0 = z \in U\). For each \(i \geq 0\), the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z\( (in the specialisation preorder) is \)z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  \(a_i\) is in the closure of \(\{z\}\) \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order \(a \prec b\) records \(b \rightsquigarrow a\)
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so \(a_i \succeq z\) in \(X\) means
138	  \(a_i \rightsquigarrow z\) in \(X\). By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  \(a \rightsquigarrow z\) with \(z\) in an open set \(U\) forces \(a \in U\), since
141	  \(U\) is open and contains \(z\), hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains \(z\) --- equivalently every \(a\) with \(a \rightsquigarrow z\)),
143	  every \(a_i\) lies in \(U\). Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle\( in \)U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at \(z\) (resp.\ at \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\)) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
