152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(p \in \Spec R\) (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains \(\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}\)
174	  ending at the prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) corresponding to \(p\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on \(\Spec R\) is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). Concretely, for
182	  \(p, q \in \Spec R\) with underlying primes \(\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}\),
183	  the relation \(p \leq q\) in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  \(q \rightsquigarrow p\) in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment \(\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}\). This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on \(\Spec R\)
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of \(p\) in \(\Spec R\)
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at \(p\),
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains \(p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of \(\mathfrak{p}\) in
195	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\) (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification \(\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}\)
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{The coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  
229	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in
234	  the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of \(z\).} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set \(X\) itself, there exists an affine open
247	  \(U \subseteq X\) with \(z \in U\). Write \(\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U\)
248	  for \(z\) viewed as a point of the subscheme \(U\).
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to \(z\) inside \(U\).} Since
251	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\) via the affine equivalence
252	  \(U \simeq \Spec R\) (with \(R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\)), the point
253	  \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\) corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,\(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\).
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of \(\Gamma(U)\) at
258	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}\). In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\) as a localisation of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\) in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where \(\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})\) is the prime-ideal height in
273	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  \(\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}\) used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from \(U\) to \(X\).} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to \(R\) and the
280	  affine identification \(U \simeq \Spec R\),
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  \(U \subseteq X\) promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)\). Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{\(\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1\)}{KrullDimLE 1}}
293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-\(1\) case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight \(= 1\) forces stalk \(\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1\)]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be a point with
310	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\). Then the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
311	  has Krull dimension at most \(1\), equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\).
320	  The Mathlib predicate \(\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R\) unfolds to
321	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1\), so the inequality \(1 \leq 1\)
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}

325	\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
326	\label{sec:coheight_consumers}
327	
328	\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
329	\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
330	The private helper
331	\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
332	inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
333	\texttt{sorry} of the form
334	\[
335	  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
336	  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
337	\]
338	After this chapter's
339	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
340	\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
341	two independent goals:
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item The right conjunct \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1\)
344	    is discharged by
345	    \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\) (the standing codim-\(1\) hypothesis
346	    on \(z\)) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
347	    pair \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
348	    and \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1\) (also from the bridge, since
349	    \(1 \leq 1\)) gives equality.
350	  \item The left conjunct \(\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
351	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
352	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
353	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
354	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A \(\to\) IsRegularLocalRing \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\)} at
355	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is \(\sim 200\)--\(300\)~LOC of
356	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
357	    chapter).
358	\end{itemize}
359	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
360	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
361	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
364	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
365	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
366	argument
367	\[
368	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
369	\]
370	at every use site. Because \(Y\) is a prime divisor of \(X\) (so
371	\(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1\) by the definition of a prime
372	divisor as a codim-\(1\) irreducible closed subset),
373	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
374	automatically from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\), so the explicit instance
375	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
376	
377	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-\(1\) infrastructure.} Any subsequent
378	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-\(1\) point has
379	Krull dim \(\leq 1\)'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-\(1\)
380	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
381	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
382	The instance form
383	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
384	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.

1893	[\(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) is reduced]
1894	  \label{lem:projlinebar_isReduced}
1895	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBar_isReduced}
1896	  % Archon-original Lean realisation of an elementary textbook fact (projective space over
1897	  % a field is reduced) via the explicit chart cover of \cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
1898	  % and the canonical \(\mathrm{val}\)-injection of a homogeneous localization into the
1899	  % corresponding plain localization. No external % SOURCE QUOTE needed.
1900	  The scheme underlying \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) is reduced. By the two-chart affine cover
1901	  (\cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}) it suffices to show that each chart ring is
1902	  reduced. The chart-\(i\) ring \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) embeds into the ordinary
1903	  localization \(\bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]\) of the polynomial ring \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) at
1904	  the powers of \(X_i\) via the canonical injection $\mathrm{val} \colon
1905	  \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \hookrightarrow \bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]$
1906	  (\(\mathtt{HomogeneousLocalization.val\_injective}\)); the codomain is a localization of a
1907	  polynomial ring at a non-zero-divisor (the powers of \(X_i\)), hence a domain, hence
1908	  reduced. A subring of a reduced ring is reduced
1909	  (\(\mathtt{Function.Injective.isDomain}\) on the \(\mathrm{val}\)-embedding), so each chart
1910	  ring is a domain --- in particular reduced --- and the global reducedness follows.
1911	\end{lemma}

1938	  \textbf{The scaling action (primary).} The \emph{multiplicative scaling action}
1939	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1940	     \text{``}\sigma_\times(x, \lambda) = \lambda x\text{''} \]
1941	  (\lean name \texttt{gmScalingP1}) is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of
1942	  \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m\) --- no indeterminacy, characteristic-free (M\"obius scaling
1943	  fixing both \(0\) and \(\infty\)) --- defined chartwise:
1944	  \begin{itemize}
1945	    \item On \(\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m\) (target chart \(x\)): \((x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x\),
1946	      a polynomial map, regular everywhere.
1947	    \item Near \(\infty\) (target chart \(u = 1/x\)): the target coordinate \(1/(\lambda x)\) rewrites as
1948	      \(u/\lambda\), regular everywhere on this chart because \(\lambda \in \mathbb G_m\) is invertible.
1949	  \end{itemize}
1950	  The two target charts cover the image and overlap consistently, so \(\sigma_\times\) is defined on
1951	  the whole surface. It is a left action of the group \(\mathbb G_m\) on \(\mathbb P^1\):
1952	  \(\sigma_\times(x, 1) = x\) and $\sigma_\times(\sigma_\times(x, \lambda), \mu) = \sigma_\times(x,
1953	  \lambda\mu)\(. Crucially it has \emph{two fixed points}, \)\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ and
1954	  \(\sigma_\times(\infty, \lambda) = \infty\) for all \(\lambda\); the fixed point \(0\) is what makes the
1955	  \(W\)-axis of the additive decomposition collapse in the primary base-case proof
1956	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}).
1957	  \medskip

1959	  Milne-faithful additive alternative we also record the \emph{translation action}
1960	  \[ \sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1961	     \text{``}\sigma(x, y) = x + y\text{''} \]
1962	  (\lean name \texttt{gaTranslationP1} [expected]), likewise a \emph{total} scheme morphism on all
1963	  of \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a\), defined chartwise: on \(\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_a\)
1964	  (chart \(x\)) it is the polynomial map \((x, y) \mapsto x + y\); near \(\infty\) (\(u = 1/x\)) the
1965	  coordinate \(1/(x + y)\) rewrites as \(u/(1 + u y)\), regular at \(u = 0\) (value \(0\) there --- \(\sigma\)
1966	  fixes \(\infty\) for every \(y\)), while the locus \(x + y = 0\) is covered by the chart \(x\) on which it
1967	  is the finite value \(0\). It is a left action of \(\mathbb G_a\) on \(\mathbb P^1\) fixing \(\infty\):
1968	  \(\sigma(x, 0) = x\) and \(\sigma(\sigma(x, y), y') = \sigma(x, y + y')\). This companion underlies
1969	  only the alternative proof of \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} and the demoted $\mathbb
1970	  G_a$-additive close of the base case (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}); it is \emph{not} on the
1971	  genus-\(0\) critical path.
1972	\end{definition}

2255	      The chart-agreement equation

2257	      \mathrm{pullback.snd} \fatsemi \mathtt{gmScalingP1\_chart}\,\bar k\,1\)
2258	      on the cover intersection
2259	      \(\mathrm{pullback}\,((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_0.f)\,
2260	      ((\mathtt{gmScalingP1\_cover})_1.f)\) is the statement that two morphisms
2261	      \(\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1 \colon \mathrm{(intersection)} \to
2262	      \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) agree on that intersection scheme. \emph{The key
2263	      observation is that \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) is separated over \(\bar k\)},
2264	      so the diagonal
2265	      \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}} \colon
2266	        \overline{\mathbb P^1} \longrightarrow

2268	      is a closed immersion. Because \(\overline{\mathbb P^1} = \Proj(\bar k[X_0, X_1])\)
2269	      is the \(\Proj\) of a graded \(\bar k\)-algebra, its separatedness over \(\Spec
2270	      \bar k\) is the standard Stacks fact ``\(\Proj(S)\) is separated'' (Stacks tag
2271	      \texttt{01KU} / lemma \texttt{lemma-proj-separated}); Mathlib supplies the
2272	      categorical packaging via \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\) and
2273	      \(\mathtt{IsSeparated.diagonal\_isClosedImmersion}\).
2274	      \emph{Bypass.} Reformulate the cocycle as: the pair-morphism
2275	      \((\mathrm{chart}_0, \mathrm{chart}_1) \colon \mathrm{(intersection)}
2276	      \longrightarrow \overline{\mathbb P^1} \times_{\bar k} \overline{\mathbb P^1}\)

2281	      \bar k\,1\)) \emph{factors through the diagonal
2282	      \(\Delta_{\overline{\mathbb P^1}}\)}. By Mathlib's
2283	      \(\mathtt{IsClosedImmersion.lift\_iff\_range\_subset}\) (the universal property
2284	      of a closed immersion: a morphism factors through a closed immersion iff its
2285	      set-theoretic image is contained in the image of the immersion), the
2286	      factorization is equivalent to the set-level containment of the cocycle's image
2287	      in the image of \(\Delta\), which on points is exactly the chart equality on

2289	      discrete (each closed point of the intersection lies over a single \(\bar k\)-

## 1. RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex
This chapter covers the Riemann-Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2). Key contents:
- **Definitions**: Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve (def:eulerChar_curve), ℓ-invariant of a Weil divisor (def:l_invariant)
- **Main Theorems**: The χ-identity χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 - g (thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus), Riemann-Roch formula in genus zero ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1 (thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero)
- **Substrate Lemmas**: Additivity of Euler characteristic on short exact sequences, iso-invariance of χ, χ(k(P)) = 1 on closed-point skyscraper sheaves
- Uses direct χ inductive proof rather than Serre duality
- Contains detailed proof sketches including the base case D=0 and inductive step for D→D+[P]

## 2. RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex
This chapter establishes Weil divisors and the degree map (RR.1). Key contents:
- **Definitions**: Prime divisor (def:prime_divisor), Weil divisor group (def:codim1_cycles), regular in codimension one (def:isRegularInCodimensionOne), order of a rational function at a prime divisor (def:order_at_point), principal divisor (def:principal_divisor), divisor of a closed point (def:divisor_closed_point), degree of a divisor (def:divisor_degree)
- **Theorems**: Degree map is a group homomorphism (thm:divisor_degree_hom), principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism (thm:principal_hom), principal divisors have degree zero on complete nonsingular curves (thm:principal_deg_zero)
- **Open immersion machinery**: Restriction and push of prime divisors, bijection under open immersion, stalk isomorphism, descent of regularity
- **Lemmas**: Finite support of the order function (lem:rationalMap_order_finite_support)
- Extensively pinned to Hartshorne II.6 and Stacks project references

## 3. RiemannRoch_OCofP.tex
This chapter constructs the line bundle 𝒪_C(P) and global sections (RR.3). Key contents:
- **Definitions**: Presheaf carrier for line bundle (def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf), carrier-submodule sheaf (def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf), line bundle of a closed point (def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint), inclusion into function field (def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField)
- **Main Results**: Global sections as Riemann-Roch space (lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff), H¹ vanishing on genus-0 curves (lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero), dimension formula dim_k H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2 (thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero)
- **Corollary**: Non-constant rational function existence (cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero)
- **Substrate helpers**: toFunctionField injectivity, backward direction of globalSections_iff, constant-function lemma for complete curves
- Uses direct cohomological vanishing via long exact sequence rather than Serre duality

## 4. RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex
This chapter constructs the invertible sheaf 𝒪_C(D) for Weil divisors (RR.2*). Key contents:
- **Definition**: Invertible sheaf 𝒪_C(D) of a Weil divisor (def:sheafOf) as subsheaf of constant sheaf at function field K(C)
- **Immediate Corollaries**: 𝒪_C(0) = 𝒪_C (lem:sheafOf_zero), 𝒪_C([P]) from RR.3 equals general sheafOf at [P] (lem:sheafOf_singlePoint), short exact sequence for D→D+[Y] (lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add)
- **Sheaf property verification**: Functoriality, k-linearity, locality, invertibility
- Uses Hartshorne II.6 pp.144-145 subsheaf-of-K construction
- Gated to RR.2 because the χ-identity proof requires the sheafOf body
- Serves as entry-point for RR.3 global-sections machinery and RR.4 degree computations

## 5. RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex
This chapter proves the genus-0 classification (RR.4). Key contents:
- **Main Theorem**: Genus-0 smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over k̄ is isomorphic to ℙ¹ (thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1)
- **Supporting Lemmas**: 
  - Morphism to ℙ¹ from two globally generating sections (lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections)
  - Degree of morphism via pole divisor (lem:degree_via_pole_divisor)
  - Degree-1 morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism (lem:degree_one_morphism_iso)
- **Proof Strategy**: Uses Riemann-Roch to get 2-dimensional H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P])), constructs explicit morphism via Proj.fromOfGlobalSections, computes degree = 1, applies degree-1 iso lemma
- **Key Dependencies**: Non-constant function from RR.3, Riemann-Roch formula from RR.2, principal degree-zero from RR.1
- References Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5, IV.2 (Hurwitz's theorem), I.6 Corollary 6.12

## 1. Albanese_AlbaneseUP.tex

This chapter covers the Albanese universal property of Pic^0_{C/k}. Key elements:

**Main Theorem (thm:albanese_universal_property)**:
- For a complete nonsingular curve C of genus g > 0 over algebraically closed field k̄
- With basepoint P_0 ∈ C(k̄) and J := Pic^0_{C/k}
- For any abelian variety A and morphism φ: C → A with φ(P_0) = η_A
- There exists a unique homomorphism ψ: J → A such that ψ ∘ ι_{P_0} = φ

**Key Sub-Lemmas**:
- lem:abel_jacobi_morphism - The Abel-Jacobi morphism ι_{P_0}: C → J
- def:symmetric_power_curve - The g-th symmetric power Sym^g C (requires project-side construction via affine patching)
- lem:symmetric_product_av_map - Symmetrisation of φ: C → A to Sym^g φ: Sym^g C → A
- lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian - Birational morphism f^{(g)}: Sym^g C → J (gated on Riemann-Roch)
- lem:descent_through_birational_sigma - Descent of Sym^g φ to a regular morphism J → A via rational map extension

**Proof Route**: Follows Milne's symmetric-power route (avoids theorem of the cube):
1. Symmetrise φ to define Sym^g φ on Sym^g C
2. Use birational morphism Sym^g C ⇢ J
3. Apply Milne Theorem 3.2 (rational_map_to_av_extends) to promote rational map to regular morphism
4. Verify factorisation and homomorphism properties

**Lean Targets**:
- AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi
- AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower (non-Mathlib construction)
- AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap
- AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian
- AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma
- AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property

**Status Notes**: SymmetricPower is marked \notready (requires affine gluing sub-build). Birationality proof depends on Riemann-Roch (Route C paused per user directive).

## 2. Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex

This chapter packages the Auslander-Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ⇒ Cohen-Macaulay" needed by A.4.a.

**Main Theorem (thm:auslander_buchsbaum)**:
- For Noetherian local ring (R, m) and nonzero finite R-module M of finite projective dimension
- depth_R(M) + pd_R(M) = depth(R)

**Key Sub-Components**:
- def:depth - I-depth of a module (regular sequence supremum)
- lem:depth_via_ext - Characterisation: depth(M) = inf{i : Ext^i(κ, M) ≠ 0}
- def:projective_dimension - Infimum of lengths of projective resolutions
- lem:depth_short_exact_sequence - Depth inequalities on short exact sequences

## 4. Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex

This is the A.4.c chapter proving Milne Theorem 3.2.

**Main Theorem (thm:rational_map_to_av_extends)**:
- For nonsingular variety X over algebraically closed field k̄
- And abelian variety A over k̄
- And rational map f: X ⇢ A
- Then f extends uniquely to a regular morphism f̃: X → A

**One-Line Proof** (Milne's proof):
Combine Theorem 3.1 (codim ≥ 2 extension for maps to complete varieties) with Lemma 3.3 (pure-codim-1 indeterminacy for maps to group varieties):
1. By Thm 3.1: codim_X(Z(f)) ≥ 2
2. By Lemma 3.3: Z(f) is empty or pure codim 1
3. Together: Z(f) = ∅, so f is defined everywhere

**Characteristic-Free**: Uses only valuative criterion of properness + regular-sequence depth + Weil-divisor arguments. No characteristic restrictions.

**Consumption**:
- In A.4.d (Albanese_AlbaneseUP): promotes rational map C^{(g)} ⇢ A to regular morphism via symmetric-product descent to J
- Alternative to autoduality route (which would invoke theorem of the cube, excised at iter-163)

## 1. Picard_FGAPicRepresentability.tex

This chapter (A.2.c) packages the FGA representability of the Picard scheme as a k-group scheme. Key sections include:

- **Setup and motivation**: Describes how the chapter assembles inputs from the Quot-scheme engine (A.2.b) and the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor (A.1.c) via Grothendieck's existence theorem and the Abel map.

- **Line bundles and the Quot/Hilbert scheme** (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}): The Abel map sends relative effective divisors to their associated invertible sheaves, representing a correspondence between the Quot/Hilbert scheme machinery.

- **The FGA representability theorem** (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}): The main theorem states that the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor Pic°_(C/k)et is representable by a k-scheme Pic_(C/k), separated and locally of finite type, which is a disjoint union of open quasi-projective k-subschemes. The proof proceeds in four steps: (1) stratify by Hilbert polynomial, (2) bound twists by m-regularity, (3) factor through the Abel map, (4) descend via the smooth-proper quotient lemma.

- **Smooth-proper quotient lemma** (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}): Given a surjection α: Z → P of étale sheaves with Z quasi-projective, R := Z ×_P Z representable, and the first projection R → Z smooth and proper, then P is representable by a quasi-projective scheme.

- **Group-scheme structure** (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}): Pic_(C/k) inherits an abelian group structure from tensor product of invertible sheaves, transferred via Yoneda from the abelian-group structure of the presheaf.

- **The Picard scheme definition** (\cref{def:pic_scheme}): Names the representing scheme as an abelian k-group scheme locally of finite type.

- **Lean encoding**: Lists the seven Lean target declarations and their role in the construction.

- **Sorry-by-sorry closure order**: Documents seven open sorries (typeclass instances) and ranks them by closure difficulty: Rank 1 (none), Rank 2 (Sorries 1-4, depending on sibling chapters or Mathlib gaps), Rank 3 (Sorries 5-7, depending on Route C substrate—Riemann–Roch).

## 2. Picard_IdentityComponent.tex

This chapter (A.3) builds the identity component Pic⁰_(C/k) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of Pic_(C/k) and identifies it as an abelian variety of dimension g(C).

- **Setup and motivation**: The identity component Pic⁰_(C/k) is the connected component of Pic_(C/k) containing the trivial bundle; it consists of degree-zero line bundles and is the Jacobian variety of C.

- **Identity component of a group scheme** (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}): Abstract definition for a k-group scheme G locally of finite type—the topological connected component of the identity element with reduced-induced subscheme structure. New project material (not in Mathlib).

- **Four theorems on identity components**:
  1. \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: G⁰ is an open and closed subscheme.
  2. \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}: The inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism.
  3. \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}: G⁰ is of finite type and geometrically irreducible.
  4. \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}: Formation commutes with base change.

- **Stacks 037Q lemma** (\cref{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}): Connected + k-rational section ⟹ geometrically connected (foundational for base-change commutation).

- **Identity component of the Picard scheme** (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}): Specialization of the abstract substrate to G = Pic_(C/k).

- **Degree map** (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}): A group homomorphism Pic_(C/k)(k) → ℤ extracting the leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf. The connected components of Pic_(C/k) are indexed by degree.

- **The Pic-zero abelian variety**: Three theorems:
  1. \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}: Pic⁰_(C/k) is proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible, and carries a group-scheme structure.
  2. \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}: dim_k Pic⁰_(C/k) = g(C).
  3. \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}: Pic⁰_(C/k)(k) = ker(deg).

- **Lean encoding**: Lists nine target declarations corresponding to the abstract and specialized theorems.

- **Out of scope**: Albanese universal property, autoduality, torsion component, degree map as a scheme morphism.

---

## 3. Picard_QuotScheme.tex

This chapter (A.2.b) packages Grothendieck–Altman–Kleiman's Quot-scheme construction, the existence engine for the Picard functor. The file exceeds 1436 lines; I'll provide a summary of the key sections:

- **Setup and motivation**: The Quot functor Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} parametrizes T-flat coherent quotients of E_T on X_T = X ×_S T with constant Hilbert polynomial Φ on fibers. It's represented by a projective S-scheme.

- **Hilbert polynomial** (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}): For a coherent sheaf F on X with support proper over S, the polynomial Φ(m) = χ(F(m)) computed relative to a line bundle L. For flat families over S, the Hilbert polynomial is locally constant on S.

- **Quot functor** (\cref{def:quot_functor}): Parametrizes equivalence classes ⟨F, q⟩ of T-flat quotients E_T ↠ F with fiberwise Hilbert polynomial Φ. The Hilbert scheme is the special case E = O_X.

- **Grassmannian scheme** (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}, \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}): A sub-development unique to this chapter. The Grassmannian Grass(V, d) of rank-d quotients of a locally free sheaf V over S is representable by a smooth projective S-scheme with a tautological quotient and Plücker embedding.

- **Representability of the Quot scheme** (\cref{thm:quot_representable}): The main theorem. The functor Quot^{Φ,L}_{E/X/S} is representable by a projective S-scheme with a universal quotient. The proof has four steps: (1) boundedness via Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity, (2) Grassmannian embedding, (3) locally closed via flattening stratification, (4) closed (hence projective) via valuative criterion.

- **Sub-lemmas**: Five supporting results on reduction to the universal case, boundedness, Grassmannian injectivity, and valuative criterion.

- **Cohomology and base change** (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}): Flat base change of cohomology; uses four project-side helper declarations (canonical base-change map, stalkwise iso, sheaf iso, tensor-presentation).

- **Iter-187 analogist-licensed substrate gaps**: Two Mathlib gaps pinned as typed sorries—Stacks 01HQ (pullback of tilde is tilde of base change) and Stacks 01XJ (pushforward of quasi-coherent is quasi-coherent).

- **Iter-189 unbundle pins**: Further structural details on Lewis F-isbasechange vectorization.

---

## 4. Picard_RelativeSpec.tex

This chapter (A.1.a) packages the relative-spectrum functor Spec_X(-): QcohAlg(X)^op → Sch/X and its three core properties. This is foundational infrastructure for the Picard construction.

- **Setup and motivation**: The relative spectrum Spec_X(A) is a scheme construction on a quasi-coherent O_X-algebra A, used by the relative Picard functor on products C ×_k T. Mathlib has absolute Spec but no general RelativeSpec; this chapter provides the specification.

- **Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras** (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}): An O_X-algebra A that is quasi-coherent as an O_X-module. QcohAlg(X) is the category of such algebras and morphisms.

- **Relative spectrum exists** (\cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}): For a quasi-coherent O_X-algebra A, there exists a scheme Spec_X(A) with an affine morphism π: Spec_X(A) → X such that π^{-1}(U) ≅ Spec(A(U)) for every affine open U ⊆ X, with compatible transition isomorphisms. Unique up to unique isomorphism over X.

- **Universal property** (\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}): Spec_X(A) represents the functor sending a scheme T to pairs (f: T → X, φ: f^*A → O_T) of an X-morphism and an O_T-algebra map. Equivalently, Hom_X(T, Spec_X(A)) ≅ Hom_{O_X-alg}(A, g_* O_T) naturally in T. (Iter-173 Lean form is weaker—IsAffineHom; Iter-174+ will upgrade.)

- **Affine base** (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}): For X = Spec R affine, Spec_X(A) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, A)) as R-schemes. The relative spectrum reduces to the absolute spectrum of global sections on affine bases.

- **Base change** (\cref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}): For g: T → X, there is a canonical isomorphism T ×_X Spec_X(A) ≅ Spec_T(g^*A). (Iter-179 Lane D closed this kernel-cleanly; Iter-174+ will refine the signature.)

- **Functoriality** (\cref{thm:relative_spec_functorial}): The construction Spec_X assembles into a contravariant functor Spec_X: QcohAlg(X)^op → AffSch/X, with the functor providing an equivalence between QcohAlg(X)^op and affine X-schemes.

- **Lean encoding**: Describes the construction via gluing affine pieces using Scheme.GlueData or AffineScheme.glueOpens, with no new core axiom required.

- **Out of scope**: Relative Picard functor (A.1.c), line-bundle pullback (A.1.b), connectedness/dimensionality/smoothness of Spec_X(A), identity component and abelian-variety structure (A.3).

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(k\) in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus \(g = g(C)\).
4	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of \(C\) in the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety even when \(C(k) = \emptyset\) (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\) and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus \(0\) the witness object is instead the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\), \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma \(+\) additivity Corollary~1.5 \(+\) Corollary~1.2 \(+\) rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.
5	\section{The Albanese construction}
6	For a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\), the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from \(C\). Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
7	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
8	Let \(C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)\) be the \(g\)\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of \(C\) (the quotient of \(C^g\) by the action of the symmetric group \(S_g\)). It is a smooth proper scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
9	\[
10	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
11	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
12	\]
13	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing \(P_0\) gives a translation isomorphism \(\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}\), and the Stein factorisation of \(\sigma\) yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
14	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of \(C\) inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over \(k\), using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
15	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
16	\begin{definition}\leanok
17	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
18	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
19	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
20	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
21	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
22	  % such \(b\) arises from a unique map \(a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}\) such that
23	  % \(a(0)=0\).  Since \(B^*\) is integral, \(a\) factors through \(P\).  Thus the
24	  % maps \(a\:B^*\to P\) and \(b\:X\to B\) are in bijective correspondence.
25	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps \(b'\:B\to B'\) such
26	  % that \(b'(0)=0\).  In particular, \(1_P\) corresponds to a natural map
27	  % \(u\:X\to A\) such that \(u(x)=0\), and every map \(b\:X\to B\) factors
28	  % uniquely through \(u\)."
29	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
30	  Let \((C, P)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with a marked \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\). A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) is an \emph{Albanese object} for \((C, P)\) if there exists a morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) sending \(P\) to the identity element \(\eta \in J\), and such that every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) factors uniquely as \(f = \iota \circ g\) for some morphism of group schemes \(g \colon J \to A\).
31	\end{definition}
32	\begin{remark}
33	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
34	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on \(J\) --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on \(\iota\) and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on \(J\) sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on \(\Jac(C)\) before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety \(A\) in the universal-property clause.
35	\end{remark}
36	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
37	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term \(h\) packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism \(\iota \colon C \to J\) together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition \(P \circ \iota = \eta_J\) and the universal-factorisation property of \(\iota\). The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
38	\begin{definition}
39	\leanok
40	[The Albanese universal morphism]
41	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
42	  
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
44	  Given a pointed curve \((C, P)\) and an Albanese term \(h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\), the \emph{universal morphism} of \(h\) is the morphism
45	  \[
46	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
47	  \]
48	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of \(h\).
49	\end{definition}
50	\begin{lemma}
51	\leanok
52	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
53	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
54	  
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
56	  The universal morphism \(h.\mathtt{ofCurve}\) sends the marked point \(P\) to the identity of \(J\):
57	  \[
58	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
59	  \]
60	\end{lemma}
61	\begin{lemma}
62	\leanok
63	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
64	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
65	  
66	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
67	  For every morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), there exists a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g\).
68	\end{lemma}
69	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point \(P \in C(k)\) as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that \(P\), and applies the corresponding helper above.
70	\begin{theorem}\leanok
71	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
72	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
74	  Let \(J_1, J_2\) be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve \((C, P)\), with universal pointed morphisms \(\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1\) and \(\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2\) (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism \(e \colon J_1 \to J_2\) satisfying \(\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e\). The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation \(\mathrm{id}\), and the produced morphism \(e\) is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of \(e\) as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
75	\end{theorem}
76	\begin{remark}
77	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
78	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism \(h \colon J_2 \to J_1\) and verifies \(g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}\) and \(h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}\) (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple \((g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})\). The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
79	  \[
80	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
81	  \]
82	  The natural strengthening would be to return
83	  \[
84	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
85	  \]
86	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
87	\end{remark}
88	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
89	\begin{definition}\leanok
90	[Jacobian]
91	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
92	  \label{def:Jacobian}
93	  
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
95	  The \emph{Jacobian} of \(C\) is the abelian variety \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) obtained as the Albanese object of \(C\): a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(k\) of dimension \(g(C)\) equipped with a universal morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\). Formally, \(\Jac(C)\) is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-\(P\)) Albanese witness for \(C\) provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
96	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the Jacobian collapses to \(\Spec k\) itself, which is the terminal object in the category of \(k\)-schemes. When \(g(C) > 0\) the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers \(C\) itself (after choosing a \(k\)-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: \(\Jac(C)\) is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-\(0\) specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over \(k\) of relative dimension \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\Spec k\).
97	\end{definition}
98	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
99	The four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
100	\begin{theorem}\leanok
101	[Group object structure]
102	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
104	  \(\Jac(C)\) carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
105	\end{theorem}
106	\begin{proof}
107	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, \(\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J\) as a \(k\)-scheme, where \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\) is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field \(\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J\), so \(\Jac(C)\) inherits it by projection.
108	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if \(\iota \colon C \to J\) is the universal morphism sending the marked point \(P\) to the identity, then the diagonal \(C \times C \to J \times J\), post-composed with the universal morphism out of \(C \times C\) (factored through \(J\) by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
109	\end{proof}

133	\begin{proof}
134	  The Lean formalisation projects the field \(\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}\) of the witness \(\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C\); the relative dimension \(g(C)\) is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
135	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of \(C\) equals the genus \(g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to \(\Jac(C)\) at the identity is canonically identified with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of \(\mathcal O_C\) is classified by an element of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\), and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\). Smoothness of \(\Jac(C)\) over \(k\) follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any \(k\)-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of \(\Jac(C)\) at every \(k\)-rational point is the same as at the identity.
136	\end{proof}

180	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety \(\Jac(C)\) is the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, \(\Jac(C)\) arises as the Stein factorisation of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) followed by a translation; \(\Sym^g(C)\) is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of \(C^g\), itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.

182	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
183	\begin{itemize}
184	  \item \(g = 0\): Then \(\Jac(C) = \Spec k\), the trivial group scheme; \(\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(0 = g\).
185	  \item \(g = 1\): Then \(\Jac(C) \cong C\) once a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\) is fixed; \(C\) is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension \(1\)).
186	  \item \(g \ge 2\): \(\Jac(C)\) is a non-trivial \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to \(C\) as a scheme.
187	\end{itemize}
188	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
189	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: \(\Jac(C)\) is the connected component of the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). An equivalent route, available whenever a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) and translate by \(-gP\).
190	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
191	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of \(C\). This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
192	\begin{definition}
193	\leanok
194	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
195	[Albanese witness for a curve]
196	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
197	  
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
199	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) over \(k\) is a bundle of the following seven fields:
200	  \begin{itemize}
201	    \item \(J\): an object of the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\) --- the candidate Albanese object.
202	    \item \(\mathtt{grpObj}\): a group-object structure on \(J\) in the category of \(k\)-schemes over \(\Spec k\).
203	    \item \(\mathtt{proper}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is proper.
204	    \item \(\mathtt{smooth}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth.
205	    \item \(\mathtt{geomIrred}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is geometrically irreducible.
206	    \item \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\): a proof that the structure morphism \(J \to \Spec k\) is smooth of relative dimension \(g(C)\). This refines \(\mathtt{smooth}\) at the specific relative dimension \(\genus\, C\) (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
207	    \item \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\): a proof that, for every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
208	  \end{itemize}
209	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
210	\end{definition}
211	\begin{remark}
212	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
213	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
214	  \[
215	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
216	  \]
217	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single \(J\) is chosen once (intrinsic to \(C\)), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
218	  \[
219	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
220	  \]
221	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to \(C\): a different choice of \(P \in C(k)\) alters the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism of \(J\), and so leaves \(J\) unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-\(P\) projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: \(\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}\), \(\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}\), and \(\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}\) each take a marked point \(P\) as input and project the corresponding component of \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P\) via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
222	\end{remark}
223	\begin{remark}
224	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}
225	  The fields \(\mathtt{smooth}\) and \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) of an Albanese witness are mathematically redundant: the relative-dimension version implies the unqualified version via Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension.smooth}. Keeping both fields is a Lean-level convenience: \(\mathtt{smooth}\) is the form required by the typeclass binder of \texttt{IsAlbanese} (and by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.instGrpObj}\) projection), whereas \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is the form required by the protected \(\mathtt{Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}\) projection. The redundancy carries no mathematical cost (the two fields agree) and no soundness cost (deriving one from the other would only re-run the same Mathlib instance synthesis at the projection sites).
226	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
227	\end{remark}

247	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field \(k\). There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(J\) over \(k\) of relative dimension \(g(C)\) such that, for every \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), the scheme \(J\) is an Albanese object for the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for \(C\) (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
248	\end{theorem}
249	\begin{proof}
250	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-\(0\) sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to the base field \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\)). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme \(J\); only the universal morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to J\) varies with the marked point \(P\). We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
251	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a \(P\)-independent witness.}
252	  The witness \(J\) depends only on \(C\), not on the marked point \(P\). Indeed, both classical constructions below produce \(J\) as an intrinsic object of \(C\) (the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\), or the Stein-factorisation target of \(\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}\) translated by a fixed \(P_0\)); a different choice of \(P\) in \(C(k)\) alters \(\iota_P\) by a translation of \(J\), which is an automorphism, and so does not change \(J\) as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling \(J\) as a single field and the universal-property data as \(\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J\). We now turn to producing \(J\).
253	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
254	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a \(k\)-scheme \(T\), write \(C_T := C \times_k T\) and let \(\pi \colon C_T \to T\) be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
257	    \[
258	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
259	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
260	    \]
261	    A \(T\)-point is a line bundle on \(C_T\) modulo a line bundle pulled back from \(T\). The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C_T)\) descends to the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) and gives \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) a natural group-functor refinement.
262	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For \(C\) smooth proper geometrically connected over \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point, \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}\) (after \'etale sheafification, if \(k\) is not algebraically closed) is representable by a \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k\), which is needed for the quotient by \(\pi^*\Pic(T)\) to be \'etale-locally injective.
263	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) be the identity component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\). As the identity component of a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; for \(C\) smooth proper of genus \(g\), it is moreover a smooth proper \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the kernel of the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\), which is locally constant). Take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
264	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each \(P \in C(k)\), the Abel--Jacobi morphism
265	    \[
266	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
267	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
268	    \]
269	    sends \(P\) to the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). It is universal among morphisms \(f \colon C \to A\) to smooth proper geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group schemes \(A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\): this is the classical Albanese property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), proved by reducing to the case \(k = \overline k\) (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on \(C \times A\) trivialised along \(\{P\} \times A\) correspond bijectively to morphisms \(C \to A^{\vee} = A\) sending \(P\) to \(\eta_A\).
270	  \end{itemize}
271	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
272	  \begin{itemize}
273	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product \(C \times_k T\) and the descent of the abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\) along \(\pi^*\). The pieces are present individually but not assembled. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC, \(\sim 6\)--\(10\) iters.)
274	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of \(\mathrm{Quot}\), hence \(\mathrm{Hilb}\), as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}. (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC, \(\sim 15\)--\(25\) iters --- the dominant Route A block.)
275	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction \(G^0 \subseteq G\), and the degree map \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k\). (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 600\)--\(900\) LOC, \(\sim 5\)--\(8\) iters.)
276	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form. \textbf{Bypass via Milne autoduality FAILS}: as Milne III \S~6 writes the proof of Proposition~6.1, it invokes Theorem~3.2 (rational-map-extension from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety) directly to upgrade the symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular map \(J \to A\), and the would-be Picard-functor / autoduality detour \(J \cong J^\vee\) routes through the theorem of the cube, excised from this project iter-163. Sub-step~A.4 therefore inherits the Theorem~3.2 + Lemma~3.3 codim-\(1\) extension + Weil-divisor API + Auslander--Buchsbaum sub-build (the last absent from Mathlib). (See iter-172 audit at \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP}, NOTE block + \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} proof.) (See per-sub-phase budget below: \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC, \(\sim 22\)--\(35\) iters.)
277	  \end{itemize}
